post_title: UKMLA Fees: The Complete 2026 Cost Breakdown
post_excerpt: How much does the UKMLA cost in 2026? A clear breakdown of UKMLA fees for UK students and IMGs — AKT, CPSA, registration, hidden costs, and local-currency figures.
seo_title: UKMLA Fees: The Complete 2026 Cost Breakdown
seo_description: UKMLA fees explained for 2026: AKT, CPSA and GMC registration costs for IMGs, what UK students pay, hidden costs, and figures in your local currency.
html_content:
UKMLA fees are one of the first things every aspiring UK-licensed doctor needs to understand before beginning the registration journey. Whether you are a UK medical student whose costs are covered by your institution, or an International Medical Graduate (IMG) paying entirely out of pocket, knowing the exact charges — and the hidden costs that sit alongside them — is essential for effective financial planning. This guide breaks down every fee associated with the UKMLA process in 2026, explains the refund and cancellation rules, compares costs across popular currencies, and offers practical strategies for reducing the overall financial burden of gaining GMC registration.

The total cost of the UKMLA pathway for an IMG can exceed £1,800 before a single day of clinical practice in the UK. That figure rises considerably once you factor in study materials, living expenses during exam preparation, travel to Manchester for the CPSA, and the annual retention fee payable after registration. Understanding where every pound goes — and which costs are avoidable — is the difference between a financial plan that works and one that leaves candidates stranded mid-journey.

In this comprehensive guide we cover the 2026 fee schedule in full, the refund and cancellation policy, currency conversions for candidates from India, Nigeria, the UAE and the United States, financial hardship options, and a detailed comparison of how UKMLA costs stack up against other international medical licensing exams.

How Much Are the UKMLA Fees in 2026?

The UKMLA pathway involves several distinct fees payable to the GMC and, for IMGs, to the Pearson VUE testing network. Each charge is separate, non-bundled, and payable at different stages of the process. As of 2026 the headline fees are: £283 for the Applied Knowledge Test (AKT), also known as PLAB 1 for IMGs; £1,036 for the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA), also known as PLAB 2 for IMGs; and £481 for the initial GMC registration fee once both assessments are passed. Together these three core charges total approximately £1,800 for an IMG completing the full pathway from first exam attempt to GMC registration.

The GMC reviews its fee schedule periodically, and the most recent formal revision took effect in April 2026 following the publication of the General Medical Council Registration Fees Regulations 2026. Candidates should always verify current charges directly on the GMC website before booking, as fees are subject to change with notice. The table below shows the main fee categories at a glance.

Fee ItemWho PaysAmount (GBP, 2026)When Payable
AKT / PLAB 1 exam feeIMGs only£283At booking
CPSA / PLAB 2 exam feeIMGs only£1,036At booking
GMC initial registrationIMGs (and some UK grads)£481On application
Annual retention feeAll registered doctors£431Annually in January
AKT re-sit feeIMGs who fail AKT£283At re-booking
CPSA re-sit feeIMGs who fail CPSA£1,036At re-booking

It is important to note that these fees are set and collected directly by the GMC and cannot be paid through third-party agents. Any agent claiming to facilitate GMC payments for a fee is operating outside official channels. Always pay through your GMC Online account using a valid debit or credit card.

UKMLA Fees for UK Medical Students

For UK medical students enrolled at a GMC-approved UK medical school, the UKMLA fees structure is fundamentally different from that facing IMGs. UK medical students do not pay the AKT or CPSA exam fees directly. Instead, the GMC charges these fees to the medical school, which incorporates the cost into its overall programme delivery. This means the cost is effectively included within the tuition fees students pay to their university rather than appearing as a separate, upfront charge.

This arrangement was a deliberate policy decision by the GMC when designing the UKMLA framework. The logic is straightforward: the UKMLA replaces the historic summative assessments that medical schools already ran as part of their degree programmes. Because universities were already funding those final-year exams, transitioning to the UKMLA did not create an additional financial burden for individual students — the cost simply shifted from an internal assessment to an external, GMC-administered one, with the medical school absorbing the invoice.

Practically, this means UK medical students receive an invitation letter from their medical school to book both the AKT and CPSA. They log in to the relevant booking portal, select dates, and confirm attendance without entering any payment details. No money changes hands directly between the student and the GMC at the exam stage. The financial consideration for UK graduates arises later, when they apply for provisional GMC registration upon graduation. At that point, a registration application fee of approximately £165 is payable, significantly lower than the full £481 fee charged to IMGs because UK graduates receive provisional rather than full registration initially.

UK graduates are therefore in a significantly more financially advantageous position than their IMG counterparts — the two largest costs (AKT and CPSA exam fees) are covered, leaving only the relatively modest registration application and the annual retention fee of £431 payable each January to maintain registration.

UKMLA Fees for International Medical Graduates (IMGs)

For IMGs, the UKMLA fees landscape is more complex and substantially more expensive. IMGs must pay for each component of the assessment individually, and these payments are non-negotiable, non-income-tested, and non-refundable except under specific cancellation circumstances. Understanding each element is critical before committing to the pathway.

Applied Knowledge Test (AKT / PLAB 1) Fee — £283

The AKT, which IMGs access through the PLAB 1 route, costs £283 per attempt in 2026. This fee is payable through the GMC Online portal and must be settled at the point of booking. IMGs can sit the AKT at Pearson VUE test centres worldwide, which means candidates in India, Nigeria, Pakistan, Egypt, and other high-volume IMG countries can take the exam without travelling to the UK. The booking window typically opens several months in advance, and popular test centre dates fill quickly, particularly in major cities like Mumbai, Lagos, Karachi, and Cairo.

The £283 fee covers a single attempt only. There is no cap on the number of attempts an IMG can make at the AKT, but each re-sit requires a fresh payment of £283. Candidates who fail the AKT should factor re-sit costs into their financial planning from the outset. Historically, a significant proportion of IMG candidates require more than one attempt, particularly in the wake of the MLA content map update that came into effect in August 2024 and introduced new topics including transgender health, genetics, social determinants of health, and expanded women's health presentations.

Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA / PLAB 2) Fee — £1,036

The CPSA, accessed by IMGs through the PLAB 2 route, is by far the most expensive single element of the UKMLA pathway at £1,036 per attempt. This fee is substantially higher than the AKT fee because it covers the costs of a full OSCE circuit administered at the GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester, including trained simulated patients, trained examiners, marking, logistics, and the infrastructure of an in-person clinical exam.

Unlike the AKT, the CPSA cannot be sat overseas — all PLAB 2 / CPSA sittings take place in Manchester. This adds a layer of additional cost for IMGs who must travel from abroad or from other parts of the UK. Many international candidates combine their CPSA booking with a period of preparation in the UK, which itself carries accommodation and subsistence costs on top of the exam fee. For a candidate travelling from India for a two-week CPSA preparation and exam trip, the total spend including flights, accommodation, food, and the exam fee can easily reach £2,000–£2,500 for this component alone.

The number of CPSA re-sits is not capped, but each costs £1,036. Refugee doctors receive a concessionary rate: the first two PLAB 2 attempts are charged at half price (approximately £518 each), with full fees applying from the third attempt onward.

GMC Registration Fee — £481

Once an IMG has passed both the AKT and CPSA, confirmed English language proficiency (IELTS Academic overall 7.5 with no component below 7.0, or OET minimum Grade B in all four sub-tests), and completed the required internship experience, they can apply for full GMC registration. The initial registration fee is £481. This is a one-time payment for the initial application; once registered, the annual retention fee of £431 applies every January to maintain active registration.

Hidden Costs Beyond the UKMLA Exam Fees

The headline fees for the AKT, CPSA, and GMC registration represent only part of the true financial commitment of the UKMLA pathway. A thorough budget must account for a range of additional costs that are easy to overlook during initial planning but can amount to several thousand pounds.

  • English language testing: IELTS Academic costs approximately £180–£200 per attempt in most countries. OET costs approximately £500 per attempt. Candidates who do not achieve the required scores on the first attempt face repeat fees.
  • Study materials and question banks: Quality UKMLA AKT question banks cost between £40 and £200 for subscription access. Many candidates use multiple resources, bringing this total to £300–£500 over a preparation period of six to twelve months.
  • CPSA preparation courses: In-person OSCE preparation courses in the UK typically cost £500–£1,500 for a multi-day intensive. Online OSCE preparation packages range from £100 to £400.
  • Travel to Manchester for CPSA: Return flights from South Asia typically cost £500–£900. Return flights from West Africa range from £600–£1,000. Domestic UK travel (if preparing elsewhere) is lower but still significant.
  • Accommodation during CPSA: Hotels in Manchester near the GMC Clinical Assessment Centre range from £60–£150 per night. A two-week preparation and exam period can cost £840–£2,100 in accommodation alone.
  • Document verification: Some primary qualifications require verification through the Education Commission for Foreign Medical Graduates (ECFMG) or equivalent national bodies, which carry their own fees.
  • Annual retention: After registration, the £431 annual retention fee is payable every January for as long as you remain on the register. Over a 40-year career, this amounts to more than £17,000.

UKMLA Fees in Your Local Currency

For IMGs from outside the UK, exchange rate fluctuations significantly affect the real-world cost of the UKMLA pathway. The table below shows approximate conversions of the main fee items as of mid-2026. These figures are indicative; always check the current rate before transferring funds, as exchange rates change daily.

Fee ItemGBPINR (India)USD (USA)AED (UAE)NGN (Nigeria)
AKT / PLAB 1£283~₹30,100~$360~AED 1,320~₦440,000
CPSA / PLAB 2£1,036~₹110,200~$1,310~AED 4,820~₦1,610,000
GMC Registration£481~₹51,200~$610~AED 2,240~₦748,000
Annual Retention£431~₹45,900~$545~AED 2,007~₦670,000
Total Core Fees£1,800~₹191,500~$2,280~AED 8,380~₦2,798,000

Currency conversion costs can themselves add 1–3% depending on your bank or transfer service. Using services such as Wise (formerly TransferWise) or a specialist international payment provider when topping up your GMC Online account can reduce conversion losses compared to standard bank transfer rates.

UKMLA Fees Compared to Other Countries' Licensing Exams

Understanding how UKMLA fees compare to equivalent licensing pathways in other English-speaking countries helps IMGs assess the relative value of pursuing UK registration versus alternatives such as the USMLE (USA), AMC (Australia), or MCCQE (Canada).

The United States Medical Licensing Examination (USMLE) involves three separate steps with fees of approximately $985 (Step 1), $985 (Step 2 CK), and $1,295 (Step 3), plus an Educational Commission for Foreign Medical Graduates (ECFMG) certification fee of approximately $200. Total pathway costs for an IMG exceed $3,500, not including travel to the US for clinical rotations that may be required. The Australian Medical Council (AMC) pathway involves a multiple-choice exam fee of approximately AUD 2,600 and an OSCE fee of approximately AUD 5,600, totalling over AUD 8,000 (approximately £4,000 GBP) before registration fees. The Medical Council of Canada's pathway involves fees in the range of CAD 3,000–4,500 across its examination components.

By comparison, the UKMLA pathway at approximately £1,800 for core exam and registration fees (assuming first-attempt passes) represents relatively good value among high-income country medical licensing systems, particularly given that the CPSA is administered in a world-class facility with trained simulated patients and that GMC registration opens access to one of the most internationally respected medical registers in the world.

How to Pay UKMLA Fees and the Refund Policy

All UKMLA-related fees are paid through your GMC Online account. To make a payment, log in to your GMC Online profile, navigate to the relevant section (PLAB booking for AKT or CPSA, or registration application for the registration fee), and pay by debit or credit card. The GMC accepts Visa, Mastercard, and most major cards. Payment is required in full at the time of booking; instalment plans are not available.

The GMC operates a tiered cancellation and refund policy for the AKT and CPSA. The amount refunded depends on how far in advance of your exam date you cancel:

  • More than 42 calendar days before the exam: Refund of the full fee minus a 10% administration/cancellation charge.
  • Between 14 and 42 calendar days before the exam: No refund is available under standard circumstances.
  • Fewer than 14 calendar days before the exam: No refund and no deferral; the full fee is forfeited.

The GMC may exercise discretion and refund cancellation fees in exceptional circumstances. Accepted circumstances typically include: visa refusal or visa processing delays that prevent travel to the test centre; introduction of government travel restrictions (such as those imposed during the COVID-19 pandemic); serious ill health supported by a medical certificate; or bereavement of an immediate family member. If you believe your circumstances qualify, you must cancel your booking through the portal first and then submit evidence by email to the GMC within a reasonable timeframe. The GMC will assess the evidence and make a decision; this process can take several weeks.

For GMC registration fees, the situation is different. Once paid, the registration application fee is generally non-refundable regardless of outcome, including if the application is refused on grounds of fitness to practise or inadequate documentation. Candidates should ensure all documents are in order before submitting a registration application.

How to Budget and Reduce Your UKMLA Costs

Given the significant total financial outlay involved in the UKMLA pathway, systematic budgeting and cost-reduction strategies can make a meaningful difference, particularly for candidates from lower-income countries where the exchange rate amplifies the pound cost considerably.

Prepare Thoroughly Before Your First Attempt

The single most impactful cost-reduction strategy is passing both the AKT and CPSA on your first attempt. Each re-sit costs £283 (AKT) or £1,036 (CPSA). A candidate who uses a single additional attempt at both assessments adds £1,319 to their costs — more than the price of many comprehensive preparation courses. Investing in quality preparation materials upfront is almost always cheaper than funding re-sits. Use GMC-approved question banks, work through the full MLA content map systematically, and complete multiple timed mock papers before your real sitting.

Book Test Centres in Lower-Cost Locations

For the AKT, Pearson VUE has test centres in over 180 countries. Candidates who live near a border with a neighbouring country where test centres are less busy or where international travel is cheaper may find it worthwhile to travel to a nearer, less-congested centre. Book as early as possible to secure your preferred date and location; last-minute bookings often face limited availability, which can push candidates toward more expensive or inconvenient centres.

Plan Your Manchester Trip Efficiently

For the CPSA, advance planning of travel and accommodation significantly reduces costs. Booking flights six to twelve weeks in advance and using budget airlines where available can reduce travel costs substantially. Sharing accommodation with other CPSA candidates (many preparation forums and social media groups facilitate this) can halve hotel costs. Choosing accommodation slightly further from the city centre with good public transport links to the GMC centre is often significantly cheaper than city-centre hotels.

Financial Aid and Hardship Concessions

The GMC offers specific fee concessions for refugee doctors through its refugee doctor assistance programme. Under this scheme, refugee doctors can take the first two PLAB 1 (AKT) sittings free of charge, and the first two PLAB 2 (CPSA) sittings at 50% of the standard fee. To qualify, doctors must hold refugee status or have a pending asylum application recognised by the relevant national authority. Evidence of status must be provided to the GMC before the concession is applied. There is currently no general income-based hardship fund for non-refugee IMGs, meaning most candidates must fund the pathway from personal savings, family support, or, in some cases, bank loans.

Timeline Your Pathway to Avoid Overlap

Each component of the UKMLA pathway has a validity period. AKT results are valid for five years. There is therefore no financial pressure to rush into the CPSA immediately after passing the AKT if your personal circumstances make it advantageous to delay. Similarly, English language test results (IELTS or OET) have a defined validity period of two years, so timing your language test to align with your overall pathway timeline avoids the cost of retesting.

UKMLA Fee History and Changes Over Time

Understanding how UKMLA-related fees have evolved over time gives context to current charges and helps with forward financial planning. PLAB fees have increased substantially over the past decade. In 2015, PLAB 1 cost approximately £225 and PLAB 2 approximately £665. By 2020, those figures had risen to approximately £239 (PLAB 1) and £869 (PLAB 2). The 2024 alignment of PLAB with the MLA standard coincided with further fee increases, bringing the charges to their current 2026 levels of £283 and £1,036 respectively. The GMC attributes fee increases primarily to rising operational costs, investment in digital infrastructure, and the costs of standard-setting and quality assurance for the new MLA framework.

The introduction of the MLA framework did not reduce fees for any cohort of candidates. Some stakeholders argued that IMGs should see a fee reduction given that the MLA content map was being applied to existing PLAB infrastructure rather than a wholly new exam; however, the GMC has maintained that the enhanced standard-setting processes, expanded content, and quality assurance measures associated with MLA compliance justify the current fee levels.

Frequently Asked Questions About UKMLA Fees

Do UK medical students pay any UKMLA fees directly?

No. UK medical students enrolled at GMC-approved medical schools do not pay for the AKT or CPSA directly. The fees are invoiced to the medical school and absorbed within the overall cost of the medical degree. Students receive booking invitations through their institution and complete the process without making any direct payment to the GMC for the assessments themselves. The only fees payable directly by UK medical graduates are the registration application fee (approximately £165 for provisional registration) and the annual retention fee (£431) once on the register.

Is the UKMLA fee refundable if I fail?

No. Exam fees are not refundable on the grounds of failure. Whether you pass or fail the AKT or CPSA, the fee paid for that sitting is retained by the GMC. Refunds are only available when you cancel your booking before the exam within the permitted cancellation window (more than 42 days before the exam, subject to the 10% cancellation charge) or in demonstrable exceptional circumstances such as visa refusal or serious illness.

How many times can I sit the AKT and CPSA, and what does it cost?

There is no official cap on the number of AKT or CPSA attempts for IMGs. Each attempt requires a fresh payment at the standard rate: £283 per AKT attempt and £1,036 per CPSA attempt. There are also no mandatory waiting periods between attempts beyond the practical constraint of available exam dates. From a cost perspective, every additional attempt is a significant financial commitment, reinforcing the importance of adequate preparation before each sitting.

Are there any fee discounts for IMGs from lower-income countries?

Currently, the GMC does not operate a sliding-scale fee system based on country of origin or income level. The only formal concession available is the refugee doctor assistance scheme, which provides free AKT attempts and half-price CPSA attempts for doctors with refugee status. There is no general hardship fund or discounted rate for IMGs from low- or middle-income countries. Candidates are encouraged to save and plan accordingly, and several community organisations and diaspora networks offer informal interest-free loans or financial support for medical licensing.

When exactly do I pay each fee — all upfront or separately?

Fees are payable separately at each stage of the process. You pay the AKT fee when you book your AKT sitting. You pay the CPSA fee when you book your CPSA sitting (which can only be done after passing the AKT). You pay the GMC registration fee when you submit your full registration application (after passing both assessments and meeting all other eligibility requirements). There is no requirement to pay all fees upfront, and no deposit system for holding a place before full payment is required.

What happens to my fee if the GMC cancels my exam?

If the GMC cancels an exam session for reasons within its control (administrative error, venue problem, safety concerns), candidates are entitled to a full refund of the exam fee or the option to transfer their booking to an alternative date at no additional charge. The GMC has historically handled such situations promptly. If an exam is cancelled due to exceptional external circumstances beyond the GMC's control (such as a national emergency or infrastructure failure), the GMC will consider cases individually and aim to minimise financial detriment to candidates.

Does the annual GMC retention fee increase each year?

The annual retention fee (currently £431) is reviewed periodically by the GMC and has increased at various points over the past decade. The GMC must consult publicly before changing retention fees and typically provides at least six months' notice of any increase. Fee changes are announced on the GMC website and in direct communications to registered doctors. Increases have historically been in the range of £10–£30 per year when they occur, but not every year sees an increase.

Conclusion

Understanding UKMLA fees in full — not just the headline exam costs but the hidden travel, preparation, language testing, and annual retention charges — is the foundation of effective financial planning for the UK medical licensing journey. For UK medical students, the pathway is largely free at the point of assessment, with costs absorbed by medical schools. For IMGs, the total outlay from first AKT attempt to GMC registration typically ranges from £1,800 (best case, first attempts only) to £3,500 or more when travel, accommodation, preparation materials, and English language testing are included.

The most effective strategies for managing UKMLA costs are thorough preparation to maximise first-attempt pass rates, early booking to secure cost-effective travel and accommodation for the CPSA in Manchester, and careful timing of the various pathway components to avoid unnecessary repetition of English language or medical knowledge tests. The fees are substantial, but they represent a one-time investment in access to one of the world's most respected medical registers and a healthcare system that offers strong career development, structured training, and competitive remuneration.

post_title: What Is the UKMLA? A Complete Beginner's Guide
post_excerpt: New to the UK Medical Licensing Assessment? This guide explains what the UKMLA is, who needs to sit it, and how it works from start to finish.
seo_title: What Is the UKMLA? A Complete Beginner's Guide
seo_description: Not sure what the UKMLA is? Our beginner's guide explains the UK Medical Licensing Assessment, who must sit it, and exactly how it works.
html_content:
What is the UKMLA? The UK Medical Licensing Assessment (UKMLA) is the General Medical Council's unified national examination that every doctor must pass before being granted a licence to practise medicine in the United Kingdom. Introduced in its current form in 2024, the UKMLA replaced a fragmented system in which UK medical school finals and the separate PLAB examination for international medical graduates (IMGs) operated to different standards and used different formats, creating an inconsistent entry threshold to the UK medical workforce. The UKMLA brings all routes to GMC registration under a single, evidence-based standard, ensuring that every newly licensed doctor — regardless of where they trained — can demonstrate the same core level of clinical knowledge, skills, and professional behaviour.

The assessment is designed around the principle that the standard for safe, independent practice as a Foundation Year 1 doctor in the UK should be identical whether you graduated from a UK medical school, a European university, or an institution in South Asia, West Africa, or the Middle East. This is both a quality assurance measure and an equity measure: patients anywhere in the NHS should be able to trust that every doctor seeing them has met the same independently verified standard.

This guide covers everything you need to know about the UKMLA — its history, purpose, structure, who must sit it, how it differs from previous assessments, what happens if you fail, key statistics, and how it fits within the broader landscape of UK medical education. Whether you are a current medical student planning your final year or an IMG exploring your pathway to UK practice, this is the reference you need.

Why Was the UKMLA Introduced? The History Behind the Assessment

Before the UKMLA, the UK had a two-track entry system. UK graduates completed their medical school's own summative assessments — typically a combination of written papers and an OSCE — which varied significantly in format, standard, and rigour between different universities. There was no single common national examination, meaning the threshold for graduation (and provisional GMC registration) was effectively set by each medical school independently. The PLAB examination, introduced in the 1990s, served as the separate licensing hurdle for IMGs, but it operated on a different content framework and had no formal alignment with what UK medical schools were testing.

This dual system attracted increasing scrutiny. Reports from medical education bodies and inquiries following patient safety incidents highlighted the risks of an inconsistent entry standard. The GMC's own research showed that the knowledge and competencies assessed by different medical schools' final examinations, and by PLAB, did not always map to the same clinical competency framework. The Medical Schools Council (MSC) and the GMC collaborated over several years to design a solution: a single national licensing assessment that would be taken by all UK graduates and, in a parallel form, by all IMGs.

Pilot testing of the UKMLA began with a small number of medical schools in 2022 and 2023. From the 2024–25 academic year, the UKMLA became the mandatory licensing assessment for all UK medical students graduating from that year onward. Simultaneously, the PLAB examination was formally aligned with the MLA Content Map standard from August 2024, meaning IMGs now take a PLAB that is built on the same blueprint as the UKMLA taken by UK graduates. The formal rebrand of PLAB to MLA-equivalent pathways (with PLAB 1 equating to the AKT and PLAB 2 equating to the CPSA) completed the structural unification.

Who Needs to Sit the UKMLA?

Understanding who must take the UKMLA requires distinguishing between two groups: UK medical graduates and IMGs.

UK medical students graduating from a GMC-approved UK medical school from the 2024–25 academic year onwards must pass the UKMLA as a condition of their degree and a prerequisite for provisional GMC registration. This is now a standard part of the medical education curriculum in all UK medical schools. The assessment is embedded in the final year of the programme, and students sit both the AKT and the CPSA as organised by their institution in partnership with the GMC and, for the AKT, the Medical Schools Council.

International Medical Graduates (IMGs) — doctors who obtained their primary medical qualification outside the UK — must pass both components of the UKMLA (via the PLAB route) as part of the GMC registration process. For IMGs, this means booking and sitting PLAB 1 (equivalent to the AKT) and PLAB 2 (equivalent to the CPSA) through the GMC's booking system. Unlike UK students, IMGs pay the full exam fees directly to the GMC and must meet additional prerequisites including English language proficiency and an acceptable internship pattern.

Some groups are exempt from UKMLA. Doctors applying for specialist or GP registration who have already achieved a recognised qualification (such as Membership of the Royal Colleges of Physicians) follow a specialist registration route and do not take the UKMLA. EEA doctors with certain qualifications obtained before specific transition dates may also benefit from grandfather provisions. The GMC website publishes up-to-date eligibility guidance for all routes.

The Two Components of the UKMLA Explained

The UKMLA is a two-stage assessment comprising the Applied Knowledge Test (AKT) and the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA). Both must be passed to satisfy the UKMLA requirement; there is no exemption from either component based on performance in the other.

The Applied Knowledge Test (AKT)

The AKT is a computer-based written examination consisting of 200 Single Best Answer (SBA) questions delivered over three hours (180 minutes). Questions are written by panels of practising clinicians and medical educators, mapped to the MLA Content Map, and reviewed through a rigorous quality assurance process before use. The AKT tests the application of clinical knowledge to realistic patient scenarios — candidates are presented with a brief clinical vignette and must select the single most appropriate answer from five options.

For UK medical students, the AKT is administered at the student's medical school or a designated Pearson VUE centre. For IMGs, the AKT is sat through the PLAB 1 route at Pearson VUE test centres in over 180 countries worldwide, allowing candidates to sit the exam without travelling to the UK. The pass mark is determined for each sitting using criterion-referenced standard setting (the Angoff method), so it varies slightly between sittings, typically falling in the range of 53–68% of raw items correct.

The Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA)

The CPSA is a face-to-face Objective Structured Clinical Examination (OSCE) comprising 18 stations of eight minutes each. It takes place at the GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester for IMGs (via PLAB 2), and at the student's medical school for UK undergraduates. The CPSA tests clinical examination skills, communication with patients and colleagues, clinical reasoning in real time, practical clinical procedures, and professional behaviours including ethical decision-making and patient safety awareness.

At each station, candidates interact with a trained simulated patient and are assessed by an examiner using a structured marking scheme combining a global rating and a detailed checklist. The CPSA cannot be taken before passing the AKT, and no credit is given for partial completion — both components must be passed independently.

What Does the UKMLA Assess? The MLA Content Map

The content of both UKMLA components is defined by the MLA Content Map, a document published and maintained by the GMC that sets out the core knowledge, skills, and behaviours required for safe, independent practice as a Foundation Year 1 doctor in the UK. The Content Map organises assessable material into four main pillars: clinical presentations, clinical conditions, professional behaviours, and practical skills.

The September 2026 update to the MLA Content Map significantly expanded the scope of assessable material. The list of core clinical conditions grew from approximately 311 to 430, with substantial additions in women's health, genetics, transgender health, and social determinants of health. The mapping grid that previously restricted which conditions could appear in which specialty contexts was removed, meaning any condition can now be tested in any clinical setting. This makes broad, systematic preparation across all specialties more important than ever.

The Content Map is publicly available on the GMC website and should be the primary reference document for any candidate preparing for the UKMLA. It is distinct from a traditional curriculum: it does not prescribe how knowledge should be taught, only what standard must be demonstrated in the assessment. This means different medical schools and different preparation resources may take different approaches to covering the same content, but all must ultimately assess against the same map.

For more detail on the MLA Content Map's structure and how to use it for revision, see our UKMLA syllabus guide.

How the UKMLA Differs from Previous Assessments

For UK medical graduates, the UKMLA represents a significant structural change from the pre-2024 system. Previously, a student at one UK medical school might sit a final-year written paper with 120 questions written entirely by that school's faculty, covering topics selected according to the school's own curriculum emphasis. A student at another school might sit a different paper with different question styles, different topic weighting, and different pass standards. There was no requirement for scores or standards to be comparable across institutions.

The UKMLA changes this fundamentally. Every UK medical student now sits the same AKT paper on the same questions (with some variation between sittings for security), marked against the same standard. The playing field is levelled in a way that has not existed before in UK medical education. Medical schools retain their autonomy over how they teach — the UKMLA is an end-point assessment, not a curriculum prescription — but the bar they must prepare their students to clear is now uniform and externally set.

For IMGs, the change is equally profound. Pre-2024 PLAB was built on a different content framework from UK Finals, meaning the two pathways to GMC registration did not formally test the same things in the same way. PLAB 1 questions tended toward recall of clinical facts, while UK Finals increasingly moved toward applied reasoning. The MLA-aligned PLAB now tests applied reasoning from the same Content Map as the UK undergraduate AKT, creating genuine equivalence between the two routes for the first time.

The UKMLA Timeline: From Sitting the Exam to GMC Registration

Understanding the full timeline from beginning your UKMLA journey to obtaining GMC registration is essential for career planning. The sequence of steps differs between UK students and IMGs, but both involve multiple stages that require careful coordination.

Timeline for UK Medical Students

UK students typically sit the AKT in the spring of their final year, with results issued within four to six weeks. The CPSA follows in the final weeks of the academic year. Results are combined with the medical school's own assessments, and successful graduates receive both their degree and confirmation of UKMLA completion. Provisional GMC registration applications can then be submitted, typically completing in time for the start of the Foundation Programme in August.

Timeline for IMGs

For IMGs the timeline is less fixed. After confirming eligibility (including English language proficiency and internship requirements), candidates book and sit PLAB 1 / AKT — which can be done at test centres worldwide. AKT results are typically released within three to four weeks of sitting. Candidates then book PLAB 2 / CPSA in Manchester; booking availability varies, and popular dates may be weeks or months away. After passing the CPSA, candidates compile their full GMC registration application, which can take two to four months to process depending on document verification requirements. The full IMG journey from first PLAB 1 attempt to active GMC registration typically takes twelve to twenty-four months.

UKMLA Pass Rates and Key Statistics

Publicly available UKMLA pass rate data is still accumulating as the assessment is relatively new. The GMC has indicated plans to publish comprehensive results data in Summer 2026. Based on early published figures and freedom-of-information disclosures, the following picture is emerging:

Candidate GroupAssessmentApproximate Pass Rate (First Attempt)Notes
UK Medical StudentsAKT82–90%Reflects 5-year UK curriculum preparation
IMGs (via PLAB 1)AKT / PLAB 155–70%Post-MLA alignment; stabilising as prep resources adapt
UK Medical StudentsCPSA85–92%Estimated; comprehensive data pending
IMGs (via PLAB 2)CPSA / PLAB 265–78%Historical PLAB 2 range, broadly maintained

The lower first-attempt pass rate for IMGs compared to UK graduates on the AKT does not indicate a harder exam for IMGs — the exam is the same standard. It reflects the difference in preparation context: UK students complete a five-year curriculum specifically structured to meet UKMLA requirements, with dedicated UKMLA preparation built into the final year. IMGs must self-direct their preparation, often while working in their home country, sometimes without access to high-quality UKMLA-specific preparation resources.

What Happens If You Fail the UKMLA?

Failing one or both components of the UKMLA does not mark the end of your UK medical career pathway. Both UK students and IMGs can re-sit the assessment.

For UK medical students, re-sit arrangements vary by medical school. Most schools provide at least one further opportunity within the same academic cycle and may offer additional support, including supervised revision, targeted feedback, and access to pastoral services. Students who fail to achieve a pass after the maximum number of school-permitted attempts may be asked to intercalate or may be referred to a fitness to practise process if there are concerns beyond academic performance alone. The specific re-sit policy is determined by each medical school in consultation with the GMC.

For IMGs, there is no cap on the number of attempts. Each re-sit requires booking and paying for a new sitting (£283 for AKT, £1,036 for CPSA). The GMC provides result feedback that shows performance by domain, helping candidates identify weaker areas for targeted re-preparation. Many IMGs make multiple attempts before passing; this is normal and should not be discouraging. The important principle is to analyse feedback carefully, adjust your preparation strategy, and address identified gaps systematically before re-sitting.

Detailed information on re-sit rules, appeals, and the feedback process is available in our appeals and re-sits guide.

The UKMLA in the Context of UK Medical Education

The UKMLA does not exist in isolation — it is one element of a broader UK medical education and training system that includes undergraduate training, the Foundation Programme, specialty training, and ongoing professional development throughout a medical career. Understanding where the UKMLA fits within this system helps candidates understand its purpose and what it does (and does not) assess.

The UKMLA sets the threshold for entry to the medical workforce. It answers the question: can this person safely practise as a newly qualified Foundation Year 1 doctor? It does not assess specialist knowledge, advanced procedural skills, or the competencies acquired during specialty training — those are tested by the Royal Colleges' specialty examinations (MRCP, MRCS, MRCGP, and others). The UKMLA is explicitly positioned as an entry-level, Foundation-Programme-threshold assessment, benchmarked against what a competent, recently graduated doctor should know and be able to do in their first clinical post.

This positioning has implications for preparation. Candidates should not try to pass the UKMLA by studying at the level of a specialist — they should aim for solid, broad competency across all areas of the MLA Content Map at the level expected of a Foundation doctor. Depth in one area does not compensate for gaps in another; the examination is designed to detect unsafe practice across all domains, not reward extraordinary performance in a single specialty.

Frequently Asked Questions About What Is UKMLA

Is the UKMLA replacing PLAB entirely?

Not exactly. The PLAB examination continues to exist as the route through which IMGs access the UKMLA pathway. From August 2024, PLAB 1 was aligned with the MLA AKT standard and PLAB 2 was aligned with the MLA CPSA standard. So IMGs still book a test labelled PLAB, but the content and standard are now identical to the UKMLA taken by UK graduates. The PLAB brand has not been abolished, but it now functions as the IMG delivery vehicle for what is effectively the same national licensing assessment. See our detailed UKMLA vs PLAB comparison for more on this distinction.

At what stage of medical school do UK students sit the UKMLA?

The UKMLA is a final-year assessment for UK medical students. Most students sit the AKT in the spring of their fifth year (or sixth year for six-year programmes) and the CPSA in the final weeks of the programme. The exact timing varies by medical school, as institutions integrate the UKMLA into their own assessment calendar. Students should check with their medical school's academic office for the specific dates applicable to their cohort.

Can I sit the UKMLA AKT outside the UK?

Yes, if you are an IMG sitting via the PLAB 1 route. Pearson VUE operates over 180 test centres worldwide, and IMGs can sit the AKT at any of these centres. UK medical students, however, sit the AKT as administered by their medical school or at designated centres within the UK. The AKT is the same examination regardless of where it is sat; only the logistics of access differ by candidate group.

What is the Medical Schools Council's role in the UKMLA?

The Medical Schools Council (MSC) works in partnership with the GMC to deliver the AKT for UK medical students. The MSC coordinates the AKT administration across all UK medical schools, handles the technical delivery of the computer-based examination, and manages the aggregated data from medical school sittings. The GMC remains responsible for the content, standard-setting, and overall quality assurance of the UKMLA. For IMGs, the MSC has no direct role; the GMC manages PLAB administration directly through its own team and the Pearson VUE network.

What does provisional GMC registration mean for UK graduates?

UK medical graduates initially receive provisional registration, which allows them to work in approved Foundation Programme posts under supervision. Provisional registration converts to full registration after satisfactory completion of the Foundation Year 1 programme, at which point doctors can work in more autonomous clinical roles. IMGs who pass both PLAB components and meet all other requirements receive full registration directly, without a provisional period, provided they already hold an acceptable internship qualification from their home country.

How long are UKMLA results valid?

AKT results are valid for five years from the date of passing. If a candidate passes the AKT but does not complete the CPSA and full registration within five years, they may need to re-sit the AKT. CPSA results do not have a standalone published validity period under the MLA framework; candidates should check current GMC guidance for any updates. For UK medical students, the UKMLA results are tied to the specific graduation cohort and do not require independent validity management.

Conclusion

The UKMLA is the most significant change to UK medical licensing in a generation. By creating a single, uniform national standard for all doctors entering the UK medical workforce — regardless of whether they trained in Edinburgh, Lagos, Mumbai, or Manila — the GMC has built a more consistent, transparent, and equitable licensing system. For UK medical students, it represents a new feature of their final year with the institutional support of their medical school behind them. For IMGs, it represents a clearly defined, rigorous, but achievable pathway to practising in one of the world's most respected healthcare systems.

Success in the UKMLA requires systematic, content-map-led preparation, with a particular focus on applied clinical reasoning rather than rote memorisation. Early engagement with the official GMC guidance, the MLA Content Map, and quality question banks gives candidates the best possible foundation for passing on their first attempt and beginning their UK medical career on the strongest footing.

Explore our full UKMLA beginner's guide →

post_title: MLA Content Map: The Blueprint Behind Every UKMLA Question
post_excerpt: Every AKT question and CPSA station is written against the MLA Content Map. Here is what the map contains and how to use it as your primary revision guide.
seo_title: MLA Content Map: The Blueprint Behind Every UKMLA Question
seo_description: The MLA Content Map is the official blueprint for all UKMLA questions. Learn what it contains, how it is structured, and how to use it for revision.
html_content:
The MLA content map is the official blueprint that defines exactly what knowledge, skills, and professional behaviours are assessed in every UKMLA examination — and understanding it is the most important strategic decision any candidate can make when planning their preparation. Published and maintained by the General Medical Council (GMC), the MLA content map provides the definitive framework against which all Applied Knowledge Test (AKT) questions are written and all Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA) stations are designed. If you are preparing for the UKMLA or the PLAB examination (which has been aligned to the same standard since August 2024), the MLA content map is not an optional supplement — it is the foundational document that should govern every hour of your revision.

Unlike a traditional medical school curriculum, the MLA content map does not prescribe how content should be taught. It specifies the standard that must be demonstrated in the assessment. This distinction matters practically: two candidates who studied at different institutions, using different textbooks and teaching styles, will both be assessed against the same content map. The candidate who knows the content map well and has deliberately mapped their revision to it will have a structural advantage over the candidate who prepares using a generic approach without reference to the official blueprint.

This guide explains the structure of the MLA content map in full, covers the major changes introduced in the September 2026 update, details the highest-yield areas by frequency, clarifies common misconceptions, and provides a practical framework for using the content map as the cornerstone of your UKMLA preparation.

What the MLA Content Map Contains: The Four Pillars

The MLA content map is organised around four main domains, sometimes referred to as pillars. Each pillar represents a distinct dimension of clinical competency, and both the AKT and CPSA assess across all four pillars — though the balance between them differs between the written and clinical components of the exam.

Pillar 1: Clinical Presentations

Clinical presentations are the patient-facing symptoms or signs that a doctor must be able to assess, investigate, and manage. The content map lists the presentations that may appear in UKMLA questions, organised loosely by body system. Examples include chest pain, shortness of breath, headache, haematuria, weight loss, altered consciousness, limb pain, skin rash, fever, and many dozens more. The September 2026 update expanded the presentations list significantly, with particular additions in women's health (including menstrual irregularities, pelvic pain, and pregnancy-related presentations), paediatrics, and mental health.

Each presentation in the content map can be tested in any clinical context — an important change introduced in September 2026 that removed the previous mapping grid that restricted which presentations could appear in which specialty setting. Chest pain might now appear in the context of a community pharmacy encounter, a GP consultation, an emergency department setting, or an inpatient ward — any of these contexts is valid in the new framework. This change rewards candidates who can apply their knowledge flexibly across settings rather than memorising condition-setting pairs.

Pillar 2: Clinical Conditions

The clinical conditions pillar lists the specific diagnoses and disease entities that are examinable in the UKMLA. The September 2026 update grew this list from approximately 311 conditions to approximately 430, representing a 38% expansion in the number of individually examinable conditions. The additions were concentrated in several areas:

  • Women's health: Expanded coverage of gynaecological conditions, obstetric complications, contraception, and menopause
  • Genetics: Introduction of clinical genetics presentations including chromosomal disorders, genetic counselling scenarios, and inherited conditions
  • Transgender health: New inclusions covering gender dysphoria, hormone therapy considerations, and gender-affirming care
  • Social determinants: Conditions and presentations related to housing, poverty, substance use, and health inequalities
  • Sepsis and infection: Updated sepsis guidance aligned with current NICE and UK Sepsis Trust protocols

For exam preparation, the clinical conditions list is the most direct guide to what can be tested. Every condition on the list is examinable; conditions not on the list cannot appear in the exam. This makes the list a practical prioritisation tool: study everything on the list, and you have theoretically covered the examinable universe. In practice, some conditions appear with much higher frequency than others — see the high-yield areas section below.

Pillar 3: Professional Behaviours

The professional behaviours pillar covers the ethical, legal, and interpersonal dimensions of medical practice. This is not a peripheral add-on to the clinical content — professional behaviours are explicitly assessed in both the AKT (through scenario-based questions about ethical dilemmas, consent, confidentiality, and duty of candour) and the CPSA (through examiner observation of how candidates interact with simulated patients and navigate professional scenarios).

Key areas within the professional behaviours pillar include: consent and capacity (including the Mental Capacity Act 2005, Gillick competence, and best-interests decision-making); confidentiality and information governance; safeguarding (child protection and adult safeguarding frameworks); duty of candour (the professional obligation to be open and honest with patients); communication skills (breaking bad news, shared decision-making, handling complaints); and patient safety (recognising and responding to deteriorating patients, prescribing safety, incident reporting). A candidate who scores consistently in the bottom quartile on professional behaviour questions is at significant risk of failing the AKT, even if clinical knowledge scores are strong.

Pillar 4: Practical Skills

The practical skills pillar lists the procedural and examination skills that are directly assessed in the CPSA and may be tested in AKT questions through description or interpretation scenarios. The skills list includes: clinical examination of major body systems (cardiovascular, respiratory, abdominal, neurological, musculoskeletal); basic life support and advanced life support; venepuncture and cannulation; urethral catheterisation; wound assessment and basic wound management; interpretation of ECGs, blood results, imaging, and spirometry; and safe prescribing including drug calculations.

For AKT preparation, the practical skills pillar means candidates must be able to interpret the results of clinical skills, not just perform them. For CPSA preparation, it means candidates must be able to demonstrate these skills competently with a simulated patient under examiner observation. Practical skills questions in the AKT often take the form of interpreting an ECG tracing, reading a spirometry result, or selecting the correct management step following a described examination finding.

The Three Underpinning Themes

Cutting across all four pillars of the MLA content map are three underpinning themes that the GMC expects to permeate all aspects of clinical practice and therefore all aspects of the UKMLA. These themes are not assessed in isolation — they appear embedded within clinical presentations, condition management, and professional behaviour scenarios throughout both assessment components.

Patient Safety

Patient safety is the primary underpinning theme and reflects the UKMLA's core purpose: ensuring that every licensed doctor is safe to practise. In the AKT, patient safety is assessed through questions about recognising clinical deterioration, appropriate prescribing (including safe dose calculation, drug interactions, and allergy management), escalation pathways, and systemic safety practices such as the World Health Organization surgical safety checklist. In the CPSA, patient safety is assessed behaviourally — does the candidate wash their hands, verify patient identity, explain what they are doing, and escalate concerns appropriately?

Safeguarding

Safeguarding reflects the doctor's statutory and professional duty to protect vulnerable patients — children and adults — from abuse, neglect, and exploitation. Safeguarding questions appear in both AKT and CPSA, typically as scenario-based cases in which a candidate must identify safeguarding concerns, understand the relevant legal framework (Children Act 1989, Mental Capacity Act 2005, Care Act 2014), and take appropriate action. A common AKT format presents a patient with inconsistent history, unusual injury patterns, or circumstances that raise concern, asking the candidate what to do next.

Prescribing

Safe prescribing is an explicit underpinning theme because prescribing errors represent one of the most common and preventable sources of patient harm in UK hospitals. Prescribing-themed questions appear throughout the AKT and may include drug calculations, drug-drug interactions, contraindications in pregnancy or renal impairment, appropriate routes of administration, and NICE-guideline-aligned first-line treatment selection. Many medical schools and preparation courses treat prescribing as a distinct revision category for the UKMLA, and this is well-justified given its prevalence in the exam.

The September 2026 Update: What Changed?

The September 2026 content map update is the most significant revision to the MLA blueprint since the examination launched. All UKMLA and PLAB assessments from September 2026 onwards are assessed against the updated map. Key changes include:

Area of ChangePre-September 2026From September 2026
Number of clinical conditions~311~430
Specialty context mappingConditions mapped to specific specialty contextsMapping grid removed; any condition in any context
Women's health coverageStandard obstetrics and gynaecologySignificantly expanded; menopause, contraception, pelvic conditions
GeneticsMinimal direct inclusionClinical genetics explicitly included
Transgender healthNot explicitly listedExplicitly included with specific presentations
Social determinants of healthImplicit in professional behavioursExplicitly listed as assessable content
Sepsis guidancePrevious frameworkUpdated to current NICE/UK Sepsis Trust guidance

Candidates sitting from September 2026 should ensure their preparation resources are explicitly updated to reflect the new content map. Question banks and revision materials published before October 2025 may not include the expanded content, particularly in genetics, transgender health, and the expanded women's health domain. Always check the publication date and content map alignment of any resource you use.

How Questions Are Mapped to the Content Map

Every single AKT question is mapped to at least one item in the MLA content map before it is approved for use. This mapping process is part of the GMC's quality assurance framework and ensures that no question appears in the examination unless it tests content that is explicitly within the examinable blueprint. The question-writing and review process involves practising clinicians at Foundation Programme level as well as senior clinical educators, ensuring that questions reflect the realistic clinical challenges of a Foundation Year 1 doctor.

Understanding the mapping process has practical implications for preparation. It means you can use the content map defensively: any topic not listed in the content map is not examinable. You do not need to prepare for advanced specialist knowledge, cutting-edge research findings, or conditions that appear only in tertiary subspecialty practice. Your preparation should be bounded by the content map on all sides.

The Content Map Versus the PLAB Syllabus

Before August 2024, PLAB had its own content framework that differed from the MLA content map. After August 2024, all PLAB examinations (both PLAB 1 / AKT and PLAB 2 / CPSA) are assessed against the MLA content map. This means the content map is now the definitive guide for PLAB candidates as well as UKMLA candidates. If you are preparing for PLAB 1 or PLAB 2 in 2025 or 2026, you are preparing for an examination assessed against the MLA content map. Any preparation resource that refers to a separate PLAB syllabus is using outdated terminology; the content they describe should be the MLA content map or it may not be current.

Top 10 Highest-Yield Areas by Frequency

While the content map formally gives equal status to all listed conditions and presentations, analysis of UKMLA and PLAB question distributions reveals that certain clinical areas appear with significantly higher frequency than others. Based on published question bank analyses and community feedback from recent candidates, the highest-yield areas include:

  1. Cardiovascular medicine — ACS management, heart failure, arrhythmias, hypertension, anticoagulation
  2. Respiratory medicine — COPD, asthma, pneumonia, pulmonary embolism, lung cancer
  3. Gastroenterology and hepatology — GI bleeding, IBD, liver disease, abdominal pain assessment
  4. Neurology — Stroke, TIA, headache differentials, seizure management, meningitis
  5. Mental health — Depression, psychosis, anxiety, eating disorders, capacity assessment
  6. Pharmacology and prescribing safety — Drug interactions, dose calculations, prescribing in special populations
  7. Obstetrics and gynaecology — Pregnancy emergencies, ectopic pregnancy, contraception, gynaecological cancers
  8. Paediatrics — Developmental milestones, common childhood illnesses, safeguarding in paediatric contexts
  9. Musculoskeletal medicine — Back pain, fracture management, inflammatory arthritides
  10. Renal medicine and urology — AKI, CKD, UTIs, haematuria, electrolyte disturbances

How to Use the Content Map for Revision

The most effective way to use the MLA content map is as both a planning tool and a progress tracker. At the start of your preparation period, download the latest version of the content map from the GMC website. Work through the clinical conditions list systematically, marking each condition as: fully confident, partially confident, or not yet studied. This gives you a visual representation of your preparation landscape and identifies priority gaps.

For each condition, your revision should cover: the typical clinical presentation (how the patient presents to a Foundation doctor); the initial investigations (what you would order first); the immediate management (initial stabilisation and treatment); definitive management (which may involve specialist referral); and key complications and their management. This five-part framework for each condition, applied systematically across the content map, builds the kind of broad, applied knowledge that the AKT rewards.

For CPSA preparation, use the practical skills list within the content map to audit your clinical skill competency. For each listed skill, practise to the standard expected in an OSCE setting: correct technique, appropriate communication, patient safety awareness, and professional behaviour throughout. Role-playing each station type with a partner and videoing your performance for self-review are highly effective preparation methods.

Common Misconceptions About the MLA Content Map

Several persistent misconceptions about the content map circulate in candidate communities and can lead to misdirected preparation. The most common include: believing that only conditions listed under a specific specialty section can appear in that specialty's clinical context (false since September 2026, when the mapping grid was removed); assuming that the content map is identical to a standard medical textbook's table of contents (it is not — the map is specifically scoped to Foundation-level practice, omitting many advanced specialist conditions); and thinking that the content map changes every year (in practice, major revisions are infrequent, with the September 2026 update being only the second major revision since launch).

Frequently Asked Questions About the MLA Content Map

Where can I download the official MLA content map?

The official MLA content map is available as a free PDF download from the GMC website at gmc-uk.org/education/medical-licensing-assessment/mla-content-map. The GMC publishes both English and Welsh language versions. Always ensure you download the most recent version — the September 2026 update supersedes all earlier versions for assessments from September 2026 onwards. The document is available without registration or payment; be cautious of third-party websites charging fees for a document that is freely available from the GMC.

Does the September 2026 content map update affect candidates who already passed their AKT?

No. If you have already passed the AKT under the previous content map, your result is valid and is not affected by the September 2026 update. AKT results are valid for five years from the date of passing. You will only sit under the new content map if your next assessment (whether AKT, CPSA, or a re-sit) takes place from September 2026 onward. Candidates mid-pathway should check whether their remaining component will fall under the new map and update their preparation accordingly.

How many conditions are in the September 2026 content map?

The September 2026 update expanded the list of examinable clinical conditions from approximately 311 to approximately 430. This represents a 38% increase in the number of individually listed conditions. The most significant expansions were in women's health, genetics, transgender health, and social determinants of health. The full conditions list is available in the downloadable PDF content map from the GMC website.

Are rare conditions likely to appear in the UKMLA?

Conditions in the content map are listed because they represent clinically important presentations that a Foundation doctor may plausibly encounter. The content map does not contain highly specialised, rare subspecialty conditions — it is bounded by the Foundation Programme threshold. Within the conditions that are listed, some are more common in the exam than others (see the high-yield areas section above), but any listed condition is theoretically examinable. A condition listed in the content map that is clinically rare may still appear as an AKT question because it tests important clinical reasoning (e.g., recognising an urgent referral need) even if the underlying condition is uncommon.

Is the MLA content map the same for both UK students and IMGs?

Yes. The MLA content map is a single unified document that applies to all UKMLA assessments — both the AKT and CPSA taken by UK medical students, and the PLAB 1 and PLAB 2 taken by IMGs. This is fundamental to the UKMLA's purpose: creating a single consistent standard for all routes to UK medical licensure. The same conditions, presentations, professional behaviours, and practical skills are examinable regardless of which route a candidate takes to licensure.

Conclusion

The MLA content map is not just an administrative document — it is the single most important resource available to any UKMLA or PLAB candidate. By defining precisely what is and is not examinable, it gives candidates the ability to bound their preparation rationally, prioritise high-yield areas, and audit their knowledge and skill coverage systematically. The September 2026 update has expanded the content significantly, making early familiarity with the updated map more important than ever for candidates preparing for assessments from September 2026 onward.

The candidates who use the content map most effectively are those who treat it as a living document throughout their preparation — not something to glance at once and set aside, but a regular reference point for checking coverage, identifying gaps, and tracking progress. Combined with a high-quality question bank, systematic clinical knowledge revision, and regular OSCE practice, mastery of the MLA content map framework gives candidates the structural foundation for UKMLA success.

Explore the full UKMLA syllabus and content map guide →
