post_title: UKMLA AKT: Format, Question Types, and How to Prepare
post_excerpt: A detailed look at what the Applied Knowledge Test involves — its structure, question style, timing, and the most effective strategies for preparation.
seo_title: UKMLA AKT: Format, Question Types, and How to Prepare
seo_description: Everything you need to know about the UKMLA AKT — format, Single Best Answer question style, timing, and the best strategies to prepare effectively.
html_content:
The UKMLA AKT (Applied Knowledge Test) is the written component of the UK Medical Licensing Assessment and the first of two examinations every doctor must pass before receiving a GMC licence to practise in the UK. Whether you are a UK medical student approaching final year or an International Medical Graduate (IMG) sitting via the PLAB 1 route, the AKT represents a carefully structured test of your ability to apply clinical knowledge to realistic patient scenarios at the level expected of a Foundation Year 1 doctor. Passing the AKT on your first attempt is the gateway to booking the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA), and a strong performance provides the confidence and momentum that carry through into clinical exam preparation.

The AKT differs from the rote-recall model of traditional medical examinations. Questions do not ask you to list the causes of a condition from memory — they present a patient in a specific clinical context and ask you to select the most appropriate next step in investigation, diagnosis, or management. This applied reasoning approach is deliberate: it tests the thinking process a Foundation doctor would actually use at the bedside, not the performance of recalling lists under pressure. Understanding this distinction is the first step in preparing effectively.

This guide covers the exact format, question anatomy, domain breakdown, standard-setting methodology, best preparation resources, common candidate mistakes, and a detailed day-of-exam strategy. By the end, you will have a complete picture of what the UKMLA AKT involves and a structured plan for passing it.

UKMLA AKT Format at a Glance

The AKT consists of 200 Single Best Answer (SBA) questions delivered on-screen, completed over a three-hour (180-minute) session. This gives candidates an average of 54 seconds per question — a pace that rewards both clinical knowledge and efficient decision-making. The examination is delivered through Pearson VUE test centres worldwide for IMGs (via PLAB 1) and at designated sites for UK medical students as coordinated by the Medical Schools Council.

The 200 questions are not split across multiple papers on separate days in the current IMG/PLAB 1 format — all 200 questions are completed in a single session. Some UK medical school versions of the AKT may have slightly different structural arrangements depending on how they integrate the national examination with their institution's own assessment calendar; students should check their medical school's specific guidance for the exact format they will encounter.

FeatureDetail
Question typeSingle Best Answer (SBA) — 5 options, select 1
Total questions200
Total time180 minutes (3 hours)
Average time per question54 seconds
Question formatComputer-based (on-screen)
Negative markingNo
Pass mark typeCriterion-referenced (Angoff method), varies per sitting
Typical pass mark range53–68% of items correct
Delivery for IMGsPearson VUE centres worldwide
Delivery for UK studentsDesignated centres coordinated by Medical Schools Council

There is no negative marking in the AKT. Every question left unanswered scores zero; every question answered incorrectly also scores zero. This means there is never a rational reason to leave a question blank — always select your best guess, even if you are uncertain. A guessed answer on a 5-option question gives a 20% chance of being correct; a blank guarantees zero.

What the UKMLA AKT Tests: Domain Breakdown

The AKT is mapped against the MLA Content Map across all four pillars (clinical presentations, clinical conditions, professional behaviours, and practical skills) and all three underpinning themes (patient safety, safeguarding, prescribing). The distribution of questions across clinical domains is not publicly specified to a precise percentage breakdown, but analysis of question bank content and community feedback from recent sittings suggests the following approximate distribution:

Clinical DomainApproximate % of AKT QuestionsKey Topics
Internal medicine (medicine)25–30%Cardiology, respiratory, gastroenterology, neurology, renal
Surgery and acute care10–15%Surgical emergencies, wound management, post-op care
Mental health and psychiatry10–12%Depression, psychosis, substance use, capacity, eating disorders
Obstetrics and gynaecology8–12%Pregnancy complications, contraception, gynaecological cancers
Paediatrics8–10%Developmental assessment, childhood illness, safeguarding
Primary care and public health6–10%Health promotion, screening, disease prevention
Professional practice and ethics8–10%Consent, confidentiality, duty of candour, GMC guidance
Pharmacology and prescribing5–8%Drug interactions, safe prescribing, dose calculation
Other (dermatology, ENT, ophthalmology, MSK, etc.)10–15%Presentations from less common specialties

These percentages are indicative and vary between sittings. The removal of the specialty context mapping grid in September 2026 means that any condition from any area of the content map can appear in any clinical setting, making broad coverage more important than ever.

SBA Question Anatomy: How AKT Questions Are Written

Every SBA question in the AKT follows a standard structural template. Understanding this template is essential for efficient, accurate answering. The standard AKT question consists of three parts:

The Clinical Vignette

The vignette presents a patient scenario. It typically includes: patient demographics (age, sex, sometimes occupation or social context); the presenting complaint; a brief history; relevant examination findings; and, often, investigation results (blood tests, imaging, ECG findings). UKMLA AKT vignettes are longer and more detailed than pre-MLA PLAB 1 vignettes, reflecting the shift toward applied reasoning rather than recall. A typical vignette is 60–120 words long. Candidates who read slowly or who try to hold every detail in working memory during reading will struggle with time management.

The Stem Question

The stem is the question itself. Common formats include: What is the most likely diagnosis? / What is the most appropriate next investigation? / What is the most appropriate initial management? / What is the most likely causative organism? / What is the most appropriate course of action regarding this patient's capacity? The stem tells you the cognitive skill being tested — diagnosis, investigation selection, management, or professional behaviour. Reading the stem before the vignette can help some candidates focus their reading on the most relevant information.

The Five Answer Options

Five answer options are provided, labelled A to E. One is the single best answer; four are distractors. In well-written SBA questions, two or three distractors will be plausible — the correct answer is not necessarily the only defensible answer, but it is the best according to current UK clinical guidelines (primarily NICE, BNF, and GMC guidance). High-scoring candidates know not just why the correct answer is right, but exactly why each distractor is less correct than the chosen option.

AKT Scoring and Standard Setting

The AKT pass mark is not fixed at a predetermined percentage. It is determined for each sitting using a process called criterion-referenced standard setting, specifically the Angoff method. In this process, a panel of practising clinicians (typically Foundation Programme-level doctors and educators) reviews each question before the sitting and estimates the probability that a minimally competent Foundation Year 1 doctor would answer it correctly. The sum of these probabilities across all questions generates the expected raw score of the minimally competent candidate — and this becomes the pass mark for that sitting.

The practical implication is that the pass mark varies between sittings, typically falling in the range of 53–68% of items correct. A particularly difficult question set will generate a lower pass mark; a more straightforward set will generate a higher one. This system is fairer than a fixed percentage because it adjusts for variation in question difficulty between sittings rather than penalising candidates who happen to sit on a difficult day.

Results are typically released three to four weeks after sitting. The result report shows a pass or fail outcome together with a domain-level performance profile. Candidates who fail receive feedback showing their performance relative to the pass mark across clinical domains, which is invaluable for targeting re-sit preparation.

AKT vs Traditional Written Medical Exams: Key Differences

Understanding how the AKT differs from the types of written examinations most medical graduates experienced during their training helps candidates adjust their preparation strategy. Many IMG candidates in particular have extensive experience with recall-based examinations from their home country systems, and may need to consciously shift their revision approach.

  • Applied reasoning vs recall: The AKT tests what you would do, not what you can list. Preparation should focus on decision-making frameworks, not fact memorisation.
  • Longer, more detailed vignettes: AKT vignettes are typically 60–120 words compared to 20–40 words in older-style PLAB questions. Speed reading and selective information extraction are important skills.
  • UK guideline alignment: All management questions are answered according to NICE guidelines, BNF, and current UK clinical practice. Candidates from non-UK backgrounds must explicitly study UK management pathways, which may differ from their home country standards.
  • No negative marking: Unlike some international examinations, the AKT does not penalise incorrect answers. Always answer every question.
  • Criterion-referenced pass mark: The pass mark adjusts per sitting, unlike percentage-threshold systems. This means comparisons of raw scores between sittings are not meaningful.

UKMLA AKT Preparation Strategies

Effective AKT preparation requires a systematic, content-map-led approach over a sustained period. Most candidates benefit from a preparation period of three to six months for a first attempt, though candidates with recent clinical experience in a UK context may require less, and candidates preparing from scratch may need more.

Build Your Foundation with the MLA Content Map

Download the current MLA content map from the GMC website and use it as your primary organising document. Work through the conditions list systematically, rather than by specialty interest or perceived difficulty. For each condition, study the typical presentation, differential diagnosis, initial investigations, immediate management, and key complications at Foundation-level depth. Avoid going deeper than Foundation level — you are preparing for an entry-level licensing examination, not a specialist membership exam.

Use a Quality Question Bank as Your Primary Learning Tool

Question banks are the most effective single preparation tool for the AKT. High-quality question banks aligned to the MLA content map include detailed explanations that teach the reasoning behind each answer, not just the correct option. When using a question bank, do not simply read the explanation for the question you got wrong — read the explanations for all five options of every question, including the ones you answered correctly. Understanding why each distractor is less correct than the right answer is a higher-level cognitive skill that the AKT specifically rewards.

Popular AKT question banks include resources from MedRevisions, Pastest, UKMLA Question Bank, AiMedQs, and Quesmed, among others. Subscription costs range from approximately £40 to £200. Using multiple question banks with different question writing styles helps expose you to the variety of approaches you will encounter in the real examination.

Simulate Exam Conditions Regularly

Use timed mock papers to simulate the pacing demands of the real AKT. Set a 180-minute timer, work through 200 questions without interruption, and resist the urge to look up answers mid-session. Time pressure is a genuine challenge in the AKT — the 54-second average gives very little room for extended deliberation on any single question. Practise the habit of spending no more than 90 seconds on a flagged question before making your best guess and moving on.

Focus on UK Guidelines

NICE guidelines are the primary reference for AKT management questions. Key guidelines to know well include those for hypertension, diabetes, asthma, COPD, heart failure, AKI, CKD, depression, schizophrenia, dementia, and common cancers. The BNF (British National Formulary) is essential for prescribing questions. The GMC's Good Medical Practice is the reference document for all professional behaviour and ethical questions. Candidates from non-UK backgrounds who are familiar with different national guidelines (WHO, SIGN, non-UK national guidelines) must actively overwrite these with UK-specific pathways for exam purposes.

AKT Booking for IMGs (PLAB 1): Logistics

IMGs sit the AKT through the PLAB 1 booking system on the GMC website. To book, you must have an active GMC Online account and have your eligibility to sit PLAB confirmed (including having your medical qualification recognised and, if required for your nationality, a valid visa or appropriate immigration status to access the test centre country). Bookings are made at Pearson VUE test centres using the GMC's booking portal; you select your preferred country, city, and date. Payment of £283 is required at the time of booking.

Popular test centres in high-demand countries (India, Nigeria, Pakistan, Egypt, the Philippines) fill quickly. It is advisable to book two to three months in advance to secure your preferred date and location. If your first-choice date is not available, consider nearby cities or neighbouring countries with available slots. Test centre experience is standardised — every Pearson VUE centre delivers the same software interface, equipment, and security protocols regardless of location.

Frequently Asked Questions About the UKMLA AKT

How many times can I sit the AKT if I fail?

There is no limit on the number of AKT attempts for IMGs. UK medical students' re-sit opportunities are governed by their medical school's academic regulations, which vary by institution. Each IMG re-sit requires a fresh booking and payment of £283. Most candidates who invest in targeted re-preparation after a fail — using their domain-level feedback report to prioritise weak areas — are successful on their next attempt. The AKT feedback report clearly shows performance across clinical domains, making it a powerful guide for focused second-attempt preparation.

Do I need to travel to the UK to sit the AKT?

No. IMGs sit the AKT (via PLAB 1) at Pearson VUE test centres in over 180 countries worldwide. You do not need to be in the UK to sit the AKT. This is a significant logistical and financial advantage compared to the CPSA, which must be taken in Manchester. You can complete the entire AKT stage of the UKMLA pathway from your home country.

What score do I need to pass the AKT?

The AKT pass mark is criterion-referenced and set separately for each sitting using the Angoff method, so there is no single fixed score. The pass mark typically falls in the range of 53–68% of the 200 questions answered correctly (106–136 questions). You will not know the exact pass mark for your sitting until results are released; focus on maximising your raw score rather than targeting a specific percentage threshold.

How long are AKT results valid?

AKT results (for IMGs) are valid for five years from the date of the sitting. You must pass the CPSA and complete GMC registration within this five-year window, or you may need to re-sit the AKT. This validity period gives IMGs substantial flexibility in the timing of their CPSA preparation without risking the expiry of their AKT result.

Is the AKT the same as PLAB 1?

From August 2024 onward, PLAB 1 is assessed against the MLA Content Map and meets the requirements of the AKT. In practical terms, if you book and sit PLAB 1, you are sitting an examination that meets the MLA AKT standard. The booking portal, fee, and logistics remain labelled as PLAB 1, but the content, question style, and standard are now equivalent to the UKMLA AKT sat by UK medical students.

Conclusion

The UKMLA AKT is a rigorous but very achievable examination for candidates who prepare systematically, understand the applied-reasoning question style, and align their revision with the MLA Content Map and UK clinical guidelines. The key insight that separates high-scorers from those who struggle is the shift from passive information absorption to active clinical reasoning practice — every question in your preparation should be an exercise in thinking through the clinical problem as a Foundation doctor would, not an exercise in checking whether you can recall a fact.

Start your preparation with the MLA Content Map as your organising framework, build solid clinical knowledge across all domains, practise extensively with high-quality SBA question banks, simulate timed exam conditions regularly, and focus on UK-specific guidelines and management pathways. With this approach, passing the AKT on your first attempt is a realistic and achievable goal for the vast majority of well-prepared candidates.

post_title: UKMLA CPSA: What the Clinical Exam Really Tests
post_excerpt: The CPSA goes beyond clinical knowledge to assess how you communicate, examine, and perform under real conditions. Here is what examiners are actually looking for.
seo_title: UKMLA CPSA: What the Clinical Exam Really Tests
seo_description: The UKMLA CPSA tests more than clinical knowledge. Discover what examiners look for in history taking, examinations, procedures, and communication stations.
html_content:
The UKMLA CPSA — the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment — is the practical, face-to-face component of the UK Medical Licensing Assessment, and for many candidates it is both the most daunting and the most rewarding part of the entire licensing journey. Unlike the Applied Knowledge Test (AKT), which you can sit at a test centre anywhere in the world, the CPSA places you in a room with trained simulated patients and clinical examiners who observe, in real time, whether you can apply your medical knowledge as a safe, communicative, and professional Foundation Year 1 doctor. No amount of theoretical preparation fully substitutes for practising the physical and interpersonal demands of an OSCE circuit under exam conditions — and understanding exactly what is tested, how it is marked, and what examiners are looking for is the foundation of effective preparation.

For UK medical students, the CPSA is integrated into the final year of the degree programme at their medical school, typically delivered in a similar format to existing OSCEs, often on familiar ground with trusted faculty. For International Medical Graduates (IMGs), the CPSA is taken via the PLAB 2 route at the GMC's dedicated Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester — a purpose-built facility staffed by professional simulated patients and trained examiners. The experience can be unfamiliar and pressured, particularly for candidates who have never experienced a UK-style OSCE, which is why preparation for the CPSA demands both clinical competence and emotional readiness.

This guide provides a comprehensive breakdown of the CPSA format, station types, marking criteria, scoring, logistics, preparation strategies, and the specific communication behaviours that consistently earn marks. By the time you finish reading, you will have a clear picture of what the CPSA is testing and how to demonstrate it confidently.

CPSA Format: Stations, Timing, and Circuit Structure

The CPSA is structured as an OSCE (Objective Structured Clinical Examination) comprising 18 stations of 8 minutes each. Candidates rotate through the stations in a circuit, with brief transition times between stations to read the instructions for the next scenario posted outside the station door. The total assessed time in the CPSA is therefore 144 minutes (18 stations Ã— 8 minutes), with additional time for transitions, briefings, and administrative procedures.

Each station is fully self-contained: you enter, address the scenario presented, and exit when the bell sounds. There is no carryover between stations — a poor performance at one station does not affect the assessment at the next. This is an important psychological fact that experienced OSCE candidates use to their advantage: whatever happened in the previous station is irrelevant the moment you cross the threshold of the next one. The circuit is designed so that consecutive stations test different skill types, preventing fatigue in any single domain.

CPSA FeatureDetail
Total stations18
Time per station8 minutes
Total assessed time144 minutes
Location (IMGs)GMC Clinical Assessment Centre, Manchester
Simulated patientsTrained professional actors
Examiner per station1 trained examiner per station
Marking schemeGlobal rating scale + structured checklist
Pass/fail determinationOverall circuit performance (not per-station)
Pre-requisiteMust have passed the AKT/PLAB 1
Fee (IMGs, 2026)£1,036

What Each CPSA Station Type May Assess

CPSA stations are designed to mirror the realistic clinical situations a Foundation Year 1 doctor would encounter. The GMC does not publish a definitive list of station types used in each sitting, but based on the MLA Content Map and the structure of PLAB 2, stations will typically assess one or more of the following skill categories.

History-Taking Stations

These stations present you with a patient (simulated) who has a clinical complaint. Your task is to take a focused, structured history within the time available. Good history-taking at CPSA level means: introducing yourself professionally, establishing rapport quickly, using open questions to elicit the presenting complaint, covering the key relevant history components (symptom onset, character, radiation, severity, timing, associated symptoms, relevant past history, medications, allergies, family history, social history where relevant), and demonstrating you are listening actively. Candidates who launch immediately into a rapid-fire checklist of closed questions without establishing rapport, or who run out of time before covering the essentials, score poorly.

Communication and Counselling Stations

These stations test your ability to communicate effectively in a clinical context. Scenarios may include: breaking bad news to a patient about a new diagnosis; explaining an investigation and obtaining consent; discussing a treatment plan and addressing concerns; navigating a patient who is anxious, angry, or in denial; or speaking to a carer or family member about a patient's condition. Communication stations are assessed heavily on both the process (how you communicate — tone, pace, empathy, checking understanding, non-verbal behaviour) and the content (whether you covered the medically necessary points accurately and completely).

Clinical Examination Stations

These stations ask you to examine a patient (simulated or standardised) or a clinical model. Systems typically include cardiovascular, respiratory, abdominal, neurological (upper limb, lower limb, cranial nerves), and musculoskeletal. The marking scheme for examination stations rewards: appropriate clinical setup (positioning, exposure, patient comfort, informed consent), a systematic and fluent examination technique, correct interpretation of findings, and professional communication throughout. Candidates who miss required examination steps, use incorrect technique, or fail to communicate findings to the examiner will lose marks.

Practical Procedures Stations

These stations assess competency in clinical procedures on task trainers or mannequins. Procedures tested at CPSA level may include: venepuncture, peripheral venous cannulation, basic life support (BLS) and advanced life support (ALS), urinary catheterisation, ECG electrode placement and interpretation, blood pressure measurement, peak flow measurement, and injection technique. As with examination stations, the marking scheme rewards correct technique, appropriate patient communication, and safety behaviours (hand hygiene, sharps safety, patient identification).

Clinical Reasoning and Decision-Making Stations

These stations present a clinical scenario and require you to demonstrate your reasoning process — for example, reviewing investigation results and formulating a management plan, or assessing a deteriorating patient and deciding on escalation. These stations test the integration of clinical knowledge with decision-making under time pressure, and they often reward candidates who think out loud, narrate their reasoning to the examiner, and demonstrate awareness of when to seek senior support.

Prescribing Stations

Some CPSA sittings include stations requiring the candidate to write or review a prescription, calculate a drug dose, or identify a prescribing error. These stations test safe prescribing practice including: correct drug name, dose, route, frequency, duration; appropriate monitoring; and awareness of contraindications and interactions. Prescribing stations are typically marked by a combination of checklist (correct entries) and global rating (did the candidate demonstrate safe prescribing behaviours overall).

What Examiners Are Looking For: The Marking Scheme

Each CPSA station is marked using a dual marking scheme consisting of a structured checklist and a global rating. Understanding both elements helps candidates understand what to prioritise during preparation.

The Structured Checklist

The checklist is a list of specific tasks, behaviours, or statements that the examiner records as present or absent during the station. For a history-taking station, checklist items might include things like: asked about onset of symptoms; asked about associated chest pain; asked about medication history; explained what you were doing next. For a procedure station, checklist items might include: washed hands before starting; verified patient identity; obtained verbal consent; used correct technique for step X. Checklist items are typically binary — the examiner marks them as done or not done. Candidates can significantly improve their checklist scores by practising with a structured approach to each station type, ensuring they systematically cover the expected components within the eight-minute window.

The Global Rating Scale

The global rating is the examiner's holistic judgment of the candidate's performance on a scale (typically 1–5 or similar). It reflects qualities that resist full capture in a checklist: the overall quality of communication, the degree of patient-centredness, clinical reasoning depth, professionalism under pressure, and the extent to which the candidate performed at the level expected of a competent Foundation Year 1 doctor. A candidate who ticks all the checklist items but does so in a cold, robotic, or rushed manner may score poorly on the global rating; a candidate who misses one checklist item but demonstrates excellent clinical reasoning and patient communication throughout may still pass the station comfortably.

How CPSA Scores Are Calculated and the Pass/Fail Decision

The pass/fail decision for the CPSA is based on overall circuit performance, not performance at individual stations. This means you can perform poorly at one or two stations without necessarily failing the overall circuit, as long as your cumulative performance across all 18 stations meets the standard. The pass mark for the CPSA is set using criterion-referenced standard setting, analogous to the AKT, and varies slightly between sittings.

Candidates who fail the CPSA receive a structured feedback report that shows their performance across station types and domains. This feedback is genuinely useful for re-preparation: it identifies whether weakness lies predominantly in clinical examination technique, communication skills, clinical reasoning, or procedural competence, allowing targeted practice in the areas that cost the most marks.

CPSA Preparation: Timeline and Methods

Effective CPSA preparation requires more lead time and a more active preparation style than the AKT. Most candidates benefit from a dedicated CPSA preparation period of eight to twelve weeks, ideally including a mix of solo practice, partner role-play, and structured feedback from experienced practitioners.

Solo Preparation

Begin by reviewing the MLA Content Map's practical skills list and ensuring you can describe the correct technique for each listed skill. Use anatomy and clinical skills textbooks (Oxford Handbook of Clinical Medicine, Macleod's Clinical Examination) to review examination technique. Watch structured clinical skills videos to see correct technique demonstrated. Write out frameworks for history-taking for each major presentation type, and practise reciting these frameworks aloud to build fluency.

Partner Role-Play Practice

Role-play with a preparation partner is the most valuable CPSA preparation activity. Set up mock stations with a partner taking the role of the simulated patient, using scenario prompts from OSCE preparation books or online resources. Use a timer set to eight minutes. After each station, exchange detailed feedback using the examiner's dual marking scheme. Rotate roles so both partners develop feedback skills as well as station skills. Candidates who practise this way weekly throughout their preparation period consistently report feeling dramatically more confident in the real circuit.

Video Review

Recording yourself performing clinical skills and reviewing the footage is an underused but highly effective preparation tool. Watching your own performance reveals habits you cannot detect in real time: poor eye contact, rushed examination technique, missed steps, unclear communication. Reviewing even two or three recordings of your history-taking or examination technique can yield insights that hours of solo study do not.

In-Person Preparation Courses

For IMGs preparing for PLAB 2 / CPSA in Manchester, structured in-person preparation courses are widely available in the UK and range from one-day introductory sessions to two-week intensive programmes. These courses typically provide professional simulated patients, mock circuits, examiner feedback, and technique workshops. The investment (£500–£1,500 for a quality course) is often justified by the significant reduction in re-sit risk it provides.

CPSA Logistics for IMGs: Booking and the Manchester Experience

The GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester is a purpose-built OSCE facility that runs PLAB 2 / CPSA sittings continuously throughout the year. IMGs must have passed PLAB 1 (AKT) before booking PLAB 2. Booking is done through GMC Online, and the £1,036 fee is payable at the time of booking. Availability at the Manchester centre is in high demand; popular dates can be booked out months in advance, so booking as early as possible after receiving your PLAB 1 pass result is strongly recommended.

On the day of the CPSA, candidates should arrive at the centre with their valid identification (passport for international candidates). Dress code is smart professional attire — typically smart trousers or skirt, shirt or blouse, and appropriate footwear. No clinical uniforms are required (the centre provides these if needed). Bring nothing unnecessary into the assessment area; a small bag can be stored in a designated area. Arrive at least 30 minutes before your start time; late arrivals may be unable to sit.

Top Communication Phrases That Score Marks

Specific phrases and communication behaviours that recur in high-scoring CPSA performances include:

  • Introduction: "Hello, my name is [name] and I'm one of the doctors here today. Would it be all right if I [took a history / examined you / discussed your results] with you?"
  • Signposting: "I'd like to start by asking you some questions about your symptoms, and then I'll examine you. Does that sound okay?"
  • Empathy: "That sounds very difficult for you. I'm sorry to hear you've been going through this."
  • Checking understanding: "I've covered quite a lot of information there — what questions do you have?" / "Can I check that I've explained that clearly enough?"
  • Escalation: "I'd like to get one of my senior colleagues involved because I want to make sure we give you the best care possible."
  • Safety-netting: "If things change or you have any concerns before your next appointment, please don't hesitate to come back or call us."
  • Consent: "Before I examine you, I'd like to explain what I'm going to do. Is that all right with you?"

Frequently Asked Questions About the UKMLA CPSA

Can I fail one CPSA station and still pass overall?

Yes. The CPSA pass/fail decision is based on cumulative circuit performance across all 18 stations, not on whether every individual station is passed. Poor performance at one or two stations can be compensated by strong performance across the remainder of the circuit. This design prevents the loss of a single station — perhaps due to nerves, an unfamiliar scenario, or a temporary lapse — from determining the overall outcome. However, consistently weak performance across multiple stations will result in a fail, and candidates should aim for confident, competent performance in every station rather than banking on compensation from strong stations.

How do I book the CPSA as an IMG in Manchester?

You must have passed PLAB 1 (AKT) before you can book PLAB 2 (CPSA). Log in to your GMC Online account, navigate to the PLAB 2 booking section, select your preferred sitting date and time at the Manchester Clinical Assessment Centre, and pay the £1,036 fee. Confirmation is sent by email with details of the centre address, arrival instructions, and what to bring on the day. GMC advises booking as early as possible as dates are in high demand.

What is the pass rate for the CPSA / PLAB 2?

Historical PLAB 2 pass rates have ranged approximately 65–78% for first-attempt IMG candidates. The pass rate for UK medical students in their CPSA is estimated at 85–92%, reflecting the advantage of completing a five-year UK curriculum that includes structured OSCE training throughout. The CPSA is designed so that a well-prepared candidate who has practised OSCE stations systematically should comfortably meet the Foundation-level standard.

Can I receive feedback on my CPSA performance after failing?

Yes. Candidates who fail the CPSA receive a structured performance feedback report that shows their scores across the different station types and domains assessed in the circuit. This feedback is sent with the result notification and provides a domain-level breakdown (e.g., communication, clinical examination, professional behaviour, practical skills) that identifies where the candidate's performance fell below the standard. This feedback should be treated as a precise re-preparation guide: invest the most study time in the domains where the feedback indicates the greatest shortfall.

What should I wear to the CPSA in Manchester?

Smart professional attire is expected for the CPSA. Think in terms of NHS clinical professional dress: smart trousers or skirt, a shirt or blouse, and appropriate closed-toe shoes. Clinical uniform or scrubs are not required — the GMC centre provides these if needed. Avoid jewellery that might interfere with clinical examination (rings that could catch on a stethoscope, long necklaces). Your clothing should be clean, professional, and practical enough to allow you to move comfortably during examination stations.

Conclusion

The UKMLA CPSA is unlike any written examination — it is a live performance assessment that tests clinical competence, communication, and professionalism in real time, under observation, across eighteen consecutive clinical scenarios. The candidates who perform best are not necessarily those with the deepest specialist knowledge, but those who have systematically practised the skills the CPSA tests, understand what examiners are looking for, and have the emotional readiness to perform consistently through a demanding circuit.

Preparation for the CPSA should be active, social, and realistic: role-play with partners using timed mock stations, seek structured feedback, record and review your performance, and attend a preparation course if your budget allows. Combine this active preparation with systematic revision of the MLA Content Map's practical skills list and the professional behaviour frameworks expected in communication stations, and you will arrive in Manchester ready to demonstrate the Foundation-level standard with confidence.

post_title: UKMLA vs PLAB: What Is the Difference?
post_excerpt: Confused about UKMLA and PLAB? Both lead to GMC registration but follow different routes. This guide clarifies who sits which exam and what changed.
seo_title: UKMLA vs PLAB: Key Differences Explained for IMGs
seo_description: Unsure about UKMLA vs PLAB? Both share the same MLA Content Map standard. We explain who sits each route, key differences, and what changed from 2024.
html_content:
Understanding the UKMLA vs PLAB distinction is one of the most frequently asked questions from International Medical Graduates (IMGs) considering the UK medical registration pathway in 2025 and 2026. The short answer is that PLAB and the UKMLA are now fundamentally the same examination assessed against the same standard — but the terminology, history, and practical differences between the two pathways remain important for candidates to understand. Confusion about whether you are sitting a PLAB or a UKMLA exam, what the differences are in prerequisites and fees, and what the transition from the old PLAB system to the MLA framework means for your registration application is understandable given how quickly this landscape has changed since 2024.

This guide untangles the full picture: the history of PLAB, how and why the MLA framework was introduced, what changed in 2024, how PLAB and the UKMLA now relate to one another, what the change means for current and future IMG candidates, and whether there are any remaining practical differences between the two routes. We also address common misconceptions, including the belief that PLAB is being abolished, and provide a comprehensive comparison table covering more than ten dimensions of difference.

The History of PLAB: Before the UKMLA

The Professional and Linguistic Assessments Board (PLAB) test has been the primary route to GMC registration for International Medical Graduates since the 1970s. It was introduced to provide a standardised assessment of the clinical knowledge and professional skills of doctors trained outside the UK who wished to work in the NHS, at a time when the volume of IMG applications to the GMC was growing rapidly and the existing mutual recognition frameworks did not cover the majority of source countries.

For most of its history, PLAB consisted of two parts. PLAB 1 was a written multiple-choice examination of clinical knowledge, and PLAB 2 was an OSCE-style clinical assessment. Both were administered by the GMC, with PLAB 2 held at the GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester. PLAB was entirely separate from the assessments used by UK medical schools for their own graduation requirements — there was no formal content or standard alignment between PLAB and UK medical finals. This meant that the threshold for GMC registration was set by two different assessment systems that did not formally test the same things in the same way.

This inconsistency attracted increasing attention from medical education researchers, patient safety advocates, and professional bodies. The GMC's own analysis showed that the entry-level knowledge and skills tested by PLAB and UK medical school finals were not equivalently defined. A series of GMC consultations, Medical Schools Council discussions, and expert reviews during the 2010s and early 2020s led to the decision to create a single national medical licensing assessment — the Medical Licensing Assessment (MLA), popularly known as the UKMLA.

What Changed in 2024: The MLA Transition

The key transition date for the MLA framework is August 2024. From this point, all PLAB 1 examinations were aligned with the MLA Applied Knowledge Test (AKT) standard and assessed against the MLA Content Map. From the same date, PLAB 2 was formally aligned with the MLA Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA) standard. This means that from August 2024 onward, IMGs who book and sit PLAB 1 and PLAB 2 are, in effect, sitting examinations that meet the UKMLA requirements — the MLA AKT and CPSA respectively.

For UK medical students, the transition happened through their medical schools: from the 2024–25 academic year onward, all UK medical school final assessments must incorporate the UKMLA AKT and CPSA as part of the graduation requirements. Medical schools that had been running pilot programmes since 2022 transitioned fully; those that had not been piloting joined the framework from 2024–25.

The significance of this change cannot be overstated: for the first time in the history of UK medical licensing, there is a single common standard — defined by the MLA Content Map — that applies equally to every route to GMC registration. Whether a doctor graduated from the University of Edinburgh, the University of Lagos, Bangalore Medical College, or Cairo University, they now meet the same licensing standard before being admitted to the UK medical register.

UKMLA vs PLAB: The Comprehensive Comparison

DimensionUKMLA (UK Medical Student Route)PLAB (IMG Route)
Who takes itUK medical graduates from GMC-approved schoolsInternational Medical Graduates (IMGs)
Assessment standardMLA Content Map (identical)MLA Content Map (identical)
Written componentAKT (200 SBA questions, 3 hours)PLAB 1 = AKT (same exam standard)
Clinical componentCPSA (18-station OSCE, Manchester or medical school)PLAB 2 = CPSA (same standard, Manchester only)
Exam fee (written)Covered by medical school (no direct cost)£283 per attempt
Exam fee (clinical)Covered by medical school (no direct cost)£1,036 per attempt
GMC registration fee~£165 (provisional registration)£481 (full registration)
English language requirementNot separately required (assessed as part of degree)IELTS 7.5 / OET Grade B in all components
Internship requirementCompleted in UK Foundation Programme12 months post-graduation internship required
Written test locationUK only (coordinated by Medical Schools Council)Pearson VUE centres worldwide (180+ countries)
Clinical test locationStudent's medical schoolGMC Clinical Assessment Centre, Manchester only
Registration outcomeProvisional then full registrationFull registration directly
Number of attempts allowedLimited by medical school re-sit policyNo cap on number of attempts

Who Sits the UKMLA?

The UKMLA in its native form — as an examination organised and administered by the medical school in partnership with the GMC and Medical Schools Council — is taken by UK medical students in their final year. All students enrolled in a GMC-approved UK undergraduate medical programme from the 2024–25 academic year onward must pass the UKMLA as a condition of graduation and as a prerequisite for provisional GMC registration. The assessment is coordinated by the student's institution; no separate application or booking is required by individual students beyond responding to their school's invitation.

UK medical graduates typically receive provisional registration upon successful graduation and UKMLA completion, proceed into the Foundation Programme (a two-year structured training programme), and receive full registration upon satisfactory completion of Foundation Year 1. This is the standard UK doctor training pathway and has not changed significantly in its broad structure with the introduction of the UKMLA — only the assessment component has been standardised.

Who Sits PLAB?

IMGs who wish to practise medicine in the UK must sit the PLAB route — which, as of August 2024, is assessed against the same MLA standard as the UKMLA. Any doctor who holds a primary medical qualification from outside the UK and who is not eligible for registration via a specialist route (such as having a recognised specialty qualification) must sit PLAB 1 and PLAB 2 to meet the knowledge and skills requirements for GMC registration.

The PLAB pathway is open to doctors from any country. There are no nationality or language-of-training restrictions. However, candidates must meet the GMC's eligibility requirements, which include: holding an acceptable primary medical qualification; demonstrating English language proficiency (IELTS Academic with an overall score of 7.5 and no component below 7.0, or OET with a minimum Grade B in all four sub-tests); and completing a recognised internship of at least twelve months following graduation from their primary medical programme. Only once these prerequisites are confirmed can a candidate book PLAB 1 and begin the formal examination pathway.

Is PLAB Being Abolished? The Future of the Pathway

No. Despite widespread speculation in IMG communities, PLAB is not being abolished. The PLAB examination continues to exist as the delivery mechanism through which IMGs access the MLA standard. What has changed is that the content of PLAB — the questions asked in PLAB 1 and the stations used in PLAB 2 — must now meet the MLA Content Map standard rather than the pre-2024 PLAB content framework. The name PLAB remains; the standard has been updated to align with the UKMLA.

The GMC has been clear on this point. In their official communications, they describe PLAB as the pathway by which IMGs meet the MLA requirements: PLAB 1 is the mechanism by which IMGs meet the AKT requirement; PLAB 2 is the mechanism by which they meet the CPSA requirement. For practical purposes, an IMG who passes PLAB 1 and PLAB 2 under the current framework has met the UKMLA standard. The examination brand persists, but it now points to the same destination as the UKMLA taken by UK graduates.

Which Route Is Harder? A Fair Assessment

A common question among candidates is whether the UKMLA route (for UK students) or the PLAB route (for IMGs) is harder. The honest answer is: neither is inherently harder in terms of the standard set — both are assessed against the same MLA Content Map at the same Foundation Year 1 level. The differences in observed pass rates (higher for UK students) reflect preparation context rather than assessment standard.

UK medical students have significant structural advantages: a five-year curriculum explicitly designed to prepare them for the UKMLA, institutional support including mock exams, faculty feedback, and OSCE practice facilities, familiarity with UK clinical guidelines and the NHS system, and the social and emotional support of their peer cohort. IMGs must self-direct their preparation, often from outside the UK, without access to the same institutional infrastructure, sometimes while working in clinical roles that leave limited time for dedicated study, and in some cases without recent exposure to the UK clinical context that the exam reflects.

These are preparation context differences, not assessment standard differences. An IMG who prepares thoroughly, using high-quality MLA-aligned resources, systematically studying the MLA Content Map and UK clinical guidelines, and practising OSCE skills regularly, can and does pass PLAB 1 and PLAB 2 at rates comparable to UK graduates. The gap in first-attempt pass rates is not inevitable — it reflects the average difference in preparation conditions, not any inherent difference in difficulty.

Transition Rules for Candidates Mid-Pathway

A common concern among IMGs who began their PLAB journey before August 2024 is what the transition means for their existing results. The GMC has handled this clearly: PLAB 1 results obtained before August 2024 retain their five-year validity and are recognised as meeting the MLA AKT requirement. PLAB 2 results obtained before August 2024 similarly retain their validity. Candidates who passed PLAB 1 under the old framework and are booking PLAB 2 after August 2024 will sit a PLAB 2 that is MLA CPSA-aligned, but their valid PLAB 1 result is still accepted.

Candidates who passed only one component before August 2024 do not need to re-sit that component — provided their result is within the five-year validity period. The GMC has not imposed any requirement to retake the newly aligned version of a component you have already passed. Always verify current guidance directly with the GMC if you are in this position, as policies may be updated.

Alternative Routes to GMC Registration

PLAB / UKMLA is not the only route to GMC registration for IMGs. The GMC also offers specialist registration pathways for doctors who already hold a recognised post-graduate specialty qualification. Doctors with MRCP(UK), MRCS, MRCGP, or equivalent qualifications from approved international bodies may be eligible for specialist or GP registration without taking PLAB. These routes are assessed by the GMC on a case-by-case basis and may require a Certificate of Eligibility for Specialist Registration (CESR) application. For most IMGs who are earlier in their careers and do not yet hold a recognised specialty qualification, the PLAB route remains the most straightforward pathway to initial GMC registration.

Common Misconceptions About UKMLA vs PLAB

Several widespread misconceptions among candidates deserve direct clarification:

  • Misconception: PLAB is easier than UKMLA. False. Both are assessed against the identical MLA Content Map at the identical Foundation Year 1 standard. Observed pass rate differences reflect preparation context, not assessment difficulty.
  • Misconception: You can choose to sit the UKMLA instead of PLAB as an IMG. In practice, IMGs sit the PLAB route, which from August 2024 onward meets the UKMLA standard. There is no separate UKMLA booking system open to IMGs distinct from PLAB booking.
  • Misconception: PLAB results obtained before 2024 are no longer valid. False. Pre-August 2024 PLAB results retain their five-year validity and are recognised within the MLA framework.
  • Misconception: The UKMLA is a new exam entirely separate from PLAB. False. The UKMLA is a framework and standard; PLAB is the delivery mechanism for IMGs within that framework. They are not two separate competing examinations.
  • Misconception: UK medical students are exempt from the UKMLA. False. UK medical students must pass the UKMLA as a graduation requirement — they are not exempt. The difference is that their medical school coordinates and funds the assessment rather than students booking and paying individually.

Frequently Asked Questions About UKMLA vs PLAB

If I pass PLAB 1 and PLAB 2 in 2026, have I met the UKMLA requirement?

Yes. From August 2024 onwards, passing PLAB 1 meets the MLA AKT requirement and passing PLAB 2 meets the MLA CPSA requirement. These together satisfy the UKMLA's two-component licensing requirement for IMGs. You do not need to sit any additional exam labelled UKMLA — PLAB 1 and PLAB 2 in their current aligned forms are the UKMLA for IMGs. Your GMC registration application will confirm that you have met the MLA requirements through the PLAB route.

Can I switch from the PLAB route to the UKMLA route mid-journey?

In practice, there is no meaningful route-switching decision for IMGs, because the PLAB route is the UKMLA for IMGs. IMGs do not have access to the UK medical school UKMLA delivery system (coordinated by the Medical Schools Council for enrolled students). The only pathway available to IMGs is PLAB 1 (AKT) and PLAB 2 (CPSA) through the GMC booking system, which from August 2024 meets the MLA standard. There is no separate UKMLA for IMGs to switch to.

Will the GMC eventually drop the PLAB name entirely and call everything UKMLA?

This is possible in the long term, but there has been no formal announcement from the GMC about a timeline for retiring the PLAB name. As of 2026, the PLAB branding remains active in all GMC booking portals, communications, and official documents. The GMC has stated that PLAB meets the MLA requirements — the two exist in parallel terminology rather than one replacing the other at a branding level. Candidates should monitor GMC communications for any future changes to naming or structure.

Does the UKMLA / PLAB change affect doctors already registered with the GMC?

No. Doctors who are already fully registered with the GMC are not required to pass the UKMLA retrospectively. The UKMLA applies only to those seeking initial GMC registration. Existing registrants maintain their registration through the annual retention fee and continuing professional development requirements, not through re-sitting the licensing assessment. There is no grandfathering cliff or retrospective requirement for established GMC registrants.

What English language level do I need for PLAB?

IMGs sitting PLAB must demonstrate English language proficiency through either IELTS Academic (overall score of 7.5 with no individual component below 7.0 in Listening, Reading, Writing, and Speaking) or OET (Occupational English Test with a minimum Grade B in all four sub-tests). IELTS and OET results are valid for two years from the test date. English language proficiency must be demonstrated before a GMC Online account can be activated and a PLAB booking can proceed. UK medical students are not required to separately demonstrate English language proficiency as this is embedded in their degree programme.

Conclusion

The UKMLA vs PLAB question resolves, ultimately, to this: they are the same standard, delivered through different systems for different candidate groups. For UK medical students, the UKMLA is embedded in the final year of a GMC-approved degree, funded by the medical school, and coordinated by the institution. For IMGs, the same standard is accessed through the PLAB route — a self-funded, self-organised process that involves booking and sitting PLAB 1 and PLAB 2 through GMC Online. The content, the pass mark methodology, and the licensing outcome are identical across both routes.

This unification is the most significant development in UK medical licensing in a generation. It creates genuine equity of standard at the point of entry to the UK medical workforce, giving NHS employers, patients, and the wider public confidence that every newly registered doctor — wherever they trained — has demonstrated the same core level of safe practice. For IMGs navigating this pathway, the message is clear: prepare rigorously against the MLA Content Map, understand UK clinical guidelines, and invest in the OSCE preparation that the CPSA demands. The same standard that UK graduates meet through five years of structured training is achievable through disciplined, well-targeted self-directed preparation.

Explore the full UKMLA vs PLAB comparison guide →

post_title: UKMLA Eligibility: Who Can Sit the Exam?
post_excerpt: Not everyone sits the UKMLA through the same route. This guide clarifies eligibility criteria for UK medical students and international medical graduates.
seo_title: UKMLA Eligibility: Who Can Sit the Exam and How
seo_description: Understand UKMLA eligibility for UK students and IMGs. Find out which route applies to you, what pre-requisites you need, and how to register.
html_content:
UKMLA eligibility determines who can sit the UK Medical Licensing Assessment, and understanding the rules fully is the essential first step whether you are a UK medical student nearing graduation or an international medical graduate (IMG) planning to practise in the United Kingdom. Getting eligibility wrong wastes time and money, so this guide covers every requirement in detail — from accepted qualifications and English language scores to the verification process, special cases, and how to check whether your specific situation qualifies.

The UKMLA consists of two components: the Applied Knowledge Test (AKT), a written single-best-answer examination, and the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA), an OSCE-style practical exam. Both must be passed to obtain a licence to practise. The eligibility rules differ slightly depending on your route — UK medical school student or IMG — so both pathways are addressed separately below.

Since 2024, the GMC has been transitioning from the legacy PLAB route to the unified UKMLA for all candidates. IMGs who started the PLAB process before January 2025 may complete their existing attempts, but new IMG applicants registering from 2025 onwards enter the UKMLA route by default. If you are unsure which route applies to you, contact the GMC directly via your GMC Online account.

UK Medical Students: UKMLA Eligibility Requirements

If you are enrolled at a UK medical school recognised by the GMC, your eligibility for the UKMLA is primarily managed by your medical school rather than directly by the GMC. Your institution is responsible for entering you into the AKT and CPSA at the appropriate points in your programme, typically in your penultimate and final clinical years.

To be eligible as a UK medical student, you must be enrolled in a GMC-approved undergraduate or graduate-entry medical degree programme. You do not need to apply directly to the GMC to sit the UKMLA — your university coordinates entries on your behalf. However, you must maintain satisfactory academic progress within your programme and comply with your medical school's fitness to practise requirements.

The AKT is normally sat during Year 4 or Year 5 (or equivalent for graduate-entry programmes), while the CPSA is typically sat during the final year. Your medical school will communicate exact internal deadlines and sitting windows. If you have concerns about timing, speak with your medical school's student support or undergraduate office well in advance.

Students with reasonable adjustments or disabilities must apply through their medical school's disability services team, which will liaise with the GMC to ensure appropriate accommodations are in place. Do not leave reasonable adjustment requests to the last minute — applications should ideally be submitted at least three months before your expected sitting date.

International Medical Graduates: UKMLA Eligibility Overview

For IMGs, UKMLA eligibility requires meeting four distinct criteria: holding an acceptable primary medical qualification (PMQ), having that qualification independently verified, demonstrating English language proficiency to the GMC standard, and making declarations about good health and good character. All four criteria must be met before you can book the AKT.

IMGs apply directly to the GMC via GMC Online. Unlike UK medical students, IMGs manage the entire process themselves, including verification, English language testing, and exam booking. This means planning and timeline management fall entirely on your shoulders — which is why building a realistic timeline is critical (see the section on timing below).

Once you have passed both the AKT and CPSA, you will then need to complete a period of satisfactory supervised practice (provisionally registered) before full GMC registration is granted. The UKMLA pass alone does not grant you the right to work in the UK; it is a prerequisite for registration.

Accepted Primary Medical Qualifications

Your medical degree must come from a medical school listed on the World Directory of Medical Schools (WDOMS) and assessed as acceptable by the GMC. The GMC does not publish a simple country-by-country approved list, but in practice degrees from the following regions are routinely accepted when the school appears on the WDOMS and meets GMC criteria:

  • South Asia: India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Nepal
  • Middle East and North Africa: Egypt, Sudan, Saudi Arabia, UAE, Jordan, Lebanon
  • Sub-Saharan Africa: Nigeria, Ghana, Kenya, South Africa, Zimbabwe
  • Southeast Asia: Philippines, Malaysia
  • Europe: EEA graduates may qualify via mutual recognition depending on when they qualified
  • The Americas: Graduates of Caribbean, US, and Latin American schools are assessed individually
  • Australia and New Zealand: Routinely accepted

Degrees from schools not on the WDOMS, or schools flagged by the GMC as not meeting baseline standards, will not be accepted. If you completed your degree at a school that has since been removed from the WDOMS, contact the GMC for individual assessment — outcomes vary on a case-by-case basis.

RegionTypically Accepted?Notes
India (MCI/NMC-approved schools)YesMust appear on WDOMS; MBBS degree
Pakistan (PMDC-approved schools)YesMust appear on WDOMS
Nigeria (MDCN-approved schools)YesMust appear on WDOMS
Egypt (Egyptian medical schools)YesMust appear on WDOMS
EEA countries (EU/EEA graduates)ConditionalPost-Brexit rules apply; see special cases section
Caribbean medical schoolsCase by caseNot all schools are accepted; check WDOMS status
Schools not on WDOMSNoDegree will not be accepted

The MyIntealth Verification Process: Step by Step

Before 2025, qualification verification was handled through EPIC (Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials). EPIC has now been retired and replaced by MyIntealth, a new platform developed by Intealth — the parent organisation of ECFMG. All new IMG applicants must use MyIntealth; EPIC portfolios that were already established may still be referenced for GMC purposes, but new credentials must go through MyIntealth.

The MyIntealth verification process involves primary-source verification, meaning Intealth contacts your medical school directly to confirm your qualification — you cannot self-certify. Here is the step-by-step process:

  1. Create a MyIntealth account at the MyIntealth website. You will need a valid email address and a government-issued ID.
  2. Enter your personal and educational details, including your full name as it appears on your degree certificate, your date of birth, and details of your medical school and graduation year.
  3. Upload your documents, including a certified copy of your medical degree certificate, your transcript, and your postgraduate qualifications if relevant.
  4. Select the GMC as a receiving organisation so that verification results are sent directly to the GMC on your behalf.
  5. Pay the verification fee. Fees vary depending on the number of credentials being verified.
  6. Wait for primary-source verification. Intealth contacts your medical school directly. This can take 4 to 12 weeks for most applicants, though delays of 3 to 6 months can occur if your medical school is slow to respond.
  7. Monitor your MyIntealth dashboard under "My Cases" to track progress. Intealth sends email notifications as each credential is verified.
  8. GMC receives results automatically. Once verification is complete, the GMC is notified and you can proceed to the next steps of your application.

Common issues that cause delays include name discrepancies between your degree and your passport, medical schools that have merged or changed names since you graduated, and lost or damaged original records. If you anticipate any of these issues, contact MyIntealth support early and explain your situation — they handle such cases regularly.

English Language Requirements for UKMLA Eligibility

English language proficiency is a non-negotiable component of UKMLA eligibility for IMGs. The GMC accepts two tests: IELTS Academic and OET (Occupational English Test) Medicine. Both must have been taken within two years of your GMC application date to be valid.

IELTS Academic Requirements

For GMC registration purposes, you must achieve an overall IELTS Academic score of 7.5 with no individual component below 7.0. This means you need at least 7.0 in each of Listening, Reading, Writing, and Speaking. You cannot average out a low component with a high one — all four must meet the minimum.

OET Medicine Requirements

For OET, you must achieve Grade B (equivalent to a score of 350 or above) in all four sub-tests: Listening, Reading, Writing, and Speaking. As with IELTS, all components must be passed — a Grade B in three components but a Grade C in one is not sufficient.

TestOverall Score RequiredMinimum Per ComponentValidityBest For
IELTS Academic7.57.0 in all four skills2 yearsCandidates with strong general English
OET MedicineGrade B (350+)Grade B in all four skills2 yearsClinicians comfortable with medical scenarios

In practice, most IMG candidates find OET more accessible because all prompts and content are medical in nature. The Writing sub-test requires writing a referral letter, which doctors practise regularly, while the Speaking sub-test uses medical consultation roleplay. IELTS Writing and Speaking use general topics that can feel less intuitive for clinically focused candidates.

For the UK Foundation Programme (UKFP), the English language requirements are higher: IELTS 7.5 in every skill (not just an overall 7.5 with a minimum of 7.0), or OET score of 400 or above in every sub-test. If you are aiming for foundation posts, plan your English testing accordingly.

There are exemptions from English language testing for candidates who qualified from a medical school in a country where English is the primary language of instruction, or who have practised as a doctor in a majority English-speaking country for a sustained period. The GMC assesses exemption applications individually; contact the GMC before assuming you qualify for an exemption.

Exemptions and Alternative Routes to UKMLA

Not all candidates need to sit both components of the UKMLA. Certain exemptions and alternative routes exist, though they are narrow in scope.

EEA Graduates Post-Brexit

Before Brexit, EEA-qualified doctors could register with the GMC via mutual recognition of their European medical qualifications without sitting a licensing exam. This changed on 1 January 2021. EEA doctors who obtained GMC registration before 31 December 2020 were not affected. However, EEA doctors who did not register before that date must now go through the full UKMLA process as IMGs, including qualification verification and English language testing (if applicable).

Specialist Doctors with Postgraduate Qualifications

Certain overseas-qualified specialists can apply for specialist or GP registration via the Certificate of Eligibility for Specialist Registration (CESR) or Certificate of Eligibility for General Practice Registration (CEGPR) routes. These routes are separate from the UKMLA and assess equivalence to a UK CCT (Certificate of Completion of Training). CESR/CEGPR applicants do not sit the UKMLA; they instead submit a portfolio of evidence. This route is complex and requires substantial documentation of specialist training and experience.

Doctors Returning to Practice

Doctors who were previously registered with the GMC but allowed their registration to lapse may be able to restore their registration without sitting the full UKMLA, depending on how long they have been out of practice and their circumstances. The GMC assesses these applications individually. If you have been out of clinical practice for five or more years, you will likely need to demonstrate currency through a returner's programme.

Special Cases and Conditional Eligibility

Some candidates fall into edge cases that the standard eligibility framework does not clearly address. Here is a guide to the most common special situations:

  • Dual-qualified doctors: If you hold qualifications from two countries, the GMC will assess the qualification that you are primarily using to support your application. In most cases, using the higher-recognised qualification is advisable.
  • Doctors with a history of fitness to practise proceedings: Any previous sanctions, conditions, or erasure from a medical register in any country must be disclosed. The GMC will assess these cases individually; disclosure does not automatically bar registration but failure to disclose will.
  • Medical graduates who have not yet practised clinically: Some IMGs complete their degree and then move into research or other fields for several years. The GMC may request evidence of how you have maintained clinical currency. While there is no formal expiry on a medical qualification, very long gaps between graduation and application may prompt additional questions.
  • Foundation Year 1 (FY1) and Internship equivalence: Some countries require a post-graduate internship year before full licensure. The GMC considers the internship year as part of the overall qualification — you do not need to have completed a UK FY1 before applying to sit the UKMLA, but you may need it before commencing clinical practice in the UK depending on the role.
  • International Foundation Programme (IFP): The IFP is a route designed specifically for IMGs to complete a foundation-equivalent year in the UK. Eligibility for the IFP requires GMC provisional registration, which in turn requires passing the AKT and CPSA. Check our full eligibility guide for IFP-specific requirements.

Eligibility Checklist for IMGs

RequirementDetailStatus to Confirm
Primary Medical QualificationDegree from WDOMS-listed school accepted by GMCCheck WDOMS and GMC guidance
MyIntealth VerificationPrimary-source verification completed via MyIntealthAccount created and case submitted
English Language TestIELTS 7.5 overall / 7.0 per component OR OET Grade B all skillsValid result within 2 years
GMC Online AccountAccount created and identity documents uploadedAccount active
Good Character DeclarationNo undisclosed fitness to practise issuesDeclarations completed honestly
Good Health DeclarationNo undisclosed health conditions affecting safe practiceDeclarations completed
Application Fee PaidGMC application fee submittedPayment confirmed

How to Check If Your Qualification Is Accepted

The fastest way to check whether your medical school is recognised is to search the World Directory of Medical Schools (WDOMS) at wdoms.org. Enter your school's name, country, and the year you graduated. If your school appears in the directory and was listed during the years of your study, that is a strong indicator your qualification will be accepted.

However, listing on the WDOMS is necessary but not always sufficient — the GMC carries out its own assessment of qualifications from certain countries and may apply additional scrutiny. For countries where the GMC has specific concerns about standards, they may require additional documentation or refuse certain qualifications even if the school is on the WDOMS.

If you are uncertain, the best approach is to contact the GMC directly via their online enquiry form. Provide the name of your medical school, your country, and your graduation year. The GMC can give you a preliminary indication of whether your qualification is likely to be accepted, though formal assessment only happens once you submit a full application.

Frequently Asked Questions About UKMLA Eligibility

Can EU or EEA graduates still apply for GMC registration without sitting the UKMLA?

No — since 1 January 2021, post-Brexit rules mean that EEA-qualified doctors who did not register with the GMC before 31 December 2020 must go through the full UKMLA process, including qualification verification and English language testing. The mutual recognition route that previously allowed EEA graduates to bypass a licensing exam is no longer available for new applicants. If you graduated in an EEA country and hold a valid EEA medical licence, you will still need to verify your qualification through MyIntealth and meet the English language requirements unless you qualify for an exemption.

Is there an age limit for sitting the UKMLA?

There is no upper or lower age limit for sitting the UKMLA or for GMC registration. Doctors of any age can apply, provided they meet all eligibility criteria. Practically speaking, you must have completed your medical degree, which typically means candidates are at least in their mid-twenties. The GMC does assess health and good character, and extremely mature applicants who have been out of practice for many years may be asked for additional evidence of clinical currency, but age itself is not a disqualifying factor.

What happens if my medical qualification is not accepted?

If the GMC does not accept your primary medical qualification, you cannot register via the UKMLA route. Your options in that situation are limited. You could pursue further medical training to obtain a recognised qualification — for example, completing an additional year of study or a recognised conversion programme. Alternatively, you might explore whether a postgraduate specialist qualification you hold could support a CESR application, though this requires substantial evidence of specialist competence. The GMC will explain the reasons for rejection in writing, and you can appeal the decision or seek legal advice if you believe the rejection was incorrect.

Do I need to complete an English language test if I trained in a country where English is widely spoken?

Exemption from English language testing is granted by the GMC on a case-by-case basis. Qualifying countries include those where English is an official language and the primary medium of medical education — such as Australia, New Zealand, Canada, the USA, and some Caribbean nations. Having attended an English-medium school in a non-English-speaking country, or having practised in English in a clinical setting, does not automatically qualify you for an exemption. You must apply for the exemption via the GMC and provide supporting evidence. If in doubt, take the test rather than applying for an exemption — it avoids delays if the exemption is not granted.

Can I sit PLAB 1 or PLAB 2 while my MyIntealth verification is still in progress?

No. The GMC will not process your application or allow you to book any UKMLA or PLAB examination until your primary medical qualification has been verified through MyIntealth and the results have been received by the GMC. This is why starting the verification process as early as possible is so important — delays in verification directly delay your ability to book an exam sitting. Some candidates start the MyIntealth process 12 to 18 months before they intend to sit the exam to build in a buffer for unexpected delays.

What is the difference between provisional and full GMC registration?

Passing the UKMLA (both AKT and CPSA) grants you eligibility for provisional GMC registration, not full registration. With provisional registration, you can work in approved Foundation Year 1 posts or equivalent supervised posts in the UK. Full registration is granted after you satisfactorily complete your provisional registration period — typically a one-year Foundation Programme. Full registration is required before you can work unsupervised as a doctor in the UK. IMGs entering via the International Foundation Programme (IFP) follow a similar pathway.

What does good character mean in the context of UKMLA eligibility?

Good character for GMC registration purposes means that you do not have any undisclosed criminal convictions, cautions, or regulatory proceedings that would call into question your suitability to practise medicine. You must disclose all cautions and convictions, including spent convictions, when applying to the GMC — this is different from standard employment disclosure rules. You must also disclose any previous fitness to practise proceedings from any medical regulatory body in any country, any periods of suspension or erasure from a medical register, and any refusal of medical registration in another country. Non-disclosure is treated very seriously and can result in your application being refused or your registration being revoked if the non-disclosure is discovered later.

Conclusion

Understanding UKMLA eligibility in full is the essential foundation of your UK medical licensing journey. Whether you are a UK medical student whose school manages the process or an IMG navigating qualification verification, English language testing, and GMC applications independently, knowing exactly what is required — and in what order — saves time and prevents costly mistakes.

The key takeaways are: start your MyIntealth verification as early as possible, choose between IELTS and OET based on your strengths, confirm your qualification is on the WDOMS, and be completely transparent in all declarations you make to the GMC. For a full overview of the exam structure once you are eligible, see our guides on the AKT exam format and CPSA exam format. If you want to understand where the UKMLA fits in the broader landscape of UK medical licensing, visit our What is the UKMLA page.
