post_title: UKMLA Key Dates and Sitting Schedule: What to Know
post_excerpt: Planning your UKMLA preparation begins with knowing the key dates. Here is how the sitting schedule works for UK students and IMGs.
seo_title: UKMLA Key Dates and Sitting Schedule 2025–2026
seo_description: Plan your UKMLA preparation around key dates and the sitting schedule. We explain how UK students and IMGs book their AKT and CPSA sittings.
html_content:
Knowing the UKMLA key dates is not optional — it is the backbone of effective planning for both UK medical students and international medical graduates who want to pass the exam on their first attempt and secure a clinical post in the UK without delays. Miss a registration deadline by even a few hours and you could be pushed back by three to six months. This guide lays out every important date, window, and deadline in detail, including the 2025/2026 sitting schedule, how dates differ for UK students versus IMGs, and how to plan backwards from your target start date.

The UKMLA is administered differently for its two main candidate groups. UK medical students have their AKT and CPSA dates set by their medical school as part of the programme, while IMGs apply directly to the GMC and must book their own exam sittings. Understanding which set of dates applies to you is the first step in building your plan.

UKMLA Key Dates for UK Medical Students

For students at UK medical schools, the AKT and CPSA are embedded within the undergraduate curriculum. Your medical school coordinates examination entries and communicates sitting dates internally. While the GMC sets the overall examination windows, your school determines exactly when within that window you sit.

Typically, the AKT is offered in Year 4 or Year 5 of a five-year programme (or Year 3 or Year 4 of a four-year graduate-entry programme). The CPSA is usually sat in the final year, often in the spring term to allow results to feed into graduation and Foundation Programme application processes. However, the exact timing varies by institution, so you must always confirm dates with your own medical school.

The relationship between UKMLA results and FPAS (Foundation Programme Application System) deadlines is critical. The FPAS ranking process takes into account your Educational Performance Measure (EPM), and a strong AKT score can enhance your application. FPAS applications typically open in the autumn of your final year, with allocation outcomes in early spring. If your AKT is scheduled for autumn of your final year and you perform poorly, there may be limited time to resit before FPAS applications close. Check with your school whether an early AKT sitting (in the penultimate year) is available as a buffer.

Typical UK Medical Student Timeline

  • Year 4 / Penultimate year: First AKT sitting offered by most medical schools
  • Year 5 / Final year, autumn: Second AKT sitting (resit if needed, or first for some programmes)
  • Year 5 / Final year, spring: CPSA sitting
  • FPAS application: Typically opens October/November of final year
  • FPAS ranking: Released February/March
  • Foundation post start: August (F1 year begins)

UKMLA Key Dates for IMGs: The PLAB and AKT Route

For IMGs, the most important UKMLA key dates are the PLAB 1 (now transitioning to AKT) sitting dates published by the GMC. As of 2025-2026, the GMC runs AKT/PLAB 1 examinations four times per year: in February, May, August, and November. These sittings happen both in the UK and at selected international test centres.

The 2026 confirmed sitting dates include February 12 and May 21, with August and November sittings expected to follow the same quarterly pattern. The GMC announces dates for the following year towards the end of the current year, so checking your GMC Online account regularly from September/October onwards is advisable if you are planning to sit in the first half of the following year.

2025/2026 Sitting Windows (PLAB 1 / AKT for IMGs)

Sitting WindowApproximate Exam MonthRegistration OpeningRegistration Closing
Window 1 (2026)February 2026October/November 20256-8 weeks before exam
Window 2 (2026)May 2026February/March 20266-8 weeks before exam
Window 3 (2026)August 2026May/June 20266-8 weeks before exam
Window 4 (2026)November 2026August/September 20266-8 weeks before exam

Registration typically closes six to eight weeks before the exam date. For high-demand centres — particularly in India, Nigeria, Pakistan, and the UAE — seats fill within minutes of booking opening. This is not an exaggeration: candidates regularly report refreshing the booking page the moment registration opens and still finding slots gone within ten minutes. Set calendar reminders for the booking opening date and be at your computer ready to book immediately.

How to Book PLAB 1 Internationally: Step by Step

Booking PLAB 1 (or the UKMLA AKT for IMGs) requires a GMC Online account and a completed eligibility check. Here is the process in sequence:

  1. Confirm your eligibility: Your MyIntealth verification must be complete and results received by the GMC before you can book.
  2. Log into GMC Online: Go to the GMC Online portal and log in with your credentials.
  3. Navigate to the exam booking section: Find the PLAB or UKMLA AKT booking section in your GMC Online dashboard.
  4. Select your preferred sitting: Choose the sitting date and test centre that works for you. If your preferred centre is full, check other centres in your country or neighbouring countries.
  5. Complete the booking and pay the fee: The exam fee must be paid at the time of booking. For 2025/2026, PLAB 1 costs £255. Keep your booking confirmation email as your proof of registration.
  6. Wait for your admission ticket: The GMC sends your formal admission letter (also called the exam admission ticket) closer to the exam date, usually two to four weeks before sitting.
  7. Plan travel and logistics: If you are sitting outside your home country, book travel and accommodation after confirming your exam slot — not before.

PLAB 2 Booking Dates After PLAB 1 Pass

Once you pass PLAB 1, you must sit PLAB 2 within two years. PLAB 2 is held exclusively in Manchester, UK, so all international candidates must travel to the UK for this component. PLAB 2 sittings run throughout the year, and the GMC typically releases blocks of dates several months in advance.

The waiting time for a PLAB 2 slot has historically been significant — often four to six months from the date of booking due to demand. Candidates are therefore advised to book PLAB 2 as soon as possible after passing PLAB 1, ideally within the first month of receiving your result. Do not wait until you feel fully prepared before booking; book a slot and prepare in parallel, as slots can become scarce.

If your PLAB 1 pass is approaching its two-year expiry and you have not yet sat PLAB 2, contact the GMC immediately. There is limited provision for extensions in exceptional circumstances, but the two-year window is strictly enforced in most cases. Missing it means resitting PLAB 1 from scratch.

AKT Validity Window and Its Implications

A critical planning factor for all candidates is the validity window of the AKT. Once you pass the AKT, your result is valid for three years (36 months). You must pass the CPSA within this three-year window. If you do not sit or pass the CPSA before the AKT validity expires, your AKT pass is voided and you must resit the AKT before attempting the CPSA again.

This three-year window gives most candidates ample time, but it can become a pressure point if you face multiple CPSA resits, career changes, health issues, or periods abroad. Plan your CPSA attempts proactively within the first 18 to 24 months after passing the AKT, leaving room for at least one resit without running into the validity boundary.

For IMGs, the equivalent PLAB validity window is: PLAB 2 must be sat within two years of passing PLAB 1. The shift to UKMLA nomenclature (AKT/CPSA) brings these validity windows in line for UK students and IMGs on the unified pathway.

What Happens If You Miss the Booking Window

Missing a booking window means waiting for the next sitting — typically three months away. This is not just a minor inconvenience; for IMGs, it can affect visa timing, employment offers, and the validity of other documents (such as an English language test result that is approaching its two-year expiry date). For UK medical students, a missed sitting could delay graduation or push you into a late FPAS application cycle, potentially affecting your foundation post allocation.

There is no mechanism to add yourself to a waiting list for a full sitting, though the GMC occasionally releases additional seats closer to the exam date due to cancellations. Checking the booking portal regularly in the weeks before an exam is worth doing if you missed the initial booking window.

If you miss a window due to a genuine emergency or medical issue, submit a mitigating circumstances application to the GMC (for IMGs) or your medical school (for UK students) as soon as possible. The earlier you submit, the more options remain available to you.

Result Release Dates

PLAB 1 / AKT results are typically released within six weeks of the sitting date. The GMC notifies candidates by email when results are available via GMC Online. Results are not released on a fixed calendar date per sitting — the turnaround can vary between four and eight weeks depending on the sitting and any marking review processes.

PLAB 2 / CPSA results take longer due to the complexity of marking OSCE assessments. Expect to wait eight to twelve weeks after your sitting date for PLAB 2 results. As with PLAB 1, results are released via GMC Online and accompanied by a detailed feedback report if you fail (which is invaluable for resit preparation).

FPAS Application Dates and Their Relationship to UKMLA

For UK medical students, the Foundation Programme Application System (FPAS) timetable runs in parallel with UKMLA examination windows. Here is how the two interact:

  • AKT results feed into the Educational Performance Measure (EPM), which is part of the FPAS application score.
  • FPAS applications typically open in October/November of the final year of medical school.
  • Shortlisting and preferences are submitted in December/January.
  • Allocation outcomes are released in March.
  • If your AKT result is not yet available when FPAS applications open, your EPM will be calculated from available data and may be updated when the result is received — check with your school and FPAS about how this is handled in your cohort year.

International Test Centre Dates and Availability

PLAB 1 is available at international centres in multiple countries, though availability and the number of seats per centre varies considerably. Countries with international PLAB 1 centres include India (multiple cities), Nigeria (Lagos and Abuja), Pakistan (Islamabad and Karachi), Egypt (historically Alexandria, though this is under review), Australia, Canada, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka.

The GMC has announced reductions in international centre availability due to declining demand — centres in Dhaka, Alexandria, Accra, and Chennai are being closed from 2027 onwards. If you were planning to sit in one of these locations, check the latest GMC guidance and adjust your plans accordingly.

Candidates in countries without an international centre must travel to sit. Some candidates from Africa or Central Asia travel to the UK specifically to sit PLAB 1, combining the exam trip with a familiarisation visit if they plan to work in the UK. Factor travel costs and visa requirements into your overall UKMLA planning budget and timeline.

Planning Backwards From Your Target Start Date

The most reliable way to plan your UKMLA journey is to work backwards from the date you want to start working as a doctor in the UK. Here is a sample backwards-planning timeline for an IMG aiming to start a clinical post in August 2027:

MilestoneTarget DateNotes
Start clinical post in UKAugust 2027Target date
GMC full registration confirmedJune 2027Allow 6-8 weeks after satisfactory provisional period
CPSA / PLAB 2 passBy December 2026Allow time for registration processing
Book CPSA / PLAB 2Immediately after AKT pass4-6 month wait for slot is common
AKT / PLAB 1 passBy May 2026First attempt target
Sit AKT / PLAB 1February or May 2026Book 6-8 weeks in advance
English language test valid resultBy November 2025Must be within 2 years of GMC application
MyIntealth verification submittedBy June 2025Allow 4-12 weeks for verification
Create GMC Online accountMay 2025Starting point for all applications

Key Deadlines Checklist

  • MyIntealth verification: Start at least 6 months before planned exam sitting
  • English language test: Complete at least 3 months before GMC application, ensuring result will remain valid throughout your UKMLA journey
  • PLAB 1 / AKT booking: Book on the day registration opens for high-demand centres
  • PLAB 2 / CPSA booking: Book immediately after receiving your AKT pass result
  • PLAB 2 validity: Must be sat within 2 years of PLAB 1 pass
  • AKT validity: CPSA must be passed within 3 years of AKT pass
  • Resit window: Typically wait for the next sitting (3 months) — check GMC guidance for minimum waiting periods

Frequently Asked Questions About UKMLA Key Dates

How far in advance should I start planning my UKMLA journey as an IMG?

You should start planning at least 18 to 24 months before you want to pass the final component of the UKMLA. This allows time for MyIntealth verification (up to 6 months in some cases), English language testing and potential retakes, exam booking and preparation, and one resit if needed without pressure. Candidates who start 12 months before their target date often find themselves rushed, especially if verification or English language testing takes longer than expected.

Can I sit PLAB 1 outside the UK?

Yes. PLAB 1 is available at international test centres in multiple countries including India, Nigeria, Pakistan, Egypt (subject to centre availability), Australia, Canada, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka. International sittings run on the same quarterly schedule as UK sittings. Note that not all countries have the same number of available seats per sitting — popular centres fill very quickly. Check the GMC website and your GMC Online account for the current list of active international centres, as some locations are being phased out from 2027.

When are PLAB 1 results released?

PLAB 1 results are typically released within four to six weeks of the sitting date, though the GMC does not publish an exact results release date in advance. You will receive an email notification when your result is available to view in GMC Online. Do not make major travel or accommodation bookings contingent on receiving a result by a specific date — build in flexibility for potential delays in the results release.

What happens if I miss the two-year window to sit PLAB 2 after passing PLAB 1?

If you do not sit PLAB 2 within two years of your PLAB 1 pass date, your PLAB 1 result expires. You will need to resit PLAB 1 before being eligible to attempt PLAB 2. This two-year window is enforced strictly, and extensions are only granted in exceptional circumstances (such as serious illness) supported by appropriate evidence. If you are approaching the expiry date and have not yet sat PLAB 2, contact the GMC immediately to discuss your options. Booking PLAB 2 as early as possible after your PLAB 1 pass — even before you feel fully prepared — is strongly advisable given the limited availability of PLAB 2 slots.

Do resit attempts affect my FPAS application as a UK medical student?

Your AKT result contributes to your FPAS Educational Performance Measure (EPM). If you resit the AKT, it is your most recent result that is used for EPM calculation in most circumstances — check the current FPAS guidance for the exact rules in your application year, as these can change. A lower AKT score from a resit could affect your EPM and therefore your foundation programme allocation. However, passing is always more important than maximising the score on the first attempt. Discuss the implications with your medical school's careers team before your FPAS application window opens.

Conclusion

The UKMLA key dates framework is one of the most important practical aspects of medical licensing in the UK. Whether you are a UK medical student managing your AKT and FPAS timing or an IMG navigating international test centres, verification timelines, and PLAB 2 booking queues, the candidates who succeed are almost always the ones who plan the furthest in advance. Use the backwards-planning table in this guide to map your personal timeline, set calendar reminders for every booking window, and build in buffer time wherever possible.

For full details on the exam content and what to study, visit our preparation guide. To understand the full cost of sitting the UKMLA, including exam fees and verification costs, see our fees guide. For information on what happens after you pass, including registration timelines, see our results and scoring page.

post_title: How to Register for PLAB 1: A Step-by-Step Guide for IMGs
post_excerpt: Booking your PLAB 1 sitting involves several steps before you reach the checkout. This guide walks IMGs through the registration process from start to finish.
seo_title: How to Register for PLAB 1: Step-by-Step for IMGs
seo_description: Ready to book PLAB 1? This step-by-step guide walks international medical graduates through the PLAB 1 registration process, documents needed, and key tips.
html_content:
Understanding how to register for PLAB 1 is the first practical hurdle every international medical graduate faces on the road to GMC registration in the UK. The process involves multiple stages across different platforms — from credential verification through MyIntealth to booking your sitting via GMC Online — and each stage has its own timeline and potential for delay. This comprehensive guide walks you through every step in sequence, with practical tips for avoiding the most common mistakes that cause IMGs to lose months to administrative errors.

PLAB 1 is a three-hour written examination comprising 180 single-best-answer (SBA) questions. It assesses clinical knowledge across the breadth of UK medical practice and forms Part 1 of the licensing assessment for IMGs. Passing PLAB 1 allows you to proceed to PLAB 2 (the OSCE), and passing both allows you to apply for GMC provisional registration. As of 2025, PLAB is transitioning to become the IMG pathway of the UKMLA — the same Applied Knowledge Test (AKT) that UK medical students sit — so the content and process will align further over the coming years.

Step 1: Confirm Your PLAB 1 Eligibility

Before you begin any application process, confirm that you meet the eligibility criteria to register for PLAB 1. The core requirements are: holding an acceptable primary medical qualification (PMQ), having English language proficiency at the GMC standard, and not having exhausted your PLAB 1 attempt limit.

Your PMQ must come from a medical school listed on the World Directory of Medical Schools (WDOMS) and assessed as acceptable by the GMC. This covers the majority of recognised medical schools worldwide, but not all — check wdoms.org to confirm your school is listed. If your school is listed, that is a necessary but not always sufficient condition; the GMC assesses qualifications from certain countries with additional scrutiny.

If you have already attempted PLAB 1 four times, you are at the standard attempt limit. A fifth attempt is possible if you have completed additional clinical training approved by the GMC. If you are in this position, you must contact the GMC directly before attempting to book a further sitting.

Review the full eligibility requirements for a detailed breakdown of accepted qualifications and English language requirements before proceeding.

Step 2: Complete MyIntealth Verification

The credential verification step is the longest and most unpredictable part of the registration process. Until 2024, this was handled through EPIC (Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials). EPIC has now been replaced by MyIntealth, the new platform developed by Intealth (the parent organisation of ECFMG). All new applicants must use MyIntealth.

How Long Does MyIntealth Verification Take?

Verification timelines vary significantly. For candidates whose medical schools respond quickly, verification can be completed in four to six weeks. For candidates whose schools are slow, have merged, have changed names, or have incomplete records, the process can take three to six months or longer. Build the worst-case scenario into your planning — assume six months and treat anything faster as a bonus.

Step-by-Step MyIntealth Process

  1. Visit the MyIntealth website and create an account using your email address and a government-issued ID.
  2. Enter your personal details exactly as they appear on your medical degree certificate — name, date of birth, and nationality.
  3. Enter your medical school's details and graduation year. If your school has merged or changed its name, use the name under which you graduated.
  4. Upload certified copies of your degree certificate, your academic transcript, and any postgraduate qualifications you want verified.
  5. Select the General Medical Council (GMC) as a receiving organisation so results are sent directly to the GMC on your behalf.
  6. Pay the verification fee. Fees depend on the number and type of credentials being verified.
  7. Monitor the "My Cases" section of your MyIntealth dashboard. You will receive email notifications as each credential is verified.
  8. Once verification is complete, the GMC receives the results automatically. No action is required from you at this stage beyond confirming receipt in your GMC Online account.

Common Verification Issues by Country

  • India: Name discrepancies between degree and passport are common. Ensure all documents use the exact same name spelling.
  • Nigeria: Delays often occur when MDCN (Medical and Dental Council of Nigeria) records need to be cross-referenced. Begin early.
  • Pakistan: PMDC records can have delays, particularly for older graduates. Allow extra time.
  • Egypt: Arabic-language documents may require certified translation. Budget additional cost and time for translation.
  • Caribbean schools: Some schools have limited administrative capacity for verification requests. Follow up directly with your school if verification stalls.

Step 3: Meet English Language Requirements

The GMC requires proof of English language proficiency through either IELTS Academic or OET Medicine. Both tests must have been completed within two years of your GMC application date.

For IELTS Academic, you need an overall score of 7.5 with no individual skill below 7.0. For OET Medicine, you need Grade B (350 or above) in all four sub-tests. The tests assess Listening, Reading, Writing, and Speaking — all four must meet the minimum in a single sitting (though the GMC allows combining two sittings within six months in limited circumstances; check current GMC guidance).

Plan your English language test early — ideally complete it before starting your MyIntealth verification so that both processes are running in parallel rather than sequentially. If your IELTS or OET result expires before you complete the PLAB process, you will need to resit the English language test, adding cost and delay to your timeline.

Step 4: Create or Log In to Your GMC Online Account

All PLAB registration and booking happens through the GMC Online portal. If you do not already have an account, create one at the GMC website. You will need:

  • A valid email address (use one you check regularly — all GMC communications go here)
  • A government-issued photo ID (passport strongly recommended, as it is accepted universally)
  • Personal details matching your MyIntealth profile exactly

Once your account is created, upload your identity documents and complete the initial application. The GMC will cross-reference your MyIntealth verification results once they arrive. Your GMC Online account will show the status of your application and, once you are cleared to book, will give you access to the exam booking system.

A common mistake is creating your GMC Online account with a different name spelling than appears on your MyIntealth profile or your identity documents. Even minor discrepancies (such as using a middle name in one place but not another, or a different transliteration of a name from Arabic or Hindi) can cause your application to be flagged and delayed. Use your full legal name exactly as it appears on your passport, consistently across all documents.

Step 5: Book Your PLAB 1 Sitting

Once your GMC Online application is approved and your eligibility confirmed, you can book your PLAB 1 sitting. This is done entirely online via GMC Online. Here is how to choose and secure your slot:

How to Choose the Best Test Centre

PLAB 1 is available at centres in the UK and at international locations including India (multiple cities), Nigeria (Lagos, Abuja), Pakistan (Islamabad, Karachi), Australia, Canada, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka. When choosing a centre, consider:

  • Distance and travel: Choose the closest available centre to minimise pre-exam fatigue from travel.
  • Availability: Popular centres fill very quickly — be flexible about location if your nearest centre is full.
  • Visa requirements: If your preferred centre is in another country, check visa requirements well in advance.
  • Sitting date: The same sitting dates apply across all centres worldwide in a given window, so date flexibility requires changing centre, not waiting for a different date.

What to Do If Your Preferred Date Is Full

If the sitting you want is fully booked, check other centres in your country or nearby countries. Occasionally, seats are released closer to the exam date when other candidates cancel. Check GMC Online regularly — there is no official waiting list, but released seats appear without notice and can be booked immediately by anyone who spots them. If no seat is available in any centre for that sitting, you will need to wait for the next quarterly window.

Step 6: Pay the Exam Fee

The PLAB 1 exam fee for 2025/2026 is £255. Payment is made online at the time of booking through the GMC Online portal. The GMC accepts major credit and debit cards. Keep a record of your payment confirmation and booking reference number — you will need these if you have any queries about your booking.

There is no fee reduction for repeat attempts. If you need to cancel your booking, check the GMC's cancellation policy carefully — fees are typically non-refundable if you cancel within a certain period before the exam date, and they are non-transferable to a different sitting without rebooking at the current fee. Plan your booking carefully to avoid unnecessary cancellations.

Complete Documents Checklist

DocumentPurposeNotes
Valid passportIdentity verificationMust be current and unexpired
Medical degree certificateMyIntealth verificationCertified copy required
Academic transcriptMyIntealth verificationOfficial transcript from medical school
IELTS or OET resultEnglish language proficiencyMust be within 2 years of GMC application
Passport-style photographGMC Online profileRecent, clear background
GMC Online confirmation emailApplication referenceSave and back up
Exam booking confirmationProof of sitting bookingPrint or save digitally
Exam admission letterEntry to test centreSent by GMC approx. 2-4 weeks before exam

Confirmation and the Exam Admission Process

After booking, you will receive a booking confirmation email from the GMC. Approximately two to four weeks before your exam date, the GMC will send your official exam admission letter. This letter contains your candidate number, the test centre address, the reporting time, and instructions for what to bring. Print this letter and keep both a physical copy and a digital backup.

The admission letter is a mandatory document for entry to the test centre. Arrive at the centre without it and you may be refused entry with no recourse. If you have not received your admission letter two weeks before your exam, contact the GMC immediately.

What to Bring on the Day

  • Your official exam admission letter (printed)
  • A valid passport (the same passport used for your GMC Online application)
  • No electronic devices — phones, smart watches, and earphones must be left outside the examination hall or in a secure locker if provided
  • No revision materials — the test centre does not permit books, notes, or printed materials
  • Water and light snacks (for before the exam — check whether the centre allows these inside)

Technical Requirements for Online Booking

The GMC Online booking system is browser-based. Use a modern browser (Chrome, Firefox, Edge, or Safari) and ensure your internet connection is stable. Do not attempt to book on a mobile device — the booking interface works best on a desktop or laptop. If you experience technical issues during booking, the GMC has a support line. However, the best strategy is to avoid technical problems by using a reliable connection and device from the outset.

Screenshot or save your booking confirmation page immediately after completing your booking, before navigating away. If the page does not load correctly, check your email for a confirmation message before attempting to rebook — double-booking the same sitting will cause complications.

Common Registration Mistakes to Avoid

  • Mismatched names: Ensure your name is identical across your passport, MyIntealth profile, and GMC Online account.
  • Expired English language test: Check that your IELTS or OET result will still be within its two-year validity window on the date of your GMC application submission, not just on the date you take the exam.
  • Booking before verification is complete: You cannot book PLAB 1 until the GMC has received your MyIntealth verification results. Attempting to book before this step is complete wastes time and can cause confusion in your application.
  • Ignoring the booking opening time: International centre seats sell out within minutes. Know exactly when booking opens for your preferred sitting and be at your computer ready to act immediately.
  • Not saving confirmation emails: Every email from the GMC is important. Create a dedicated folder in your email account for all GMC correspondence and never delete it.
  • Cancelling without checking the refund policy: If your plans change, understand the cancellation terms before cancelling a booked sitting to avoid losing the full £255 fee.

After Booking: Your Preparation Timeline

Once your PLAB 1 sitting is booked, shift your focus entirely to preparation. Most candidates who pass PLAB 1 on their first attempt dedicate three to four months of structured study. Those who study for shorter periods tend to find themselves underprepared for the breadth of topics covered.

A typical preparation timeline looks like this: in the first four weeks, complete a diagnostic assessment using a question bank, identify your weakest domains from the MLA Content Map, and begin systematic topic review. In weeks five through ten, work through all major clinical domains using question banks and clinical guidelines, aiming to complete at least 1,500 practice questions. In weeks eleven and twelve, focus exclusively on timed full-paper mock exams under exam conditions, review all incorrect answers, and finalise weak areas.

Visit our preparation guide for a detailed week-by-week study plan. See the key dates page for sitting windows and how to plan your registration timeline from start to finish.

Frequently Asked Questions About Registering for PLAB 1

What ID documents are accepted on the day of PLAB 1?

The GMC requires you to bring the same passport you used for your GMC Online application to the test centre. A passport is the only universally accepted identity document at all PLAB 1 centres, both in the UK and internationally. National identity cards, driving licences, or other forms of ID may not be accepted — check the specific requirements stated in your admission letter, as centre policies can vary. If your passport has expired or been renewed since you applied to the GMC, update your GMC Online profile and contact the GMC before the exam to ensure there are no issues on the day.

How many times can I attempt PLAB 1?

You are permitted a maximum of four attempts at PLAB 1. A fifth attempt may be granted if you have completed additional clinical experience or training that the GMC considers appropriate. If you have reached your fourth attempt, you must contact the GMC before booking to discuss eligibility for a further attempt. There is no provision for a fifth attempt simply due to illness on the day or other mitigating circumstances — mitigating circumstances affect the way the result is treated, but they do not add extra attempts beyond the standard limit.

Can I change my test centre after booking?

Changes to test centre bookings are subject to the GMC's booking amendment policy, which may involve cancelling your existing booking and rebooking. This could result in losing your original seat if the sitting you want is now full, and may incur a financial penalty depending on how close to the exam date you make the change. Contact the GMC as soon as you know you need to change your centre rather than waiting until close to the exam date.

What happens if I am unwell on the day of the exam?

If you are too unwell to sit your exam, do not attend the test centre. Contact the GMC as soon as possible and, if applicable, submit a mitigating circumstances application with supporting evidence (such as a medical certificate from your doctor). Turning up and attempting the exam while genuinely unwell is unlikely to produce a good result and the attempt will count towards your attempt limit regardless. If your mitigating circumstances application is accepted, the GMC may allow the attempt not to count or may give you priority booking for the next sitting.

Conclusion

Registering for PLAB 1 is a multi-stage process that requires careful coordination between MyIntealth verification, English language testing, and GMC Online booking. The candidates who navigate it most smoothly are those who start early, keep meticulous records, and treat every deadline seriously. Now that you know exactly how to register for PLAB 1, you can build your personal timeline and start the process with confidence. Once registered and booked, the focus shifts to preparation — visit our preparation guide and our overview of AKT exam pattern to get started.

post_title: PLAB 2 Preparation: How to Excel in the Clinical Assessment
post_excerpt: PLAB 2 is the clinical assessment for IMGs. Here is a practical, evidence-based preparation guide covering station types, communication, and practice strategies.
seo_title: PLAB 2 Preparation Guide: How to Excel in the Clinical Exam
seo_description: Preparing for PLAB 2? Our practical guide covers what to expect in each station type, how examiners score performance, and the best preparation strategies.
html_content:
Effective PLAB 2 preparation is what separates candidates who pass the GMC's clinical assessment on their first attempt from those who face the expense and stress of a resit. PLAB 2 — formally the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA) in the UKMLA framework — is a high-stakes OSCE held exclusively in Manchester that tests not just clinical knowledge but communication, empathy, professionalism, and safe practice across a circuit of 16 clinical stations. With a pass rate of approximately 70-75%, one in four candidates fails on their first attempt, and most of those failures come down to communication skills and preparation method rather than lack of clinical knowledge.

This guide covers every dimension of PLAB 2 preparation: the exam's circuit structure, types of stations, marking methodology, preparation timelines, recommended resources, logistics in Manchester, and the most common mistakes to avoid. Whether you have three months or twelve weeks, this is the preparation framework that works.

What PLAB 2 Assesses: The OSCE Format and MLA Content Map

PLAB 2 is an Objective Structured Clinical Examination (OSCE) held at the GMC's clinical assessment centre in Manchester. There are currently two PLAB 2 test centres, both located in Manchester city centre. The exam circuit consists of 16 scored stations plus at least two rest stations, making for a total examination time of just over three hours.

Each station lasts 8 minutes, with 90 seconds between stations to read the next station brief. The brief is posted on the door of the station and gives you the clinical scenario you are about to enter. You must read it quickly but carefully — candidates who misread the brief and enter the wrong type of station (for example, treating a counselling station as a history-taking station) lose marks immediately.

The content is drawn from the MLA Content Map, the same blueprint used for the AKT. Stations are drawn from all clinical domains: acute medicine, primary care, surgery, obstetrics and gynaecology, paediatrics, psychiatry, and more. The GMC does not publish a specific station list, but analysis of candidate experiences shows that history-taking, communication and counselling, examination, and breaking bad news are the most frequently occurring station types.

PLAB 2 Circuit Structure and Station Types

Station TypeWhat Is TestedFrequencyCommon Examples
History takingSystematic, patient-centred history; ICE frameworkVery commonChest pain, low mood, weight loss
Communication / counsellingExplanation of diagnosis, treatment, or procedureVery commonExplaining diabetes management, pre-op consent
Breaking bad newsEmpathy, structure, patient supportCommonNew cancer diagnosis, miscarriage, death
Clinical examinationSystematic examination techniqueCommonCardiovascular, respiratory, abdominal, neuro
Practical proceduresTechnical skill on manikin or modelOccasionalCannulation, suturing, venepuncture, catheterisation
Data interpretationECG, CXR, blood results in clinical contextOccasionalECG in chest pain, interpreting ABG
Emergency / acutely unwell patientABCDE approach, prioritisationOccasionalSepsis, anaphylaxis, ACS management
Ethics and professionalismConsent, capacity, confidentialityOccasionalRefusing treatment, disclosing error, FGM
Handover (SBAR)Structured clinical communicationOccasionalHanding over an unwell patient to registrar

How PLAB 2 Scoring Works

Understanding the marking system is fundamental to PLAB 2 preparation because it tells you where to invest your effort. Each station is assessed across three domains:

  1. Data gathering / Technical skills: How effectively you gathered clinical information (history, examination findings) or performed the technical procedure.
  2. Clinical management: Your ability to synthesise information, form a differential diagnosis, and propose an appropriate management plan including safety-netting.
  3. Interpersonal skills: Your communication, empathy, rapport, respect, and professionalism in your interaction with the patient (actor).

The UKMLA CPSA uses borderline regression methodology for standard-setting. Rather than a fixed pass mark, the pass mark is calculated statistically based on the distribution of scores across candidates and examiner global ratings. This means the pass mark can vary slightly between sittings. To pass, you must typically pass at least 10 out of 16 stations AND achieve an overall total score above the statistically determined threshold.

Crucially, interpersonal skills carry disproportionate weight in the examiner's global rating. Research consistently shows that the global impression an examiner forms of a candidate is heavily influenced by their communication quality, even when technical data collection is good. This is why communication-focused preparation is the highest-leverage investment you can make.

The ICE Framework in History-Taking Stations

The ICE framework — Ideas, Concerns, and Expectations — is non-negotiable in PLAB 2 history-taking stations. Every history you take must include all three components to score fully on the interpersonal skills domain. ICE is not just a box-ticking exercise; it represents genuine patient-centred practice and scores marks across multiple domains simultaneously.

  • Ideas: "What do you think might be causing your symptoms?" — Acknowledges the patient's own thinking and builds rapport.
  • Concerns: "Is there anything in particular you're worried about?" — Opens the door to the patient's underlying anxiety (often the key issue in the station).
  • Expectations: "What were you hoping we could do for you today?" — Clarifies the patient's goals for the consultation.

In breaking bad news stations, a modified approach is used: Warn first ("I have some difficult news to share"), pause, deliver the news clearly, then use ICE to explore the patient's response. Never rush through ICE — take your time, allow silence, and respond to what the patient says rather than moving immediately to your next planned question.

History-Taking Stations: A Detailed Approach

History-taking stations follow a consistent structure, and memorising this structure until it becomes automatic frees your cognitive bandwidth to actually listen to and respond to the patient. The SOCRATES framework is the backbone for symptom inquiry, supplemented by systematic review of systems, relevant past medical history, drug history, family history, and social history.

Begin every history station with an open question: "Tell me what's brought you in today." Allow the patient (actor) to speak for at least 30 seconds without interruption. This gives you information, shows patient-centred practice, and gives you a moment to calm your nerves. Then systematically use SOCRATES to explore the presenting complaint, followed by targeted systems review relevant to the likely differentials.

Always ask about red flag symptoms relevant to the presenting complaint. Examiners notice if you miss red flags — for example, failing to ask about weight loss, night sweats, or haemoptysis in a patient presenting with chronic cough is a significant omission that suggests poor clinical reasoning. Practise identifying the three or four most important red flags for the twenty most common presenting complaints before your exam.

Communication, Counselling, and Breaking Bad News

Communication stations are where PLAB 2 is won or lost. The technical content of what you say matters less than how you say it. Examiners assess empathy, clarity, pacing, non-verbal cues (in face-to-face stations), and whether you genuinely engage with the patient's emotional responses rather than bulldozing through a scripted explanation.

A useful structure for any explanation or counselling station is: Establish what the patient already knows, Fill in gaps with clear, jargon-free information (use chunk-and-check — give a piece of information, pause, check understanding), Address emotions as they arise, Provide a safety net and next steps, and Summarise.

Phrases that score well in communication stations include: "I can see that's very upsetting, and I'm sorry you're going through this"; "Before I explain the treatment, can I check what you already understand about your condition?"; "That's a really important question — let me explain"; "Is there anything you're particularly worried about that we haven't discussed?"; and "I want to make sure I've explained that clearly — can you tell me back what you understand we've discussed?"

Phrases that lose marks include jargon without explanation ("You have a PE and we need anticoagulation"), failing to acknowledge patient emotions, rushing through explanations without checking understanding, and inappropriate use of technical language ("Your ECG shows ST elevation with reciprocal changes in the inferior leads") without translating into lay terms.

Practical Procedure and Examination Stations

For examination stations, use a systematic approach every time. The examiner expects to see a standard UK clinical examination framework — wash hands, introduce yourself, gain consent, expose appropriately, and follow the standard sequence (inspection, palpation, percussion, auscultation for cardiovascular and respiratory; inspection, palpation, percussion, auscultation for abdominal). Do not skip any step even if you think there is nothing to find — the structure itself scores marks.

For procedure stations involving manikins, talk through what you are doing as you do it. "I'm going to clean the skin with an alcohol swab in a circular motion and allow it to dry" — narrating your actions demonstrates safety awareness and clinical reasoning even when the technical execution is being assessed. Candidates who perform silently often score lower than those who speak throughout, even if their technique is equivalent.

PLAB 2 Preparation Timeline

12-Week Preparation Plan

  • Weeks 1-2: Read PLAB 2 preparation textbook (Saeed's or 1000 PLAB 2 OSCE Cases), review station types and marking criteria, begin practising station openings and closings aloud.
  • Weeks 3-6: Daily practice with a partner — ideally two to three stations per day minimum. Focus on history-taking and communication. Record yourself and watch back to identify verbal and non-verbal habits.
  • Weeks 7-9: Expand to all station types including examinations and procedures. Practice full 16-station mock circuits. Join a preparation course if budget allows.
  • Weeks 10-11: Full mock exams under exam conditions (8 minutes per station, 90-second transitions). Debrief every station. Focus on eliminating remaining habits that lose marks.
  • Week 12: Light revision, confidence-building, and logistics preparation (Manchester accommodation, route to test centre).

8-Week Plan

Compress weeks 1-2 and 3-6 into weeks 1-4 with more intensive daily practice. This requires at least three to four hours of active practice per day and is only feasible if you are not working clinically during preparation. Mock circuits from week 5.

Preparation Courses and Resources

PLAB 2 preparation courses provide structured practice with simulated patients and experienced feedback. When choosing a course, look for: small group sizes (so you get adequate practice time per person), experienced OSCE examiners or PLAB 2 passers as tutors, structured feedback on communication skills specifically, and full mock circuits as part of the course rather than just station practice.

Recommended resources include: Saeed's 1000 PLAB 2 OSCE Cases (the standard reference text), clinical examination guides (Talley and O'Connor or Macleod's), the GMC's Good Medical Practice guidance (particularly for ethics stations), and online case banks from established PLAB 2 providers. TalkingCases.ai and similar platforms offer digital practice scenarios that supplement face-to-face preparation but should not replace it — PLAB 2 is ultimately a human interaction test.

Manchester Logistics: What to Know Before You Arrive

PLAB 2 takes place at two GMC-operated assessment centres in Manchester. Both are in the city centre, accessible by public transport from Manchester Piccadilly station. Allow at least 30 minutes extra for travel on exam day — do not arrive with zero margin. Test centre locations and reporting times are confirmed in your admission documentation from the GMC.

Accommodation: Book in Manchester city centre for the night before your exam. Avoid travelling from another city on the morning of the exam — the anxiety and fatigue of long-distance travel before a three-hour OSCE is a significant disadvantage. Many PLAB 2 candidates stay in the same hotels near the test centres and form informal revision groups the evening before — this peer practice can be valuable but avoid late-night cramming that compromises sleep.

What to wear: Dress professionally and appropriately for a clinical setting. Business casual or smart professional attire is appropriate — a collared shirt or blouse, trousers or skirt, and clean shoes. For examination stations, you may be examined by the simulated patient — ensure your clothing allows easy demonstration of examination positioning. Avoid dangling jewellery that could interfere with procedures. Ties should be tucked away or absent (infection control awareness is noted by examiners).

After PLAB 2: Results and What Happens Next

PLAB 2 results are released approximately eight to twelve weeks after your sitting date via GMC Online. You will receive an email notification when results are available. If you pass, you will receive a pass certificate and instructions for proceeding to GMC registration. If you fail, you will receive a detailed feedback report highlighting performance by domain across stations — read this carefully before planning your resit strategy.

After passing PLAB 2, you can apply for provisional GMC registration, which allows you to work in approved Foundation-level posts in the UK. This application is made via GMC Online and typically takes two to four weeks to process. See our results and scoring page for a full breakdown of what comes after passing both PLAB components.

Common PLAB 2 Mistakes and How to Avoid Them

  • Treating it as a knowledge test: The biggest mistake. Examiners know you know the clinical content. They are assessing whether you can communicate and practise safely. Prioritise communication practice over reading clinical textbooks in your final weeks of preparation.
  • Scripting responses: Candidates who memorise scripts sound robotic and fail to respond to what the patient actually says. Practice frameworks, not scripts.
  • Forgetting to safety-net: Every station should end with a safety-netting statement — what the patient should do if things get worse, when to come back, or who to contact. Missing this is a frequent, avoidable mark-loser.
  • Not managing time: Eight minutes passes very quickly. Practice pacing your consultations so that you reach a natural conclusion with 30-60 seconds to spare for summary and safety-netting, rather than being cut off mid-history.
  • Ignoring the station brief: The 90-second reading time before each station is precious. Use it fully. Identify the station type, the key clinical scenario, and the likely focus area before you open the door.

Frequently Asked Questions About PLAB 2 Preparation

How many stations are in PLAB 2 and how long does the exam take?

PLAB 2 consists of 16 scored clinical stations plus at least two rest stations, giving a total of 18 or more stations in the circuit. Each station lasts 8 minutes, with 90 seconds between stations. Including rest stations, the total examination time is approximately three hours and ten to fifteen minutes. You must maintain focus and professionalism throughout the entire circuit — examiner fatigue or candidate fatigue late in the circuit is a real phenomenon, and maintaining energy and communication quality throughout is part of what PLAB 2 tests.

What is the pass rate for PLAB 2?

The pass rate for PLAB 2 hovers around 70 to 75%. This means approximately one in four candidates fails on their first attempt. The most common reasons for failure are poor communication skills rather than lack of clinical knowledge, poor time management within stations, and failure to use a patient-centred approach. Candidates who invest heavily in communication practice — particularly with a partner role-playing as the patient — perform significantly better than those who focus primarily on reading clinical content.

Can I prepare for PLAB 2 on my own without a course?

It is possible but significantly harder to prepare effectively without face-to-face practice with a partner. PLAB 2 tests human interaction skills that cannot be adequately practised by reading or watching videos alone. At minimum, find a preparation partner — ideally another PLAB 2 candidate — and practise stations together with feedback. If your budget allows, a structured course with experienced tutors and simulated patients provides feedback that is difficult to replicate through self-study. Many successful candidates use a hybrid approach: self-study from textbooks and case banks plus a focused one-week or two-week intensive course in the final preparation phase.

How soon after passing PLAB 1 should I sit PLAB 2?

You must sit PLAB 2 within two years of passing PLAB 1. Beyond this technical requirement, most candidates benefit from a preparation period of three to four months between PLAB 1 and PLAB 2. Sitting too soon — within weeks of PLAB 1 without dedicated OSCE preparation — is the most common cause of first-attempt PLAB 2 failure. Book your PLAB 2 slot as soon as you receive your PLAB 1 pass result (given the four-to-six-month waiting time for slots), and use the time between booking and your sitting for structured preparation.

What happens if I fail PLAB 2?

If you fail PLAB 2, you will receive a detailed feedback report highlighting your performance by domain across stations. Review this report carefully before planning your resit. Identify whether your failures were consistent (suggesting a systematic issue with your approach) or station-specific (suggesting gaps in particular clinical areas). Most candidates who fail PLAB 2 do so due to communication and interpersonal skills deficits rather than clinical knowledge gaps — adjust your preparation accordingly. You can rebook PLAB 2 for the next available slot. The same two-year validity window applies to the resit — as long as your PLAB 1 pass has not expired, you can resit. See our resits and appeals guide for full information on attempt limits and resit strategy.

Conclusion

Successful PLAB 2 preparation is built on communication practice, structured clinical technique, and Manchester logistics. The candidates who pass on their first attempt are not necessarily the most clinically knowledgeable — they are the ones who have practised their consultations until patient-centred communication feels natural rather than scripted, who know how to manage eight minutes without rushing, and who arrive in Manchester rested and ready. Use the preparation timeline in this guide, prioritise face-to-face practice with a partner or course, and treat every practice station as if it were the real exam. For further UKMLA preparation resources, visit our main preparation guide.

post_title: UKMLA Resits: Rules, Limits, and How to Bounce Back
post_excerpt: Failing a UKMLA component is not the end of the road. Here is what you need to know about resit rules, attempt limits, and building an effective comeback plan.
seo_title: UKMLA Resits: Rules, Attempt Limits, and Comeback Strategies
seo_description: Failed the UKMLA AKT or CPSA? Our guide covers resit rules, attempt limits, mitigating circumstances, and the most effective strategies for bouncing back.
html_content:
UKMLA resits are a reality that many candidates face — and understanding the rules, limits, and how to rebuild effectively after a setback is just as important as knowing how to prepare for the first attempt. Whether you have just received a fail result for the AKT, the CPSA, PLAB 1, or PLAB 2, the most important thing to know is that a single fail is not the end of your UK medical licensing journey. Most candidates who fail once go on to pass on a subsequent attempt when they change their approach. This guide covers everything you need to know: attempt limits, validity windows, mitigating circumstances, resit strategy, and the psychological dimension of bouncing back.

The GMC distinguishes between UK medical students and IMGs in how resit rules are structured. UK medical students' resit rules are largely managed at the medical school level within GMC-set parameters, while IMGs apply resit attempts directly through GMC Online. Both pathways are covered in detail below.

How Many Attempts Are Allowed? Complete Attempt Limit Table

The GMC sets maximum attempt limits for each component of the UKMLA. For IMGs taking the legacy PLAB route (still available to candidates who started before the UKMLA transition), the limits are well-established. For the full UKMLA pathway, the GMC has confirmed that up to four attempts at each component are permitted, with a fifth attempt possible in exceptional circumstances.

Exam ComponentCandidate TypeStandard Attempts AllowedAdditional Attempts
AKT (UKMLA)UK medical studentsSet by medical school (typically 2-3)Appeal through medical school
AKT / PLAB 1 (UKMLA)IMGs45th with approved additional training
CPSA (UKMLA)UK medical studentsSet by medical schoolAppeal through medical school
CPSA / PLAB 2 (UKMLA)IMGs45th in exceptional circumstances

It is critical to note that previous PLAB 1 and PLAB 2 attempts may count towards the UKMLA attempt limits as the GMC transitions to the unified examination system. If you have already attempted PLAB 1 or PLAB 2 multiple times, clarify with the GMC how your previous attempts will be counted against your UKMLA attempt limit before booking your next sitting.

What Happens When You Reach Your Attempt Limit

Reaching the standard attempt limit does not automatically mean your medical career in the UK is over, but the options become significantly more restricted. For IMGs who have exhausted four PLAB 1 attempts, a fifth attempt may be permitted if you complete additional clinical training or supervised practice that the GMC considers appropriate and relevant. The GMC must approve the training before you sit a further attempt — you cannot simply complete any clinical work and assume it will be accepted.

For candidates who have exhausted all permitted attempts, the realistic alternatives include: the CESR/CEGPR route if you have postgraduate specialist qualifications and substantial specialist experience; exploring registration with another country's medical authority and then applying for specialist recognition; or, in rare cases, retraining through a recognised programme to obtain a new qualifying degree. Candidates in this position should seek advice from the GMC directly and may benefit from professional legal or regulatory advice.

For UK medical students, reaching a school's internal attempt limit will typically trigger a formal academic review, which may result in required additional training, programme extension, or in extreme cases, withdrawal from the programme. These processes are managed by individual medical schools according to their own regulations and the GMC's overarching fitness to practise and academic progress standards.

Validity Periods and Timing Windows

Understanding validity periods is essential for UKMLA resit planning because running out of validity on a passed component means starting again from scratch — at significant cost and delay.

  • AKT validity: Three years (36 months). The CPSA must be passed within this window.
  • PLAB 1 validity (for legacy pathway candidates): Two years (24 months). PLAB 2 must be sat and passed within this window.
  • English language test validity: Two years from the date of the test. If your test expires before you complete your GMC application and registration, you must resit the English language test.

The most dangerous scenario is accumulating multiple CPSA/PLAB 2 resits that push you towards the three-year AKT/PLAB 1 validity boundary. If you have failed your CPSA twice and are now in your third year after the AKT, you must prioritise your CPSA resit above all else to avoid the AKT expiring. If the AKT expires while you are still attempting the CPSA, you lose your AKT pass and must resit from the beginning.

Mitigating Circumstances: What They Are and How to Apply

Mitigating circumstances are genuine external factors beyond your control that affected your ability to perform to your usual standard in the exam. Examples include serious illness or injury in the period immediately before or during the exam, bereavement of a close family member, significant family emergencies, or traumatic events.

Mitigating circumstances do not guarantee a pass — they allow the relevant body (your medical school for UK students, or the GMC for IMGs) to consider whether the attempt should be treated differently. Depending on the outcome, this might mean the attempt does not count towards your attempt limit, or that you are given priority booking for the next sitting.

Mitigating Circumstances Application Process (Step by Step)

  1. Act immediately: Contact your medical school (UK students) or the GMC (IMGs) as soon as you know you are affected by a mitigating circumstance. Do not wait until results day.
  2. Complete the formal application form: Obtain the mitigating circumstances form from your medical school or GMC Online. Fill it out fully and accurately.
  3. Gather supporting evidence: Evidence must be objective, from an appropriate third party, and specifically relate to the timing of the examination. See the section below on acceptable evidence.
  4. Submit before the deadline: For UK medical students, schools typically require mitigating circumstances applications within a specific window after the exam (often within 7-14 days of the sitting). For IMGs applying to the GMC, submit as soon as possible.
  5. Await the outcome: The reviewing panel will consider your application and evidence and issue a decision. If your application is successful, you will be informed of how the attempt will be treated.
  6. Appeal if unsuccessful: If you believe your application was incorrectly refused, you have the right to appeal. See our appeals guide for information on the appeal process.

What Counts as Acceptable Evidence

  • Medical certificate or letter from a doctor confirming illness and its timing
  • Death certificate or funeral director's documentation for bereavement
  • Police report or official documentation for traumatic events
  • Hospital attendance or admission records
  • Letter from a GP confirming an ongoing health condition that flared up
  • Employer or supervisor letter for work-related emergencies (though these are assessed with more scrutiny)

Self-certification (i.e., your own written account without supporting documentation) is rarely accepted. Personal statements about exam anxiety, financial stress, or general life pressures are not considered mitigating circumstances in the formal sense, though some medical schools have wellbeing support pathways that may be relevant for such situations.

Root Cause Analysis: Why Did You Fail?

The most important step before beginning a resit preparation is identifying the root cause of the failure. Candidates who fail and simply do "more of the same" preparation rarely improve significantly. Failing a medical licensing exam typically falls into one of three categories:

  1. Knowledge gaps: You did not know enough of the clinical content. Identified by consistently getting questions wrong in specific domains, or by finding that you had not covered certain areas of the syllabus.
  2. Technique and exam strategy issues: You knew the content but struggled to apply it under exam conditions — misreading questions, overthinking, poor time management, changing correct answers, or misidentifying what the question was asking.
  3. Exam anxiety and psychological factors: Your preparation was adequate but performance on the day was affected by anxiety, sleep deprivation, physical illness, or other psychological factors.

Each of these requires a different response. For knowledge gaps, the solution is more structured content review with emphasis on weak domains. For technique issues, the solution is more timed practice under realistic exam conditions with deliberate reflection on process. For anxiety, the solution may involve mindfulness, counselling, or anxiety management strategies alongside continued preparation.

Use your result feedback report — which the GMC provides to failing candidates — as your primary diagnostic tool. This report identifies performance by domain and, for the CPSA/PLAB 2, by station type. Read it carefully and objectively before building your resit plan.

AKT Resit Strategy

If you have failed the AKT, a structured resit strategy should address the specific domains where you underperformed, not just general revision. Use the feedback report to identify your two or three weakest domains and make these the priority for your resit preparation.

Common improvements that make the difference in AKT resits include: switching from passive reading to active question-based learning (doing questions, reviewing explanations, noting patterns); using a spaced repetition system for high-yield facts in weak domains; practising under strictly timed conditions to build exam pace; and deliberately practising the technique of reading the lead-in question first before the clinical vignette, which reduces time wasted and improves focus.

Also examine your previous preparation method critically. Did you rely on a single question bank? Did you use questions that were genuinely UKMLA-standard? Did you practise under exam conditions or just casually doing questions while relaxed? The resit is an opportunity to correct preparation method as much as content knowledge.

CPSA / PLAB 2 Resit Strategy

PLAB 2 resits require an honest assessment of your communication skills. Review your feedback report and identify whether failures were concentrated in the interpersonal skills domain — if so, communication practice is the central priority. If failures were in data gathering, focus on systematising your history-taking and examination approach. If failures were in clinical management, focus on clinical decision-making and management planning.

For communication-based failures, the most effective resit preparation involves a significant increase in face-to-face practice with a partner, ideally with specific feedback on identified behaviours (such as not using ICE, rushing through explanations, or failing to safety-net). A structured PLAB 2 preparation course in the lead-up to your resit is often the single most valuable investment for a candidate who failed primarily on communication.

The Psychological Impact of Failing and How to Manage It

Failing a medical licensing exam is genuinely hard. Many candidates experience a period of shock, embarrassment, self-doubt, and grief following a fail result — these are normal responses to a significant setback in a high-stakes professional context. Acknowledging these feelings rather than suppressing them is an important part of recovering effectively.

Practical strategies for managing the psychological impact include: giving yourself a short (but time-limited) period to process the result before beginning resit planning; talking to trusted friends, family, or a mentor who understands the medical training context; using the BMA's wellbeing support services or your medical school's student support team; and reframing the fail as diagnostic information rather than a verdict on your worth as a doctor or person.

The most damaging response to a fail is immediate all-day studying driven by panic, without taking time to do root cause analysis or plan a structured resit strategy. This leads to burnout, reinforced poor habits, and often another fail. Give yourself time to regroup, then plan carefully.

Financial Planning for Resits

Resits have a real financial cost that candidates should plan for honestly. The exam fees alone add up: PLAB 1 is £255 per sitting and PLAB 2 is significantly more expensive. Add MyIntealth verification costs, English language test resits if needed, travel and accommodation for Manchester (for PLAB 2), preparation course fees, and lost income from time spent studying rather than working.

If you are an IMG who is not yet working in the UK, the cumulative cost of multiple resit attempts can be several thousand pounds. Plan your financial runway realistically — if you are on attempt three of four for PLAB 1, ensure you have the financial resources to sustain the preparation period without financial pressure that could affect your exam performance.

Using the UKMLA Feedback Report to Guide Resit Prep

The GMC provides a performance feedback report to all candidates who fail the AKT or CPSA. This report is one of the most valuable resources available to a resitting candidate and should be the foundation of your resit preparation plan. For the AKT, the report breaks down performance by clinical domain and question type. For the CPSA, it breaks down performance by station type and assessment domain (data gathering, clinical management, interpersonal skills).

Create a structured analysis of your feedback report by categorising your weaker areas, estimating the size of the gap between your score and the pass mark in each domain, and using this to allocate proportional preparation time in your resit plan. Domains where you scored very close to the pass mark need less attention than domains where you scored well below it.

Frequently Asked Questions About UKMLA Resits

Is there a waiting period between attempts?

Yes. There is typically a minimum waiting period between PLAB 1 / AKT attempts, which in practice means waiting for the next scheduled quarterly sitting. You cannot sit the same exam component twice in rapid succession. For PLAB 2 / CPSA, the waiting period depends on the next available slot in Manchester, which is commonly four to six months. Check the GMC guidance for any specific minimum waiting period rules that apply to your situation, as these can be updated.

Do mitigating circumstances automatically mean my attempt doesn't count?

No. Submitting a mitigating circumstances application does not guarantee that the attempt will not count towards your limit. The reviewing panel must assess your application and the supporting evidence. Some applications are successful in having the attempt discounted; others result in alternative arrangements (such as a free resit or priority booking) rather than the attempt being removed from the count. The outcome depends on the nature and severity of the mitigating circumstances and the quality of the evidence provided.

Can I resit PLAB 1 while my appeal is ongoing?

This depends on the timing and the nature of the appeal. In most cases, appeals do not automatically prevent you from booking a resit, but sitting a resit while an appeal is pending can affect the appeal process — for example, if the appeal is about whether your previous attempt should count. Contact the GMC before booking a resit if you have an active appeal, and seek advice on the implications of sitting before the appeal is resolved.

What happens to my attempt count during the UKMLA transition?

The GMC has confirmed that previous PLAB 1 and PLAB 2 attempts will count towards the corresponding UKMLA AKT and CPSA attempt limits. If you have already sat PLAB 1 three times and are now registering under the UKMLA system, you have one remaining standard attempt at the AKT. The exact mechanics of how attempts are counted during the transition period should be confirmed directly with the GMC, as the rules continue to be refined during the transition.

When should I consider not resitting?

This is a deeply personal question. Consider seeking external advice — from a medical career adviser, a mentor, or the BMA — if you have reached or are approaching your attempt limit, if you are experiencing significant psychological distress that is not improving with support, or if the cumulative financial and emotional cost of repeated resit attempts is unsustainable. Alternative pathways do exist, including the CESR route for experienced specialists, and there are medical careers outside the UK clinical licensing framework that your medical training qualifies you for. A fail is not the end of your medical career — but it may signal a need to reconsider which pathway is right for you at this time.

Conclusion

UKMLA resits are challenging but manageable with the right approach. The key is honest root cause analysis, a structured resit strategy based on your feedback report, appropriate management of the psychological impact, and careful attention to attempt limits and validity windows. Most candidates who fail once and apply the lessons learned go on to pass. Use the guidance in this article alongside our preparation guide and appeals and resits guide to build your comeback plan with confidence.
