post_title: Safeguarding Adults and Children: A UKMLA Revision Guide
post_excerpt: Safeguarding questions appear in both UKMLA components. Here is what you need to know about recognising abuse, escalation pathways, and legal duties.
seo_title: Safeguarding Adults and Children: UKMLA Revision Guide
seo_description: Safeguarding is a core UKMLA domain. Learn how to recognise abuse, understand escalation pathways, and apply legal duties in AKT questions and CPSA scenarios.
html_content:
Safeguarding in the UKMLA: A Comprehensive Guide to Children and Adults Safeguarding

Safeguarding is one of the highest-weighted topic areas in the UKMLA, and a robust understanding of both children's and adults' safeguarding frameworks is essential for passing the Applied Knowledge Test and for performing competently in Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment stations. In this comprehensive guide to safeguarding for the UKMLA, we cover every major piece of legislation, every category of abuse, the clinical indicators you need to recognise, and the referral processes you must be able to navigate. Whether you are approaching safeguarding from scratch or refining your exam technique, this guide will give you the depth of knowledge the UKMLA expects.

Children Safeguarding: The Legal and Policy Framework

Children Act 1989 and Children Act 2004

The Children Act 1989 remains the cornerstone of children's law in England and Wales. It introduced the paramountcy principle — that the welfare of the child is the court's paramount consideration — and created the framework of parental responsibility, care orders, and child protection processes. Key provisions include:

  • Section 17: Local authorities have a duty to safeguard and promote the welfare of children in need
  • Section 20: Local authorities can provide accommodation to children whose parents consent or where there is no person with parental responsibility
  • Section 31: Courts can make a Care Order (placing a child in local authority care) or a Supervision Order if the child is suffering or is likely to suffer significant harm attributable to inadequate parental care or the child being beyond parental control
  • Section 47: Local authorities have a duty to investigate when they have reasonable cause to suspect that a child in their area is suffering or is likely to suffer significant harm

The Children Act 2004 strengthened multi-agency working following the public inquiry into the death of Victoria Climbié. It created Local Safeguarding Children Boards (subsequently replaced by Safeguarding Children Partnerships under Working Together 2018/2023), and introduced the duty on local authorities, NHS bodies, police, and other agencies to cooperate to safeguard and promote the welfare of children.

Working Together to Safeguard Children 2023

Working Together to Safeguard Children is the statutory guidance that sets out how organisations and individuals should work together to safeguard children. The 2023 edition (updated from 2018) emphasises earlier intervention, strengthened multi-agency safeguarding arrangements, and clearer thresholds for action. For UKMLA candidates, the most important elements are:

  • The information sharing framework — when and how to share information without consent in safeguarding contexts
  • The threshold for referral to children's social care
  • The child protection process: referral → strategy discussion → Section 47 enquiry → child protection conference → child protection plan
  • The role of health professionals in contributing to multi-agency assessments

NICE Guideline NG76: Child Abuse and Neglect

NICE guideline NG76 (updated 2022) provides evidence-based guidance on recognising and responding to child abuse and neglect. It is directly relevant to UKMLA clinical stations. Key points include: recognising that most injuries in non-mobile infants are non-accidental; the importance of a full body examination; documentation of injuries using body maps; the threshold for skeletal surveys; and the importance of taking a full developmental and social history.

Categories of Child Abuse and Clinical Indicators

Physical Abuse (Non-Accidental Injury, NAI)

Physical abuse involves inflicting physical harm on a child, including hitting, shaking, throwing, poisoning, burning, drowning, or suffocating. Fabricated or induced illness (FII, formerly Munchausen syndrome by proxy) is also classified as physical abuse.

Key clinical indicators that should raise concern include:

  • Bruising in unusual sites — bruising on the trunk, back, buttocks, face, ears, or neck in a pre-mobile infant or toddler; bruising in a child who does not yet walk; patterned bruising suggesting an implement
  • Retinal haemorrhages — bilateral retinal haemorrhages extending to the periphery in an infant are highly associated with abusive head trauma (shaken baby syndrome) and require urgent ophthalmological assessment and investigation. They are rarely caused by accidental mechanisms
  • Posterior rib fractures in infants — particularly at the costo-vertebral junctions, caused by compressive squeezing forces during shaking; these are not caused by normal handling or CPR in infants
  • Classic metaphyseal lesions (CML / bucket-handle fractures) — small corner fractures at the metaphyses of long bones in infants, caused by sudden traction or twisting forces; highly specific for non-accidental injury
  • Multiple fractures of different ages — on skeletal survey, fractures at different stages of healing strongly suggest repeated trauma
  • Delay in presentation — presenting days after an injury occurs, without a plausible explanation
  • Inconsistent history — explanations that change, are inconsistent between caregivers, or are incompatible with the child's developmental stage (e.g. a 3-month-old described as having rolled off a sofa)

Differential diagnoses that must be considered and excluded include: osteogenesis imperfecta (brittle bone disease), bleeding disorders (always check a coagulation screen before attributing bruising to NAI), Mongolian blue spots (common in darker-skinned infants, often mistaken for bruising — do not fade on pressure), and metabolic bone disease of prematurity.

Emotional Abuse

Emotional abuse is the persistent emotional maltreatment of a child in a way that severely and persistently affects their emotional development. It may include conveying to a child that they are worthless or unloved, developmentally inappropriate expectations, overprotection, and exposure to domestic abuse. Emotional abuse is present in all other forms of abuse. Clinical indicators include: developmental regression, low self-esteem, anxiety, withdrawal, extreme behaviour, and poor peer relationships. It is often the most difficult category to identify and evidence.

Sexual Abuse

Child sexual abuse involves forcing or enticing a child to take part in sexual activities, whether or not the child is aware of what is happening. It includes contact abuse (touching) and non-contact abuse (exposure to sexual material, grooming online). Indicators include: sexually explicit or precocious knowledge or behaviour; sexualised drawings or play; unexplained genital injury; recurrent urinary tract infections in young girls without anatomical cause; STI in a prepubertal child; and behavioural changes including withdrawal, aggression, or regression.

Child Sexual Exploitation (CSE) is a specific form of sexual abuse where the child is coerced into sexual activity in exchange for something they need or want (e.g. affection, food, accommodation, drugs). Warning signs include: being found in cars with older males; having multiple mobile phones; going missing repeatedly; unexplained gifts or money; withdrawn or secretive behaviour; self-harm or substance misuse; and being known to frequent areas associated with exploitation.

Neglect

Neglect is the persistent failure to meet a child's basic physical and/or psychological needs. It is the most commonly recorded form of abuse in England. Categories include: physical neglect (failure to provide food, clothing, shelter, warmth, or medical care), emotional neglect (failure to provide love and affection), educational neglect (failure to ensure school attendance), and supervisory neglect (leaving young children unsupervised). Clinical indicators include: poor growth and weight gain not explained by medical causes; dental caries; repeated missed medical appointments; dirty, inappropriate, or ill-fitting clothing; and children who present frequently with minor infections or injuries that have not been attended to.

Female Genital Mutilation (FGM)

FGM is a mandatory reporting duty for healthcare professionals in England, Wales, and Northern Ireland. Under the Female Genital Mutilation Act 2003 (as amended by the Serious Crime Act 2015), if a healthcare professional, in the course of their work, identifies that FGM appears to have been carried out on a girl under 18, they must report this to the police. This is a mandatory personal duty — it cannot be delegated. There is no public interest balancing test. The duty also requires asking about FGM in appropriate antenatal consultations. UKMLA candidates must know that the mandatory reporting duty applies specifically to under-18s; for adult women, the standard safeguarding and information sharing principles apply.

Radicalisation and the Prevent Duty

The Prevent duty (under the Counter-Terrorism and Security Act 2015) requires specified authorities, including NHS bodies, to have due regard to the need to prevent people from being drawn into terrorism. In practice, healthcare professionals who are concerned that a patient (including a child) may be vulnerable to radicalisation should refer to the Channel programme through their local Prevent lead. Indicators of radicalisation concern include: expressions of extremist ideology; sudden change in behaviour or social circle; increased secretiveness; expressions of grievance that justify violence; and accessing extremist material online. Channel is a multi-agency early intervention programme — a referral is not a police report and does not automatically trigger criminal investigation.

Trafficking and Modern Slavery

The Modern Slavery Act 2015 criminalises human trafficking and slavery. Children who have been trafficked may present with: unexplained injuries; signs of physical or sexual abuse; malnourishment; signs of excessive work; accompanied by controlling adults who are not family; reluctance to discuss home life; and not knowing their address or seeming confused about their location. Healthcare staff should refer concerns about potential victims to the National Referral Mechanism (NRM) and, for children, to children's social care.

Domestic Abuse and Its Impact on Children

Exposure to domestic abuse is a recognised form of child abuse in its own right. Under the Domestic Abuse Act 2021, a child who sees, hears, or experiences the effects of domestic abuse, and is related to the abuser or victim, is recognised as a victim of domestic abuse. Healthcare professionals should ask about domestic abuse routinely using validated tools, and should be aware that children living with domestic abuse are at significantly increased risk of physical, emotional, and developmental harm.

The Child Safeguarding Process: What Healthcare Professionals Must Do

Information Sharing: When Consent Is Not Required

In safeguarding situations, information can and must be shared without consent if: sharing is necessary to protect a child from harm; the risk would be increased by seeking consent; or seeking consent is not reasonably practicable. The GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018 do not prevent information sharing in safeguarding contexts — they require that sharing is lawful, necessary, and proportionate. Working Together 2023 states that the fear of sharing information must not be allowed to stand in the way of the need to promote the welfare and protect the safety of children.

Referral to Children's Social Care

If a healthcare professional has concerns about a child's safety or welfare, they should:

  1. Discuss the concern with their named/designated safeguarding professional (doctor, nurse, or midwife) in the first instance, unless doing so would cause delay that would place the child at risk
  2. Make a referral to children's social care — this can be by phone (followed up in writing within 48 hours) if there is urgent concern, or in writing if the concern is non-urgent
  3. Document all concerns, observations, and discussions clearly and contemporaneously
  4. If a referral is made and social care decides not to investigate, the referring professional can challenge this decision

Child Protection Medical Assessment

A child protection medical assessment is carried out by a designated or named doctor for child protection, or by a paediatrician with appropriate training. It involves: a detailed history (including full developmental, social, and medical history); full physical examination including a body map; appropriate investigations (skeletal survey, blood tests, ophthalmology review); photography of injuries with consent; and production of a written medical report for the child protection conference. The assessment should always be child-centred and trauma-informed.

Child Protection Plan and Looked After Children

Following a child protection conference, if a child is found to be at continuing risk of significant harm, they are placed on a child protection plan (CPP). The plan sets out the actions required to reduce risk and promote the child's welfare, and is reviewed regularly at review conferences. If a child becomes Looked After (in local authority care), healthcare professionals have specific duties to ensure the child's health needs are assessed at the time of entry into care (initial health assessment) and reviewed annually (review health assessment). These assessments cover physical, emotional, and developmental health needs.

Child Death Review

All child deaths (under 18) in England should be reviewed by a Child Death Review (CDR) process. This involves two components: the Rapid Response (an immediate multi-agency review for unexpected child deaths) and the Child Death Overview Panel (CDOP), which reviews all child deaths in a local area to identify patterns and learning. Healthcare professionals may be required to contribute information to CDR processes.

Adults Safeguarding: The Legal and Policy Framework

The Care Act 2014

The Care Act 2014 placed adult safeguarding on a statutory footing for the first time in England. Section 42 of the Care Act requires local authorities to make enquiries (or cause enquiries to be made) when they have reasonable cause to suspect that an adult in their area:

  • Has needs for care and support (whether or not the authority is meeting those needs)
  • Is experiencing, or is at risk of, abuse or neglect
  • As a result of those care and support needs, is unable to protect themselves from abuse or neglect or the risk of it

This is the Section 42 enquiry — the adult equivalent of the children's Section 47. The Care Act also established Safeguarding Adults Boards (SABs), multi-agency bodies responsible for overseeing and coordinating adult safeguarding in each local area.

The Six Principles of Adult Safeguarding

The Care Act and associated statutory guidance set out six principles that should underpin all adult safeguarding activity:

  1. Empowerment — People being supported and encouraged to make their own decisions and informed consent
  2. Prevention — It is better to take action before harm occurs
  3. Proportionality — The least intrusive response appropriate to the risk presented
  4. Protection — Support and representation for those in greatest need
  5. Partnership — Local solutions through services working with their communities
  6. Accountability — Accountability and transparency in delivering safeguarding

These principles appear in UKMLA scenarios, particularly in questions about balancing a vulnerable adult's right to autonomy against the duty to protect them from harm. The Care Act's emphasis on empowerment means that adult safeguarding is not about removing autonomy — it is about ensuring that adults with care needs can exercise genuine choice and control.

Categories of Adult Abuse

The Care Act 2014 and associated statutory guidance define ten categories of adult abuse:

Category Definition Clinical Indicators
Physical abuse Hitting, slapping, pushing, kicking, misuse of medication, restraint, inappropriate physical sanctions Unexplained injuries; bruising in unusual sites; injuries at different stages of healing; fear or flinching around carers
Psychological / emotional abuse Threats, humiliation, blaming, controlling, intimidation, coercion, harassment, verbal abuse, isolation Low self-esteem; anxiety or depression; deference to carer in consultation; reluctance to speak freely
Financial or material abuse Theft, fraud, exploitation, pressure in connection with wills or financial transactions, misuse of property or benefits Unexplained withdrawal of funds; inability to pay for essentials; new or changed power of attorney; sudden change in will
Sexual abuse Rape, indecent exposure, sexual harassment, unwanted touching, sexual acts without consent, producing sexual material Unexplained genital injury; STI without explanation; psychological distress; sexualised behaviour
Neglect and acts of omission Ignoring medical, emotional, or physical care needs; failure to provide access to services; withholding medication or food Malnutrition; pressure ulcers; poor hygiene; missed medications; untreated medical conditions
Discriminatory abuse Abuse based on a protected characteristic (race, gender, disability, religion, sexual orientation, age) Derogatory language; denial of care based on identity; isolation from cultural community
Organisational / institutional abuse Neglect and poor care practice within an institution or care setting; systematic abuse of multiple people Institutional routines that disregard dignity; blanket policies overriding individual needs; inadequate staffing
Modern slavery Slavery, human trafficking, forced labour, domestic servitude Signs of physical abuse; malnourishment; restricted movement; appears controlled; no access to earnings
Domestic abuse Any incident of controlling, coercive, threatening behaviour, violence or abuse between those aged 16+ who are or have been intimate partners or family members Frequent attendances; injuries inconsistent with explanation; partner accompanies and answers for patient; minimises or defends partner
Self-neglect Neglecting to care for one's personal hygiene, health, or surroundings; hoarding Serious malnutrition; untreated medical conditions; unsafe home environment (fire or fall hazards); extreme hoarding

Coercive Control and Domestic Abuse

The Domestic Abuse Act 2021 extended the legal framework significantly, creating a statutory definition of domestic abuse that includes physical, sexual, financial, emotional, and psychological abuse. The Act recognised children as victims in their own right (as noted above) and strengthened protection for victims. From a criminal law perspective, coercive control (a pattern of controlling or coercive behaviour in an intimate or family relationship) has been an offence under the Serious Crime Act 2015 since 2015. Healthcare professionals should be familiar with the signs of coercive control, which may be subtler than physical violence: partners who attend all appointments and answer on the patient's behalf; patients who appear to require permission to speak or to make decisions; unexplained financial difficulties; and social isolation.

Domestic Abuse Risk Assessment Tools

The DASH (Domestic Abuse, Stalking, Honour-Based Violence and Forced Marriage Risk Identification Checklist) is the most widely used risk assessment tool for domestic abuse in the UK. It helps practitioners identify whether a victim is at standard, medium, high, or very high risk of further harm. High-risk cases are referred to a MARAC (Multi-Agency Risk Assessment Conference), a regular meeting of statutory and voluntary agencies that share information to manage the highest-risk cases of domestic abuse. An Independent Domestic Violence Adviser (IDVA) provides specialist support to high-risk victims and coordinates the response to their needs.

Mental Capacity Act and Adult Safeguarding

MCA and Safeguarding Interface

One of the most complex areas in adult safeguarding is the interface between the Mental Capacity Act 2005 and safeguarding decisions. The key principle is that adult safeguarding must respect the autonomy of adults who have capacity to make their own decisions, even if those decisions appear unwise or place them at risk. An adult with capacity who chooses to remain in an abusive relationship, or who chooses to live in unsanitary conditions (self-neglect), has the right to make that choice. The role of safeguarding professionals is to ensure the choice is genuinely informed and uncoerced, to offer support and information, and to document that the risks have been discussed.

When an adult may lack capacity, a formal MCA capacity assessment must be carried out. If they are found to lack capacity for the relevant decision, a best interests process involving all relevant parties must be undertaken. Deprivation of liberty (e.g. restricting a person's movement to protect them from harm) requires authorisation under either the Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards (DoLS) or the new Liberty Protection Safeguards (LPS).

Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards (DoLS) and Liberty Protection Safeguards (LPS)

DoLS (under the Mental Capacity Act 2005, schedule A1, inserted by the Mental Health Act 2007) currently apply to care homes and hospitals. If a person who lacks capacity is being cared for in circumstances that amount to a deprivation of their liberty (as defined by the Cheshire West Supreme Court ruling 2014 — the acid test: is the person under continuous supervision and control, and not free to leave?), a DoLS authorisation must be obtained from the supervisory body (local authority for care homes, hospital managers for NHS settings). The application is made by the care home or hospital (the managing authority).

The Liberty Protection Safeguards (LPS), introduced by the Mental Capacity (Amendment) Act 2019, are intended to replace DoLS. At the time of writing, LPS have not yet been implemented. UKMLA candidates should be familiar with both systems and be aware that DoLS is the current legal framework, while LPS represents the intended future system.

Key Roles and Organisations in Safeguarding

Role / Body Context Function
MASH (Multi-Agency Safeguarding Hub) Children and adults Single point of contact for safeguarding referrals; multi-agency information sharing at point of referral to enable better risk assessment
SAB (Safeguarding Adults Board) Adults Strategic oversight body for adult safeguarding in each local area; publishes Safeguarding Adult Reviews (SARs) following serious cases
Safeguarding Children Partnership Children Multi-agency body replacing LSCBs; oversees child safeguarding arrangements; commissions Child Safeguarding Practice Reviews (CSPRs)
LADO (Local Authority Designated Officer) Children Manages allegations against adults who work with children; must be notified within one working day of an allegation against a professional
MARAC (Multi-Agency Risk Assessment Conference) Adults (domestic abuse) Multi-agency meeting to manage high-risk domestic abuse cases; shares information and coordinates protective actions
IDVA (Independent Domestic Violence Adviser) Adults (domestic abuse) Specialist advocate for high-risk domestic abuse victims; attends MARAC; supports victims through criminal justice and civil processes
DHR (Domestic Homicide Review) Adults Review commissioned when a person aged 16+ dies as a result of domestic abuse; identifies learning to prevent future homicides
Named Doctor for Safeguarding Children and adults Clinical expert within a provider organisation; supports and advises staff on safeguarding; trains and develops safeguarding practice
Designated Doctor for Safeguarding Children Senior clinician in the ICB/CCG providing strategic leadership and expert advice on child safeguarding across a health area

Key Legislation Summary

Legislation Year Key Provision for UKMLA
Children Act 1989 1989 Paramountcy principle; S17 (children in need); S47 (duty to investigate significant harm); care and supervision orders
Children Act 2004 2004 Multi-agency cooperation; Local Safeguarding Children Boards (now Safeguarding Children Partnerships)
Female Genital Mutilation Act 2003 (as amended by Serious Crime Act 2015) 2003/2015 Mandatory reporting duty for healthcare staff identifying FGM in under-18s; offence of failing to report
Mental Capacity Act 2005 2005 Capacity assessment; best interests; LPA; advance decisions; DoLS (Schedule A1)
Safeguarding Vulnerable Groups Act 2006 2006 Barred lists; DBS checks for those working with children and vulnerable adults
Care Act 2014 2014 Statutory adult safeguarding duty; S42 enquiries; Safeguarding Adults Boards; six safeguarding principles
Serious Crime Act 2015 2015 FGM mandatory reporting; coercive control offence
Counter-Terrorism and Security Act 2015 2015 Prevent duty; Channel referrals for those at risk of radicalisation
Modern Slavery Act 2015 2015 Trafficking and slavery offences; National Referral Mechanism
Domestic Abuse Act 2021 2021 Statutory definition of domestic abuse; children as victims; new protections for victims

Referral Thresholds: Knowing When to Act

One of the most practically important skills in safeguarding is knowing the appropriate threshold for action. The following framework applies in both children's and adults' safeguarding:

Level of Concern Examples Action
Universal (no additional needs) Healthy child attending routine checks; adult with no care needs No specific safeguarding action; provide universal services
Additional needs (early help) Child with developmental delay; family under stress; adult with emerging care needs Early help assessment; signpost to support services; common assessment framework
Child in need (S17) Child whose health or development is impaired or likely to be impaired without services Referral to children's social care for assessment; family support services
Significant harm / abuse (S47 / Care Act S42) Suspected physical abuse; domestic abuse with child in home; adult with care needs being financially exploited Urgent referral to children's or adults' social care; strategy discussion / S42 enquiry; consider immediate protective action (police, emergency court order)
Immediate danger Child in immediate physical danger; adult at imminent risk of serious harm Call 999 / police; Emergency Protection Order (children); if no one else to act — clinician may need to take immediate protective steps

Safeguarding in the UKMLA: Common Exam Scenarios

Scenario Type 1: The Infant with Unexplained Injuries

A 4-month-old is brought to A&E by their mother with a swollen thigh. X-ray shows a mid-shaft femoral fracture. The mother says the child "must have got stuck in the cot." What do you do? In this scenario: femoral fractures in pre-mobile infants are highly suspicious of non-accidental injury; the explanation is implausible for the injury; the correct actions include admitting the child, informing the on-call paediatric consultant, informing your safeguarding lead, requesting a skeletal survey (to look for other injuries), and making a referral to children's social care — not discharging the child with the parents until a full assessment has been completed. Do not confront the parents aggressively, but explain that you need to investigate the cause of the fracture fully.

Scenario Type 2: The Adult with Capacity Refusing Help

An 80-year-old woman with moderate dementia (documented to have fluctuating capacity) is referred by her GP. Her family believe her husband of 60 years is financially abusing her — withdrawing large sums from her account. She says she is happy and does not want anyone to "interfere." What do you do? Key considerations: first, carry out a formal capacity assessment for the specific decision (whether to engage with safeguarding support). If she lacks capacity for this decision, a best interests decision must be made. If she has capacity, respect her autonomy but ensure she has had the opportunity to receive information about the support available, document the conversation, and consider a S42 enquiry by the local authority regardless (the Care Act duty to enquire does not depend on victim consent). Referral to the local authority should be made because she has care and support needs and appears to be at risk of financial abuse, even if she is reluctant to engage.

Scenario Type 3: Disclosure of Domestic Abuse

A 32-year-old woman attends her GP for the third time in six months with minor injuries — this time a bruised forearm. Her male partner is in the waiting room. She discloses that her partner hits her when he has been drinking, but begs you not to tell anyone because she is afraid of what he will do. What do you do? Correct approach: thank her for telling you; validate her experience; ensure her immediate safety (is she safe to go home today?); provide information about support services (IDVA, local domestic abuse organisation, the 24-hour National Domestic Abuse Helpline); ask about any children in the household (their safety may require a separate safeguarding referral); complete a DASH risk assessment; document carefully; do not breach confidentiality without her consent unless there is an immediate risk to life or children are at risk. Arrange follow-up. Offering to contact the police without her consent, unless there is immediate risk to life, is not the correct response.

Frequently Asked Questions About Safeguarding and the UKMLA

Do I need consent to make a safeguarding referral?

For children's safeguarding, you should seek consent from parents/carers and (where appropriate) the child before making a referral to children's social care, unless doing so would place the child at increased risk of harm. You do not need parental consent if you believe obtaining it would compromise the child's safety. For adult safeguarding, you should ordinarily seek consent from the adult at risk, respecting their autonomy. However, a referral can be made without consent if the person lacks capacity to consent, if seeking consent would increase the risk of harm, or if there is a serious public interest in disclosure. The safeguarding concern, risk, and consent discussion should all be documented clearly.

What is the difference between a Section 47 enquiry and a Section 17 assessment?

A Section 47 enquiry is triggered when there is reasonable cause to suspect that a child is suffering, or is likely to suffer, significant harm. It is led by children's social care and involves police and health professionals. Its purpose is to determine whether action needs to be taken to safeguard the child. A Section 17 assessment relates to children in need — those whose health or development is impaired, or is likely to be impaired, without provision of services. The threshold for Section 17 is lower than for Section 47; it does not require suspected harm, only unmet need. S47 investigations can run concurrently with S17 assessments if both apply.

What should I do if a colleague is suspected of abusing a patient?

Allegations against professionals who work with children must be reported to the Local Authority Designated Officer (LADO) within one working day. Failure to report in a timely way is itself a safeguarding concern. The LADO coordinates the response to allegations against professionals and ensures that the investigation is conducted appropriately, separating any criminal investigation from any disciplinary or child protection enquiry. For allegations involving adults, the equivalent process involves notifying the Safeguarding Adults Board and the appropriate regulatory bodies. You should not attempt to investigate the allegation yourself.

How does the Mental Capacity Act interact with safeguarding adults?

The MCA and adult safeguarding run in parallel but serve different purposes. The MCA governs decision-making for people who lack capacity; adult safeguarding applies to people with care and support needs who are at risk of abuse or neglect, regardless of their capacity. A person with capacity can still be subject to a Section 42 enquiry if they have care and support needs and are at risk. However, if a person with capacity has made an informed, uncoerced decision to remain in a situation that others consider dangerous, safeguarding professionals must respect that decision and focus on empowering the person and reducing risk rather than overriding their choice.

Is it ever appropriate to break confidentiality in safeguarding without the patient's consent?

Yes, in specific circumstances. Confidentiality can be broken without consent in safeguarding when: a child is at risk of significant harm; an adult lacks capacity and the disclosure is in their best interests; the risk of harm to others (including children in the household) is serious, real, and imminent; there is a mandatory reporting duty (FGM in under-18s); or there is a statutory obligation to share information (e.g. during a Section 47 enquiry). The decision to break confidentiality must be documented carefully, including the reason for the decision and (where appropriate) whether an attempt was made to obtain consent. Even in safeguarding contexts, only the minimum necessary information should be shared.

Conclusion: Building Safeguarding Confidence for the UKMLA

Safeguarding in the UKMLA requires you to move beyond knowing the legislative framework to demonstrating the kind of professional judgement that keeps vulnerable people safe. The exam scenarios will test your ability to recognise abuse indicators, apply the correct threshold for referral, navigate consent and capacity in safeguarding contexts, and understand the roles of the different professionals and organisations involved. Practise with scenario-based questions, revisit the key indicators for each category of abuse, and make sure you can distinguish between the children's and adults' safeguarding frameworks without confusing them.

To reinforce your understanding, explore our detailed UKMLA Syllabus Guide, which maps safeguarding topics to the MLA Content Map, or visit our UKMLA Preparation Hub for curated question bank recommendations and revision strategies tailored to the professional and ethical components of the exam.

post_title: How to Write UKMLA Revision Notes That Actually Work
post_excerpt: Not all revision notes are equally effective. Here is how to structure UKMLA notes using active recall principles and the MLA Content Map as your scaffold.
seo_title: How to Write UKMLA Revision Notes That Actually Work
seo_description: Effective UKMLA revision notes are built on active recall, not passive re-reading. Learn how to structure notes around the MLA Content Map for maximum retention.
html_content:
UKMLA Revision Notes Strategy: How to Study Smarter and Retain More for the MLA

An effective UKMLA revision notes strategy is the single biggest factor separating candidates who pass with confidence from those who spend hundreds of hours studying and still feel underprepared on exam day. The UKMLA Applied Knowledge Test (AKT) covers an enormous breadth of clinical medicine, public health, ethics, and professional practice across the full MLA Content Map. The Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA) demands not just knowledge but the ability to apply it under pressure in a realistic clinical environment. In this comprehensive guide, we break down the science of effective revision, map the best resources available for UKMLA preparation, explain how to build an evidence-based revision system using notes, flashcards, and question banks, and show you how to track and learn from your errors so that every hour of study translates into genuine exam performance.

Why Passive Revision Fails: The Science of Learning

Most medical students arrive at UKMLA preparation having spent years using the same revision techniques they learned in secondary school: re-reading notes, highlighting textbooks, and transcribing lecture slides into tidy summaries. These methods feel productive because they create a sense of familiarity with the material — a phenomenon cognitive psychologists call the fluency illusion. When you re-read something and it flows easily, your brain interprets that ease as evidence that you have learned it. But familiarity is not memory, and recognition is not recall.

The Failure of Highlighting and Re-Reading

A landmark 2013 meta-analysis by Dunlosky and colleagues (published in Psychological Science in the Public Interest) evaluated the utility of ten common study techniques. Highlighting and underlining received a "low utility" rating — there was little to no benefit compared to reading without marking. Re-reading received the same low utility rating. Both techniques are passive: they do not force the brain to work hard, and information is not encoded deeply enough to be retrievable under exam conditions.

Transcribing notes — copying out lecture content into your own exercise books — is slightly better because it requires some active processing, but the benefit is largely from the selection and reframing involved, not from the act of writing itself. If you are simply copying text verbatim, you are spending time on a low-yield activity. And critically, a well-organised folder of typed notes is not revision — it is preparation for revision. The learning happens when you put the notes away and try to retrieve the information.

Cognitive Load and Overloading Working Memory

Another reason passive revision fails for the UKMLA is cognitive load. The AKT presents complex clinical scenarios that require you to hold multiple pieces of information in working memory simultaneously — patient demographics, presenting complaint, examination findings, investigation results, differential diagnoses, and management options — and to apply reasoning across all of them in a short time. If your knowledge is only superficially encoded (because you have re-read it rather than practised retrieving it), working memory becomes rapidly overloaded and performance deteriorates. Deep encoding through active retrieval practice frees up working memory by automating the retrieval of core knowledge, allowing higher-order reasoning to function effectively.

The Principles of Effective Revision

Retrieval Practice

Retrieval practice — the act of recalling information from memory, rather than passively reviewing it — is the single most evidence-supported learning technique available. Every time you retrieve a memory, you strengthen its neural trace and make it easier to retrieve in the future. This is the testing effect, robustly demonstrated across decades of cognitive psychology research. For UKMLA purposes, retrieval practice means doing questions, not reading. Every session with a question bank is a retrieval practice session. Closing your notes and trying to write out the management of a condition from memory is retrieval practice. Using flashcards to test yourself (covering the answer before reading it) is retrieval practice. Simply reading the question and then immediately checking the answer is not.

The practical implication for UKMLA revision is this: your primary activity in each study session should be answering questions or testing yourself, not reading. Notes and resources are reference material to consult after you have tested yourself and identified gaps. This is the opposite of how most students naturally organise their revision.

Spaced Repetition and the Ebbinghaus Forgetting Curve

Hermann Ebbinghaus, a 19th-century German psychologist, described the forgetting curve — the exponential rate at which newly learned information is forgotten without reinforcement. Without review, the average person forgets approximately 40% of new material within 20 minutes, 56% within an hour, and 66% within a day. However, each time a memory is retrieved and reinforced, the forgetting curve resets and flattens — the next forgetting curve is shallower, and subsequent review intervals can be progressively lengthened.

Spaced repetition is the practice of scheduling reviews at increasing intervals — typically one day, three days, one week, two weeks, one month — to intercept the forgetting curve at the optimal moment. Reviewing material just as you are about to forget it produces far stronger encoding than reviewing it when it is still fresh. Spaced repetition is the algorithmic principle behind flashcard apps such as Anki, and it is why Anki-based revision is substantially more efficient than reading notes at a fixed schedule.

For UKMLA preparation, spaced repetition means revisiting question bank topics and flashcard decks at intervals, not just working through them once. A common mistake is to complete a question bank topic, score 85%, feel satisfied, and never return to it. Six weeks later, when the topic appears in the AKT, the information has largely been forgotten. Building a spaced repetition schedule — either through Anki or through a manual system of topic review — ensures that material stays retrievable across the full preparation period.

Interleaving

Interleaving — mixing different topics within a study session, rather than blocking all content by subject — is another evidence-based technique that feels less efficient in the moment but produces superior long-term retention. Blocked practice (doing 50 cardiology questions, then 50 respiratory questions, then 50 neurology questions) produces rapid within-session improvement but poor retention. Interleaved practice (mixing cardiology, respiratory, and neurology questions within each session) produces slower apparent progress but far better retention and transfer to novel scenarios.

The UKMLA AKT naturally uses interleaved presentation — you will not be given a block of 30 cardiology questions; you will move between topics unpredictably. Training your brain to switch between topics fluently is therefore both a learning strategy and an exam strategy. Randomising your question bank sessions (rather than doing one topic at a time) from mid-preparation onwards prepares you for the unpredictability of the real exam.

Elaboration

Elaboration means connecting new information to what you already know, asking yourself why something is true rather than simply what is true. For example, rather than memorising that atenolol is relatively contraindicated in asthma, elaborative encoding asks: why? (Beta-2 blockade in bronchial smooth muscle causes bronchoconstriction.) What are the exceptions? (Highly cardioselective agents such as bisoprolol in very low doses may be used with caution in mild, well-controlled asthma when the benefit clearly outweighs the risk, with specialist supervision.) How does this connect to other pharmacology? (Beta-2 agonists treat bronchoconstriction — they work on the same receptors in the opposite direction.) This web of connections makes the knowledge far more robust and transferable than a simple rule learned by rote.

Dual Coding

Dual coding combines verbal information with visual representations — diagrams, flowcharts, tables, timelines, and clinical algorithms. Processing the same information in two modalities (language and image) creates two distinct memory traces that reinforce each other. For UKMLA purposes, this means drawing out clinical algorithms (e.g. the NICE CKS management pathway for chest pain), creating timeline diagrams for disease progression, and using ECG and CXR images alongside textual descriptions. The evidence for dual coding in medical education is strong: students who study ECG patterns using both annotated images and verbal descriptions perform better on interpretation tasks than those who use either alone.

Choosing Your UKMLA Revision Resources

Question Banks: The Core of UKMLA Revision

For the AKT, the question bank is the most important resource you will use. Extensive practice with well-written, exam-style questions is more valuable than any textbook. The following question banks are the most widely used for UKMLA preparation:

Resource Format Cost (approx.) AKT Focus CPSA Focus Key Strengths Limitations
Passmedicine Web / mobile, SBAs ~£60–80/year Excellent Limited Huge bank (~7,000+ questions); mapped to MLA Content Map; detailed explanations with NICE/BNF links; widely used by UK students Interface somewhat dated; limited clinical skills content
Quesmed Web / mobile, SBAs + OSCE material ~£79–120/year Excellent Good Strong explanations; CPSA station preparation material; custom quiz modes; active development for UKMLA Smaller bank than Passmedicine; newer platform
BMJ OnExamination Web, SBAs ~£120–180/year Very good Limited High-quality questions; strong clinical reasoning; professional interface; peer-reviewed content Expensive; less specifically mapped to UKMLA vs MRCP
UWorld UKMLA Web / mobile, SBAs ~£100–150/year Very good Limited Very detailed, educationally rich explanations; excellent for deep understanding; US pedigree adapted for UK Some US content may not directly map to UKMLA; expensive
MedRevisions Web, SBAs + notes ~£50–80/year Good Limited Integrated notes and questions; good for foundational knowledge; affordable Smaller bank; explanations less detailed than competitors
GMC Sample Questions (official) Web, free Free Essential Essential Written by the exam setters; authentic question style and difficulty; available on the GMC website Small number of questions; not sufficient alone
MSC Practice Exam Web, simulated exam Free / low cost Essential Essential Closest simulation of the real AKT format and CPSA stations; timed; official format Limited availability; should be saved for late preparation

Official Resources: Non-Negotiable

No question bank can replace the official resources that the exam setters expect candidates to know. These include:

NICE CKS (Clinical Knowledge Summaries) — The UKMLA is aligned with NICE guidelines. NICE CKS provides concise, regularly updated summaries of NICE guidance for primary care conditions, and the AKT question writers use these summaries when writing clinical management questions. Rather than using CKS as primary reading, use it to verify and refine your answers when you get questions wrong in a question bank — look up the CKS summary for the condition and check your management against the current guideline.

The BNF (British National Formulary) — The BNF is the authoritative reference for prescribing in the UK. Dose calculations, drug interactions, contraindications, and side effect profiles in UKMLA questions are based on BNF content. The BNF is freely available online and via the BNF app. Knowing which drugs are in which categories, their key interactions, and their main contraindications is essential AKT preparation. The BNF should be used as a reference to check prescribing details you are unsure of, not as revision reading.

GMC Good Medical Practice 2024 — As discussed in our UKMLA overview, GMP is the ethical framework tested across both the AKT and the CPSA. Read GMP in full at least once, and use the domain structure to organise your ethics revision.

MLA Content Map — Published by the Medical Schools Council (MSC), the MLA Content Map is the official syllabus for the UKMLA. It lists all the conditions, presentations, and professional themes that can be tested. Using the Content Map as a checklist — systematically working through each item and assessing your confidence — ensures comprehensive coverage and prevents gaps.

Textbooks and Reference Texts

The UKMLA does not require any specific textbook. The vast majority of candidates use question banks and online resources rather than reading textbooks cover to cover. However, a single integrated medical textbook can be valuable as a reference for conditions where you need deeper understanding than a question bank explanation provides. Commonly used options include:

  • Kumar and Clark's Clinical Medicine — comprehensive and well-organised; useful for reading around complex conditions
  • Davidson's Principles and Practice of Medicine — similar depth; some candidates prefer the presentation style
  • Cheese & Chalk (Oxford Handbook of Clinical Medicine) — concise and portable; good for on-the-ward revision and quick reference
  • Illustrated Colour Text (ICT) series — system-based; useful for visual learners

The key principle: use textbooks selectively and reactively, not as the primary revision activity. Read about a condition in a textbook after getting related questions wrong, not as your first engagement with the material.

Making Effective UKMLA Revision Notes

The One-Page Summary Approach

The one-page summary (or one-page A4 sheet per condition) is one of the most effective formats for UKMLA notes. The discipline of condensing everything you know about a condition onto a single page forces you to prioritise the most important information. A well-structured one-pager for a condition typically includes:

  • Definition / epidemiology — who gets it, how common, key risk factors
  • Pathophysiology — one or two sentences explaining the underlying mechanism (crucial for elaborative encoding)
  • Clinical features — the classic presentation, key discriminating features, red flags
  • Investigations — first-line, gold standard, what findings to expect
  • Management — aligned with NICE guidelines; first-line, second-line, specialist referral thresholds
  • Complications — what to watch for
  • Key differentials — one or two conditions it is commonly confused with and how to distinguish

Do not create one-pagers for every condition from the outset. Create them reactively — when you encounter a condition you consistently get wrong, or when you identify a topic that keeps appearing in your error log (see below). If you try to create comprehensive one-pagers for every condition in the MLA Content Map at the start of revision, you will spend all your time making notes and none of your time actually learning.

Comparison Tables

Comparison tables are particularly valuable for groups of conditions that are frequently confused in UKMLA scenarios: the inflammatory arthropathies (rheumatoid, psoriatic, ankylosing spondylitis, reactive, gout, pseudogout); the causes of a raised ANCA (PR3 vs MPO, GPA vs MPA vs EGPA); the antihypertensives and their specific indications and contraindications; the inherited bleeding disorders; and the different types of renal tubular acidosis. A well-constructed comparison table takes about 30 minutes to create and provides a reference you can test yourself against repeatedly.

Mnemonics: When to Use Them

Mnemonics are useful for memorising lists that have no inherent logic or structure — the causes of hypercalcaemia (CHIMPANZEES), the features of Horner's syndrome (PAM — Ptosis, Anhydrosis, Miosis), or the Wells score criteria. However, there are two important caveats. First, a mnemonic only works if you remember what each letter stands for and can immediately apply it clinically — a mnemonic that produces a list you cannot explain is worthless. Always learn the clinical meaning behind each item in a mnemonic. Second, creating your own mnemonics for the things you personally struggle to remember is more effective than borrowing someone else's, because the act of creating the mnemonic is itself a form of deep encoding.

Anki Flashcards: The Optimal UKMLA Tool

Anki is a free, open-source flashcard application that uses a spaced repetition algorithm to schedule card reviews at optimal intervals based on your individual performance. It is, by a significant margin, the most evidence-aligned digital learning tool available to medical students. For UKMLA revision, Anki offers several distinct advantages:

  • Image occlusion — the Image Occlusion Enhanced add-on allows you to cover specific labels on ECGs, chest X-rays, ABG results, or blood film images and test yourself on identification. This is particularly valuable for the CPSA, which involves interpreting clinical investigations. Creating Anki cards from real ECG strips (e.g. atrial fibrillation, STEMI, third-degree heart block) with occluded labels is one of the highest-yield things you can do for investigations-based revision
  • Spaced repetition algorithm — Anki manages the forgetting curve for you, scheduling cards for review at the point where you are about to forget them. You rate each card as Again (failed), Hard, Good, or Easy, and Anki adjusts the interval accordingly
  • Portability — Anki decks are available on iOS and Android, allowing revision during commutes, lunch breaks, or any spare time
  • Customisation — you can create cards precisely targeted at your personal weaknesses

A note on shared Anki decks: several UKMLA-specific Anki decks are available for free download from the AnkiWeb shared deck library, and some are of high quality. However, using a shared deck uncritically is risky: decks may contain errors, outdated information, or content misaligned with the current MLA Content Map and NICE guidelines. The most effective approach is to build your own deck progressively, creating new cards as you encounter gaps in knowledge through question bank practice, and supplementing with carefully reviewed shared cards only for topics you have verified for accuracy. Do not fall into the trap of downloading a 5,000-card shared deck and spending months trying to keep up with it before you have built a solid foundation through question practice.

The Error Log System

Why You Must Track Wrong Answers

The error log is one of the most powerful and most underused tools in UKMLA preparation. Every question you answer incorrectly is a diagnostic signal: it tells you exactly where your knowledge is weak. Without a systematic way of capturing and learning from these signals, you will make the same mistakes repeatedly, wasting practice questions and revision time.

An error log is a systematic record of every question you get wrong, including the root cause of the error and the corrective action you have taken. It transforms your question bank from a pass/fail scoring exercise into a personalised learning system.

How to Build an Error Log

Your error log should be a simple spreadsheet or digital document with the following columns:

  1. Date — when you answered the question
  2. Topic / condition — what the question was about
  3. Question summary — one-sentence description of the clinical scenario
  4. My answer — what you chose
  5. Correct answer — what the right answer was
  6. Root cause — why did you get it wrong? (see below)
  7. Action taken — what you did to address the gap
  8. Reviewed? — a checkbox for weekly review

The most important column is root cause. There are typically five types of error in UKMLA practice:

  • Knowledge gap — you simply did not know the information. Action: create a one-pager or Anki cards for the topic
  • Application error — you knew the facts but failed to apply them correctly to the scenario. Action: practise more scenario-based questions on this topic; review the clinical algorithm
  • Reading error — you misread the question (missed a key word such as "most likely," "best initial," or "most appropriate immediate action"). Action: practise careful question reading; flag as a technique issue
  • Distractor trap — you were drawn to a plausible-sounding distractor that contained a subtle error. Action: review the explanation carefully; understand why the distractor was wrong
  • Careless error — you knew the answer but made an error through haste or inattention. Action: slow down; review the question before confirming your answer

Classifying errors by root cause allows you to identify patterns: if 60% of your errors are knowledge gaps in respiratory medicine, you need more targeted revision in that area. If 40% of your errors are reading errors, the problem is technique rather than knowledge.

Weekly Error Log Reviews

Once per week, review your error log from the previous seven days. For each error, cover the answer column and try to recall the correct answer from memory. Errors you can now answer correctly get a tick; errors you are still getting wrong get flagged for further action (convert to a new Anki card, re-read the CKS summary, discuss with a study partner). This weekly review is itself a spaced repetition exercise and a powerful way of consolidating learning from your question bank sessions.

Converting Errors to Anki Cards

For knowledge gap errors, the most efficient corrective action is to convert the learning point into an Anki card immediately after reviewing the explanation. Do not create a long written note — create a focused, testable card. For example, if you get a question about the management of hypertension in pregnancy wrong, create a card: front — "First-line antihypertensive in pregnancy? (excluding pre-eclampsia)"; back — "Labetalol (oral); alternatives: nifedipine, methyldopa. Avoid ACE inhibitors, ARBs, thiazides." This card will appear for review according to the Anki algorithm, ensuring the learning point sticks.

Organising Revision Around the MLA Content Map

Using the Content Map as a Checklist

The MLA Content Map is the official document defining what can be tested in the UKMLA. It is publicly available on the Medical Schools Council website and is organised by clinical specialty and by professional and ethical theme. Using the Content Map as a revision checklist is the most systematic way to ensure comprehensive coverage.

Print or download the Content Map and work through each item systematically. For each condition or topic, rate your confidence on a three-point scale:

  • Red — significant knowledge gap; need active revision and question practice
  • Amber — moderate confidence; needs reinforcement through questions and spaced repetition
  • Green — confident; maintain through periodic spaced repetition reviews

Review and update this RAG (Red-Amber-Green) rating at regular intervals throughout preparation. As your revision progresses, the number of red items should decrease and the proportion of green items should increase. If items remain consistently red despite revision, they need a different approach: read the CKS summary, create detailed Anki cards, attempt more questions, or seek clinical exposure.

Colour-Coding Your Notes

Colour-coding works best when it carries semantic meaning rather than being purely aesthetic. A consistent colour scheme across all your notes makes retrieval faster. A widely used approach: red for absolute contraindications and red flags; orange for relative contraindications and cautions; green for first-line treatments; blue for investigations; purple for complications. Applied consistently across your one-pagers and comparison tables, this coding system creates visual cues that accelerate recognition during exam practice.

Digital vs Paper Revision Notes

The debate between digital and paper notes is genuine, and the evidence is nuanced. Several studies have found that handwriting notes (even in summarised form) produces better conceptual retention than typing, because the slower speed of handwriting forces more active selection and processing. For initial note creation — especially for complex topics requiring deep understanding — handwriting may be superior. For reference, searchability, and portability, digital notes have clear advantages.

A practical hybrid approach: use handwritten notes for creating one-pagers on difficult topics, and digital tools (Anki, Notion, OneNote, or a Google Doc) for your error log, Content Map checklist, and revision schedule. Avoid the trap of over-engineering your note organisation system — a perfectly structured digital note system that takes hours to maintain is a displacement activity, not revision.

Timing of Note-Making in Your Revision Calendar

When you make notes matters as much as how you make them. The optimal sequence is:

  1. Early preparation (months 4–6 before the AKT) — Focus on building foundational knowledge through reading, initial question bank practice, and creating your Content Map RAG rating. Create one-pagers for topics identified as knowledge gaps through early question practice.
  2. Mid preparation (months 2–4 before the AKT) — Shift the majority of time to question bank practice. Use your error log actively. Create Anki cards from errors. Begin spaced repetition review of early-created notes and cards. Start interleaving topics rather than blocking by specialty.
  3. Late preparation (6–8 weeks before the AKT) — Primarily question practice and Anki review. Minimise creation of new notes — at this stage, your time is better spent retrieving and refining existing knowledge than creating new material. Reserve the GMC sample questions and MSC practice exam for the final 2–4 weeks as a calibration tool.

CPSA-Specific Revision Strategies

The CPSA requires a different revision approach from the AKT because it tests applied clinical skills rather than pure knowledge recall. For CPSA-specific preparation:

  • Structured clinical examinations — practise clinical skills (history-taking, examination, procedural skills, communication scenarios) in simulated conditions with feedback. Your medical school will provide CPSA preparation time; use it fully.
  • Checklist-based revision — CPSA stations are marked using structured mark sheets. Knowing what examiners are looking for (gathering information, clinical reasoning, communication skills, professionalism) allows you to deliberately practise hitting the assessment criteria.
  • Video resources and clinical skills platforms — platforms such as Quesmed CPSA modules, Geeky Medics (for structured OSCE approaches), and your own medical school's clinical skills resources are valuable for procedural and communication skills stations.
  • ECG and CXR interpretation — this is tested in both the AKT (as image-based questions) and the CPSA. Systematic practice with labelled ECG and CXR images using Anki image occlusion cards is particularly effective.

Study Groups, Peer Teaching, and Social Accountability

Teaching is one of the most powerful consolidation strategies available. When you explain a topic to someone else, you are forced to organise your understanding, identify gaps in your explanation, and resolve inconsistencies. This is the protégé effect — you learn better when you teach. UKMLA study groups can be highly productive if they are structured: each session, one member teaches a topic from the Content Map to the group, others ask questions, and the group discusses errors from the week's question bank practice. This is far more productive than simply reading notes aloud to each other.

Social accountability is also a genuine motivational tool. Committing to a study partner that you will complete a set number of question bank questions or review a specific set of Anki cards by a specific date creates external accountability that supplements internal motivation, particularly in the low-energy periods of long revision campaigns.

Managing Note Quality Over Quantity

A critical principle of effective UKMLA note-making is that you should have fewer, higher-quality notes rather than more, lower-quality ones. Many candidates accumulate hundreds of pages of notes that they never review. The accumulation of material creates an illusion of progress without producing learning. A targeted set of 50–70 one-page summaries for high-yield conditions, a well-maintained Anki deck of 500–800 personally created cards, a systematic error log, and a completed RAG-rated Content Map is more valuable than three folders of typed notes that have never been reviewed.

Before creating any new note or summary, ask yourself: will I review this again? If the honest answer is no, do not create it. Instead, create an Anki card on the specific learning point and move on to the next question.

Frequently Asked Questions About UKMLA Revision Notes and Strategy

How many questions should I do each day in the lead-up to the AKT?

There is no universally correct answer, but a commonly recommended target for the main preparation phase (2–4 months before the exam) is 40–60 questions per day, reviewed carefully with explanations — not done at speed without reading the explanations. Quality of review matters more than raw question count. Doing 100 questions per day and skimming the explanations produces far less learning than doing 40 questions and reading each explanation carefully, cross-referencing errors against CKS, and creating Anki cards for knowledge gaps. In the final 2–4 weeks, many candidates increase to 80–100 questions per day in timed, mixed conditions to build exam stamina and pace.

Should I use Anki from the start or wait until I have a knowledge base?

You do not need a pre-existing knowledge base to benefit from Anki — the cards themselves build the knowledge base. However, the most effective approach is to integrate Anki into your question bank workflow from the start of preparation: do questions, identify errors, create cards for knowledge gaps. Starting Anki from day one of preparation maximises the time the spaced repetition algorithm has to work before the exam. Trying to build a large Anki deck in the final four weeks before the exam is ineffective because the algorithm has insufficient time to space reviews appropriately.

Is it worth buying more than one question bank?

For most candidates, one primary question bank used thoroughly is better than two question banks used superficially. The primary question bank should be whichever of Passmedicine or Quesmed you find most comfortable and well-aligned to your learning style. A second question bank (such as BMJ OnExamination or UWorld) can be valuable if you complete your primary bank and want additional practice with different question styles, or if your primary bank has weak coverage in specific areas. Do not switch between question banks repeatedly — pick one, commit to it, and use your error log to track performance.

How do I revise for the CPSA differently from the AKT?

The AKT and CPSA test overlapping but distinct skills. AKT preparation focuses on knowledge retrieval through question bank practice. CPSA preparation requires clinical skills practice in simulated settings: structured history-taking with role players, systematic examination practice (ideally filmed for feedback), communication skills scenarios (breaking bad news, gaining consent, managing disagreement), and procedural skills practice (venepuncture, cannulation, ECG recording, prescribing). The knowledge underpinning the CPSA is the same knowledge tested in the AKT, so integrated AKT preparation supports CPSA performance — but knowing the management of heart failure does not automatically mean you can conduct a structured cardiovascular examination and communicate findings to a patient. Allocate specific time for clinical skills simulation separate from question bank revision.

What is the most common mistake candidates make with UKMLA notes?

The most common mistake is spending the majority of revision time making notes rather than using notes. Notes are a tool for review, not a product in their own right. Candidates who spend months creating beautifully formatted summaries, colour-coded flowcharts, and comprehensive written resources — but who do not spend the majority of their preparation time answering questions and testing themselves — consistently underperform relative to their preparation effort. The exam tests retrieval, not recognition. Build a lean, targeted set of notes and then put them down and do questions.

Conclusion: Build a System, Not Just a Study Session

The most important insight in UKMLA revision strategy is that effective preparation is a system, not a series of individual study sessions. A system has defined inputs (question bank sessions, Anki reviews, error log updates), defined processes (retrieval practice, spaced repetition, elaboration), and defined outputs (measured progress on the Content Map RAG checklist, declining error rates, improving question bank scores). When your system is running well, each component feeds into the others: question bank errors generate Anki cards, Anki reviews reinforce weak areas identified in the error log, error log patterns direct targeted question bank sessions, and Content Map ratings track overall progress.

Candidates who build and maintain this kind of systematic approach — even imperfectly — will consistently outperform those who rely on inspiration, last-minute cramming, or passive revision. Start early, prioritise active over passive study, track your errors relentlessly, and use spaced repetition to make sure everything you learn stays learned.

For guidance on how to structure your overall UKMLA preparation timeline, visit our UKMLA Preparation Hub. To understand exactly what the exam tests and how it is structured, explore our detailed guide to the AKT format and the CPSA format, and review the full UKMLA syllabus breakdown to ensure your revision covers every area of the MLA Content Map.
