post_title: Gastroenterology and Hepatology in the UKMLA AKT
post_excerpt: GI presentations are a core Content Map domain. Here is a focused guide to the conditions, investigations, and management decisions most likely to appear in the AKT.
seo_title: Gastroenterology in the UKMLA AKT: High-Yield Revision Guide
seo_description: GI presentations are a core UKMLA domain. Revise abdominal pain, GI bleeding, jaundice, IBD, and liver disease with this focused AKT revision guide.
html_content:
Gastroenterology UKMLA AKT questions span the full breadth of abdominal medicine — from the acutely unwell patient with upper GI bleeding to the outpatient presenting with altered bowel habit and weight loss. This system is consistently high-yield, and mastering it requires fluency in clinical scoring tools, investigation algorithms, and the major guidelines from NICE and the British Society of Gastroenterology. Whether you are revising inflammatory bowel disease, acute pancreatitis, jaundice workup, or colorectal cancer screening, this guide covers the data tables and clinical frameworks that appear most frequently in gastroenterology UKMLA AKT questions.

Acute Abdominal Pain: Differential Diagnosis by Location

Localising abdominal pain is one of the most tested gastroenterology UKMLA AKT skills. The differential shifts significantly with the quadrant involved, and exam questions frequently present a clinical vignette requiring you to identify the most likely diagnosis based on location, character, and associated features.

RegionKey DifferentialsDistinguishing Features
Right upper quadrant (RUQ)Biliary colic, cholecystitis, cholangitis, hepatitis, Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndromeMurphy's sign (cholecystitis); Charcot's triad (cholangitis: RUQ pain, fever, jaundice); raised LFTs
Left upper quadrant (LUQ)Splenic infarct/rupture, gastric ulcer, pancreatitis (tail), left lower lobe pneumoniaTrauma history; radiation to shoulder; Kehr's sign
EpigastricPeptic ulcer disease, GORD, pancreatitis, MI (inferior), aortic aneurysmRelieved by antacids (PUD); radiation to back (pancreatitis, AAA); raised lipase/amylase
PeriumbilicalEarly appendicitis, small bowel obstruction, mesenteric ischaemia, AAAMigrates to RIF (appendicitis); colicky + vomiting + distension (obstruction)
Right iliac fossa (RIF)Appendicitis, ovarian pathology, Crohn's (terminal ileum), ectopic pregnancy, psoas abscessRebound, guarding, Rovsing's sign (appendicitis); positive hCG (ectopic)
Left iliac fossa (LIF)Diverticulitis, sigmoid volvulus, ovarian pathology, UC, constipation, ectopicFever + LIF pain (diverticulitis); absolute constipation + distension (volvulus)
SuprapubicUTI, cystitis, urinary retention, PID, bladder pathology, fibroidsDysuria/frequency (UTI); cervical excitation (PID); distended bladder (retention)
Generalised/diffusePeritonitis, IBD flare, mesenteric ischaemia, bowel obstruction, DKABoard-like rigidity (peritonitis); absent bowel sounds; metabolic acidosis

GI Bleeding: Upper vs Lower, Scoring, and 2WW Referral

Upper GI Bleeding: Glasgow-Blatchford Score

Upper GI bleeding presents with haematemesis (fresh blood or coffee-ground vomiting) or melaena (black, tarry, malodorous stool). The Glasgow-Blatchford score (GBS) is used pre-endoscopy to risk-stratify patients and identify those who can be managed as outpatients (GBS 0 = low risk, can be discharged for outpatient endoscopy within 24 hours).

VariableScore
BUN ≥6.5 to <8 mmol/L2
BUN ≥8 to <10 mmol/L3
BUN ≥10 to <25 mmol/L4
BUN ≥25 mmol/L6
Haemoglobin 120–129 g/L (male)1
Haemoglobin 100–119 g/L (male)3
Haemoglobin <100 g/L (male)6
SBP 100–109 mmHg1
SBP 90–99 mmHg2
SBP <90 mmHg3
HR ≥100 bpm1
Melaena present1
Syncope2
Liver disease2
Heart failure2

GBS 0 = low risk (outpatient); GBS ≥1 = hospital admission. Upper GI endoscopy (OGD) should be performed within 24 hours in non-variceal bleed, and immediately (within 1 hour) in suspected variceal bleed with haemodynamic instability. Terlipressin and prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone) are given before endoscopy in variceal bleeding; band ligation is the endoscopic treatment of choice for oesophageal varices.

Lower GI Bleeding

Bright red rectal bleeding (haematochezia) is usually lower GI in origin. Differentials vary by age: young patients — haemorrhoids, fissure, IBD, infective colitis; middle-aged — polyps, IBD, diverticular disease; older — diverticular disease, colorectal cancer, ischaemic colitis, angiodysplasia. Two-week-wait (2WW) colorectal cancer referral: rectal bleeding with no anal symptoms in age ≥40; change in bowel habit to looser/more frequent stools in age ≥60; iron deficiency anaemia in men or post-menopausal women; palpable right-sided mass; rectal mass on PR examination; raised CEA. Bowel cancer screening programme (BCSP): faecal immunochemical testing (FIT) offered every two years from age 50–74 in England (now extended toward 45); FIT ≥10 micrograms haemoglobin/g faeces triggers colonoscopy referral.

Jaundice Investigation Algorithm

Jaundice (bilirubin >35 micromol/L; visible sclerae >~50 micromol/L) is classified by mechanism: pre-hepatic, hepatic, or post-hepatic (obstructive). LFT patterns are the key discriminator.

TypeBilirubinALPALT/ASTCauses
Pre-hepatic (unconjugated)Raised (unconjugated)NormalNormalHaemolysis (sickle cell, G6PD, autoimmune), Gilbert's syndrome
Hepatocellular (hepatic)Raised (mixed/conjugated)Normal or mildly raisedMarkedly raised (>1000 in viral/drug)Viral hepatitis A/B/C, alcohol, drug-induced, autoimmune hepatitis, Wilson's, haemochromatosis
Cholestatic (post-hepatic)Raised (conjugated)Markedly raisedNormal or mildly raisedGallstones (choledocholithiasis), pancreatic cancer, cholangiocarcinoma, PSC, PBC, drug-induced

Investigation sequence for jaundice: LFTs, FBC, clotting (PT), bilirubin fractions, hepatitis serology (A IgM, HBsAg, HCV Ab, EBV, CMV), ANA, ANCA, smooth muscle Ab (autoimmune hepatitis), immunoglobulins, caeruloplasmin (Wilson's), ferritin/transferrin saturation (haemochromatosis), USS abdomen (biliary dilatation = obstruction), MRCP (biliary tree imaging), ERCP (therapeutic — stenting, stone removal), CT abdomen (malignancy staging).

Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn's vs UC

IBD management follows the 2025 BSG guidelines, which expanded biologic treatment options and emphasised early use of targeted therapies for moderate-severe disease. Differentiating Crohn's disease from ulcerative colitis remains a classic gastroenterology UKMLA AKT question.

FeatureCrohn's DiseaseUlcerative Colitis
LocationAnywhere from mouth to anus; most common in terminal ileum and colonRectum, extending proximally; continuous; limited to colon
Skip lesionsYesNo (continuous inflammation)
Depth of inflammationTransmural (full thickness)Mucosal only
HistologyNon-caseating granulomas, cobblestone mucosa, rose-thorn ulcersCrypt abscesses, pseudopolyps, goblet cell depletion
SymptomsRIF pain, diarrhoea (may be non-bloody), weight loss, mouth ulcers, perianal diseaseBloody diarrhoea, urgency, tenesmus, mucus PR
ComplicationsStrictures, fistulae, abscesses, malabsorption, B12 deficiencyToxic megacolon, colorectal cancer (long-standing pan-colitis), primary sclerosing cholangitis
Extra-intestinal manifestationsErythema nodosum, pyoderma gangrenosum, episcleritis, uveitis, arthropathy, ankylosing spondylitisSame, plus PSC more closely associated with UC
SurgeryNot curative (recurrence); minimise bowel resectionCurative total colectomy + ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA)
Drug treatmentSteroids (induction), azathioprine/6-MP (maintenance), methotrexate, anti-TNF (infliximab, adalimumab), vedolizumab, ustekinumab, JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, upadacitinib)Aminosalicylates (mesalazine) for mild-moderate UC; biologics for moderate-severe

Acute Pancreatitis: Severity Scoring and Management

Acute pancreatitis most commonly presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea and vomiting. Diagnosis requires two of three: characteristic pain, serum lipase/amylase >3Ã— upper limit of normal, and characteristic imaging (CT). The commonest causes are gallstones (~45%) and alcohol (~35%) — remembered by GET SMASHED (Gallstones, Ethanol, Trauma, Steroids, Mumps, Autoimmune, Scorpion venom, Hyperlipidaemia/Hypercalcaemia, ERCP, Drugs).

Severity is assessed using the Glasgow (Imrie) score — 8 criteria, score ≥3 indicates severe pancreatitis requiring HDU/ITU: PaO2 <8 kPa, albumin <32 g/L, neutrophil count >15 Ã—10â¹/L, calcium <2 mmol/L, raised urea >16 mmol/L, AST/ALT >200 U/L, blood glucose >10 mmol/L, LDH >600 U/L. CT severity index (CTSI) is used 48–72 hours into admission to identify necrosis. CRP >150 at 48 hours also predicts severe disease. Management: aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (crystalloid), analgesia (opioids), nil by mouth initially transitioning to early enteral nutrition (nasojejunal tube preferred to TPN), thromboprophylaxis, monitoring in HDU if severe. ERCP and sphincterotomy within 72 hours if biliary pancreatitis with obstructive jaundice or cholangitis. Infected pancreatic necrosis: IV antibiotics, then necrosectomy (endoscopic or surgical).

H. pylori: Testing and Eradication

Helicobacter pylori causes peptic ulcer disease, chronic gastritis, MALT lymphoma, and is a risk factor for gastric cancer. Testing: urea breath test (gold standard non-invasive; must stop PPIs 2 weeks before), stool antigen test (less commonly used), CLO test (rapid urease test at endoscopy — invasive). Serology is not recommended for diagnosis of active infection. First-line eradication (NICE): 7-day triple therapy — full-dose PPI (e.g. omeprazole 20 mg BD) + amoxicillin 1 g BD + clarithromycin 500 mg BD. Second-line (if clarithromycin resistance suspected): PPI + amoxicillin + metronidazole 400 mg BD; or PPI + bismuth + tetracycline + metronidazole (quadruple therapy). Confirm eradication with urea breath test ≥4 weeks after completing eradication therapy. PPIs must be stopped 2 weeks before testing.

IBS, Coeliac Disease, and GORD

Irritable Bowel Syndrome

IBS is diagnosed using Rome IV criteria: recurrent abdominal pain at least 1 day per week in the last 3 months, associated with two or more of: related to defecation; associated with change in stool frequency; associated with change in stool form. Red flags that exclude IBS: age >60, rectal bleeding, unintentional weight loss, family history CRC/IBD, anaemia, raised CRP/faecal calprotectin, palpable mass. NICE management: lifestyle advice (regular meals, adequate fluid, reduce insoluble fibre); antispasmodics (mebeverine, hyoscine) for cramps; loperamide for diarrhoea-predominant; laxatives for constipation-predominant; low FODMAP diet (refer to dietitian); TCAs (amitriptyline 5–10 mg at night) or SSRIs if refractory; CBT/hypnotherapy for psychological component. Routine colonoscopy not required if Rome IV met and no red flags — test with FBC, CRP, coeliac serology, and faecal calprotectin to exclude organic disease.

Coeliac Disease

Coeliac disease is an autoimmune enteropathy triggered by gluten (wheat, rye, barley) causing villous atrophy in the small bowel, leading to malabsorption. Presentations: diarrhoea, weight loss, iron deficiency anaemia (most common presentation in adults), folate deficiency, osteoporosis, fatigue, dermatitis herpetiformis (itchy blistering rash on extensor surfaces). Investigation: tissue transglutaminase antibody (TTG IgA) is first-line serology — check total IgA to exclude IgA deficiency (use IgG-based tests if deficient). Positive serology: refer for upper GI endoscopy and duodenal biopsy (gold standard: Marsh classification III — subtotal/total villous atrophy). Management: lifelong strict gluten-free diet. Annual monitoring: FBC, B12, folate, iron, calcium, vitamin D. Screen for complications: DEXA scan, risk of T-cell lymphoma (refractory coeliac).

GORD Management (NICE Stepwise)

Step 1: lifestyle advice (lose weight, elevate head of bed, avoid trigger foods, small meals, avoid lying down after eating). Step 2: antacids or alginate for mild intermittent symptoms. Step 3: full-dose PPI (omeprazole 20 mg, lansoprazole 30 mg) for 4–8 weeks. Step 4: if persisting, H2RA (ranitidine/famotidine) can be added or PPI dose doubled. Investigate with OGD if red flags (dysphagia, odynophagia, unexplained weight loss, persistent vomiting, age >55 with new onset dyspepsia, iron deficiency anaemia). Barrett's oesophagus (metaplasia of squamous to columnar epithelium) is a premalignant complication of chronic GORD — surveillance OGD is recommended. Oesophageal adenocarcinoma risk is increased in Barrett's.

Liver Disease: Hepatitis B, C, Alcohol, and NAFLD

Hepatitis B Serology

HBsAg = surface antigen (indicates current infection — acute or chronic). Anti-HBs = surface antibody (indicates immunity — from vaccination or resolved infection). HBeAg = envelope antigen (high replication, high infectivity). Anti-HBc IgM = acute infection. Anti-HBc IgG = past exposure or chronic carrier. HBV DNA quantification guides treatment. Treatment: entecavir or tenofovir (antivirals) in chronic HBV with active replication or significant fibrosis. Screen contacts and offer vaccination.

Hepatitis C

HCV antibody testing (anti-HCV) is first-line; if positive, HCV RNA confirms viraemia (active infection). Genotyping guides treatment duration. Treatment: direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) — sofosbuvir-based regimens; cure rates >95%. All diagnosed patients with chronic HCV should be offered treatment. Blood-borne virus; test PWID, MSM, healthcare workers with needlestick, prison population.

Alcohol and Liver Disease

AUDIT-C: three-item screening (how often do you drink; how many units per session; how often do you have 6+ units). Score ≥5 in men or ≥4 in women suggests hazardous drinking. Safe limits: up to 14 units/week (both sexes), spread over 3+ days, 2 alcohol-free days/week. Alcoholic liver disease spectrum: fatty liver (reversible with abstinence) → alcoholic hepatitis (jaundice, AST:ALT ratio >2:1, raised GGT; Maddrey's discriminant function ≥32 indicates severe disease — consider prednisolone) → cirrhosis. Complications of cirrhosis: ascites (spironolactone Â± furosemide; large-volume paracentesis + albumin replacement), spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP — prophylaxis with norfloxacin; treat with ceftriaxone), hepatic encephalopathy (lactulose, rifaximin), variceal bleeding, hepatorenal syndrome, hepatocellular carcinoma (6-monthly USS + AFP screening).

NAFLD/NASH

Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) is the most common liver disease in the UK, closely linked to obesity, type 2 diabetes, dyslipidaemia, and metabolic syndrome. NASH (non-alcoholic steatohepatitis) is the progressive inflammatory subtype that can lead to cirrhosis and HCC. Diagnosis: raised LFTs (ALT predominant), fatty liver on USS, exclude other causes, FIB-4 score or ELF test to stage fibrosis non-invasively. FibroScan (transient elastography) measures liver stiffness. Liver biopsy is gold standard for staging. Management: lifestyle (weight loss 5–10% reduces steatosis), manage cardiovascular risk factors, vitamin E (for NASH without diabetes), semaglutide showing benefit in trials; resmetirom licensed in USA 2024.

Nutritional Assessment and Colorectal Cancer Screening

MUST Score and Nutritional Support

The Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool (MUST) assesses BMI (0–2 points), unintentional weight loss (0–2 points), and acute disease effect (0 or 2 points). Score 0 = low risk (routine care); score 1 = medium risk (observe, dietary advice); score ≥2 = high risk (refer to dietitian, nutritional support). Refeeding syndrome occurs when malnourished patients receive rapid nutritional support after starvation — phosphate, potassium, and magnesium drop dramatically as anabolic processes resume. Risk factors: BMI <16, minimal intake >5 days, low electrolytes before feeding. Prevention: correct electrolytes before starting, start nutritional support slowly (50% of target on day 1), thiamine supplementation (IV Pabrinex before glucose-containing feeds), monitor electrolytes daily.

Gastrointestinal Motility Disorders and Functional GI Disease

Functional gastrointestinal disorders are diagnosed when symptoms are present without structural, biochemical, or microbiological abnormality. Beyond IBS, the UKMLA tests knowledge of gastroparesis (delayed gastric emptying, common in diabetes — nausea, vomiting, early satiety; managed with metoclopramide, domperidone, or erythromycin), functional dyspepsia (Rome IV criteria: epigastric pain or postprandial distress syndrome; managed with lifestyle, PPI, H2RA, low-dose TCA), and achalasia (failure of lower oesophageal sphincter relaxation due to loss of Auerbach's plexus — dysphagia to both solids and liquids, regurgitation, weight loss; barium swallow shows "bird's beak" appearance; manometry is gold standard; treatment: endoscopic pneumatic dilatation or Heller myotomy).

Oesophageal motility disorders include diffuse oesophageal spasm (intermittent dysphagia and chest pain, "corkscrew" oesophagus on barium swallow) and nutcracker oesophagus (high-amplitude peristaltic contractions causing chest pain). Both are diagnosed on high-resolution manometry. Management: calcium channel blockers, long-acting nitrates, PPI if GORD component.

Constipation assessment and management: chronic constipation is defined as fewer than three bowel movements per week, straining, hard stools, or sensation of incomplete evacuation. Secondary causes to exclude: hypothyroidism, hypercalcaemia, opioids, TCAs, calcium channel blockers, Parkinson's, spinal cord disease, colon cancer. Investigations: thyroid function, calcium, FBC, colonoscopy if red flags. Management: dietary fibre (20–30 g/day), adequate fluid intake (2 L/day), bulk-forming laxatives (ispaghula husk) first-line, osmotic laxatives (lactulose, macrogol), stimulant laxatives (senna, bisacodyl) for resistant constipation, suppositories or enemas for distal constipation, prucalopride (5HT4 agonist) for women with chronic constipation not responding to laxatives. Avoid phosphate enemas in renal failure (risk of hyperphosphataemia).

Upper GI Cancer: Oesophageal and Gastric Cancer

Oesophageal cancer: two main types — squamous cell carcinoma (middle and upper third, associated with smoking, alcohol, achalasia, hot drinks) and adenocarcinoma (lower third and gastro-oesophageal junction, associated with GORD, Barrett's oesophagus, obesity). Presentation: progressive dysphagia (first to solids, then liquids), weight loss, odynophagia, hoarseness (RLN involvement). Investigation: OGD with biopsy (diagnostic); CT thorax/abdomen/pelvis (staging); endoscopic USS (local staging); PET-CT (distant metastases). Treatment: early stage — surgery (Ivor-Lewis oesophagectomy or transhiatal); locally advanced — neoadjuvant chemotherapy (FLOT or CROSS protocol — chemoradiotherapy) then surgery; metastatic — palliative chemotherapy (FOLFOX, ECF), immunotherapy (nivolumab/pembrolizumab for PD-L1 positive). Palliative: oesophageal stent for dysphagia; PEG tube if unable to swallow.

Gastric cancer: presentation is often late — epigastric pain, weight loss, early satiety, anaemia, dysphagia, vomiting. Risk factors: H. pylori infection (most important modifiable risk), smoking, salt-preserved foods, previous gastric surgery, blood group A, family history, Lynch syndrome, hereditary diffuse gastric cancer (CDH1 mutation). Virchow's node (left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy), Sister Mary Joseph nodule (periumbilical metastasis), Krukenberg tumour (ovarian metastasis), and Irish node (left axillary) are paraneoplastic signs of advanced disease. Two-week-wait: age 55 or over with weight loss AND dysphagia, nausea/vomiting, reflux, dyspepsia, or upper abdominal pain. Also 2WW for haematemesis at any age. Treatment: endoscopic mucosal resection for T1a; surgery (gastrectomy with D2 lymphadenectomy); perioperative chemotherapy (FLOT); HER2-positive: add trastuzumab.

Gastrointestinal Investigations: Endoscopy, Colonoscopy, and ERCP

Upper GI endoscopy (OGD — oesophago-gastro-duodenoscopy): visualises oesophagus, stomach, and duodenum to D2. Indications: dysphagia, haematemesis/melaena, dyspepsia with red flags, suspected coeliac (duodenal biopsy), Barrett's surveillance, PUD follow-up. Complications: perforation (<0.01%), bleeding (rare), aspiration. Capsule endoscopy: small bowel imaging using a swallowed camera; used when OGD and colonoscopy are negative but small bowel pathology suspected (e.g. obscure GI bleeding, Crohn's of small bowel); contraindicated in suspected stricture (risk of capsule retention). Colonoscopy: visualises entire colon and terminal ileum; gold standard for colorectal cancer diagnosis, polyp removal, IBD assessment. Complications: perforation (~1:1000), bleeding, missed lesions (adenoma miss rate 6–12%). Bowel preparation is required — patients fast from midnight and take sodium picosulphate or polyethylene glycol solution. ERCP (endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography): therapeutic procedure to access biliary and pancreatic ducts via a duodenoscope; used for choledocholithiasis (stone removal), biliary stricture (stenting), cholangitis (drainage), sphincterotomy. Complications: post-ERCP pancreatitis (3–5%), cholangitis, perforation, bleeding. MRCP (magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography) is a non-invasive diagnostic alternative — no radiation, no risk of pancreatitis; used when therapeutic intervention not needed or as a pre-ERCP roadmap.

Frequently Asked Questions About Gastroenterology in the UKMLA AKT

What is the difference between Crohn's and UC on histology?

Crohn's disease shows transmural inflammation affecting all layers of the bowel wall, with non-caseating granulomas, cobblestone mucosal appearance, and skip lesions (areas of normal bowel between affected segments). Ulcerative colitis shows mucosal and submucosal inflammation only, with crypt abscesses, goblet cell depletion, and pseudopolyps. These are continuous changes starting from the rectum and extending proximally without skip areas. Biopsy findings guide treatment decisions in the AKT — if granulomas are described, think Crohn's.

How do I remember the Glasgow severity score for pancreatitis?

Use the mnemonic PANCREAS: PaO2 <8 kPa, Age >55, Neutrophils >15, Calcium <2 mmol/L, Raised urea >16, Enzymes (AST >200, LDH >600), Albumin <32, Sugar (glucose) >10 mmol/L. A score of 3 or more within 48 hours of admission indicates severe acute pancreatitis requiring HDU-level monitoring. Note that the Glasgow score uses different variables from the Ranson criteria (which includes WBC, not used in Glasgow) — the AKT typically tests Glasgow in UK clinical contexts.

When should I refer for 2WW colorectal cancer?

NICE NG12 2WW criteria for colorectal cancer: age ≥40 with unexplained weight loss + abdominal pain; age ≥50 with unexplained rectal bleeding; age ≥60 with IDA or change in bowel habit; positive FIT test on BCSP; palpable rectal or right-sided abdominal mass at any age; PR examination suggesting rectal cancer. Also consider 2WW if age ≥50 with rectal bleeding AND any of: abdominal pain, weight loss, change in bowel habit, or IDA. The FIT threshold of 10 micrograms/g used in BCSP is different from the lower thresholds being evaluated for symptomatic patients in some pathways.

What are the NICE criteria for H. pylori testing in dyspepsia?

NICE recommends a test-and-treat strategy for uninvestigated dyspepsia in patients under 55 without alarm features: test for H. pylori (urea breath test or stool antigen test) and treat if positive before considering PPI therapy. If symptoms persist despite eradication, consider OGD. In patients ≥55 with new-onset dyspepsia, or those with red flag symptoms (dysphagia, weight loss, iron deficiency anaemia, persistent vomiting, epigastric mass), direct OGD referral is recommended. PPIs must be stopped at least 2 weeks before urea breath testing to avoid false negative results.

What is the significance of faecal calprotectin in UKMLA questions?

Faecal calprotectin is a non-invasive inflammatory marker released by neutrophils in the bowel mucosa. Elevated calprotectin (>50–200 micrograms/g depending on the local threshold) indicates intestinal inflammation and favours IBD over IBS. It is used in primary care to differentiate between IBS (low calprotectin) and IBD or bowel cancer (elevated calprotectin), guiding referral for colonoscopy. Normal calprotectin in a patient with IBS-type symptoms supports a diagnosis of functional bowel disorder and avoids unnecessary endoscopy. In UKMLA questions, calprotectin is often the discriminating investigation that determines whether to refer for colonoscopy or manage as IBS.

Conclusion

Gastroenterology UKMLA AKT questions reward candidates who know their scoring tools (Glasgow-Blatchford, CURB-65 equivalents for pancreatitis, MUST), can interpret LFT patterns, and know the management algorithms for high-frequency presentations like IBD, GI bleeding, jaundice, and pancreatitis. Ensure you know the 2WW referral thresholds for GI cancers, the H. pylori test-and-treat pathway, and the colorectal cancer screening programme. Cross-reference with the UKMLA syllabus and see preparation resources for recommended question banks covering gastroenterology scenarios.

post_title: Neurology in the UKMLA: High-Yield Topics and Revision Tips
post_excerpt: Neurological presentations are a significant UKMLA domain. This guide covers stroke, epilepsy, headache, and the key examination and investigation skills.
seo_title: Neurology in the UKMLA: High-Yield Topics and Revision Tips
seo_description: Neurological presentations are a core UKMLA domain. Our guide covers stroke, epilepsy, headache, dementia, and the clinical skills neurological stations require.
html_content:
Neurology UKMLA questions reward pattern recognition and a systematic approach to clinical presentation. Representing approximately 6–9% of AKT items, neurology spans acute emergencies such as stroke and status epilepticus through to chronic disease management in epilepsy, Parkinson's disease, and dementia. The NICE NG128 stroke guideline, NICE NG217 epilepsy guideline (updated January 2025), and the DVLA guidance on neurological conditions are particularly high-yield. This guide covers every major neurology UKMLA topic with comparison tables, management algorithms, and FAQs to consolidate your revision.

Stroke and TIA: Neurology UKMLA Core Knowledge

Stroke is a medical emergency. Neurology UKMLA questions on stroke test your ability to use assessment tools, understand the thrombolysis pathway, and initiate correct secondary prevention. FAST (Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 999) is the public-facing tool; ROSIER (Recognition of Stroke in the Emergency Room) is the validated clinical tool used in A&E, scoring for asymmetric facial/arm/leg weakness, speech disturbance, and visual field defect, with loss of consciousness and seizure activity subtracting one point each.

Stroke Management Timeline

Time WindowInterventionKey Criteria
0–4.5 hours (onset to treatment)IV thrombolysis (alteplase 0.9 mg/kg, max 90 mg)Ischaemic stroke confirmed; no haemorrhage on CT; no contraindications (recent surgery, anticoagulation, BP >185/110, prior ICH)
0–6 hours (large vessel occlusion)Mechanical thrombectomyMCA/ICA occlusion; accessible vessel; good pre-stroke function
6–24 hours (selected patients)Thrombectomy (wake-up stroke / DAWN/DEFUSE criteria)CT perfusion showing viable penumbra; good functional baseline
Within 24 hoursAspirin 300 mg loading doseAfter haemorrhage excluded on CT; do NOT give before CT
Within 24 hoursAdmit to hyper-acute stroke unit (HASU)All suspected stroke; swallowing screen before oral medication/food
24 hours to 2 weeksSwitch to clopidogrel 75 mg (or aspirin 75 mg + dipyridamole MR 200 mg BD) after initial aspirinSecondary prevention; TIA also requires antiplatelet if no AF
2 weeks post-TIA / after stroke if AFAnticoagulation (DOAC preferred: apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, dabigatran)AF-related stroke/TIA; DOAC preferred over warfarin

TIA vs Minor Stroke

TIA: focal neurological deficit lasting <24 hours with no infarct on diffusion-weighted MRI. Minor stroke: deficit resolves or nearly resolves but DWI shows infarct. Both require urgent assessment. ABCD2 score: Age ≥60 (1), BP ≥140/90 (1), Clinical features — unilateral weakness (2) or speech disturbance without weakness (1), Duration — 60+ minutes (2) or 10–59 minutes (1), Diabetes (1). ABCD2 ≥4 = high risk — specialist assessment within 24 hours. All TIA patients should receive dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 300 mg + clopidogrel 300 mg loading) immediately if no haemorrhage and no indication for anticoagulation, continued for 21 days then clopidogrel alone. Carotid imaging (carotid Doppler/CTA) within 24 hours if anterior circulation TIA — carotid endarterectomy if symptomatic stenosis 50–99%.

Haemorrhagic Stroke

Subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH): sudden-onset thunderclap headache, "worst headache of my life," reaching maximum intensity within seconds. Causes: ruptured berry aneurysm (80%), AVM, trauma. CT head (non-contrast): hyperdensity in basal cisterns. CT may miss 2–5% of SAH within 6 hours — LP at 12 hours to detect xanthochromia (yellow discolouration of CSF from haemoglobin degradation). Management: neurosurgical referral, nimodipine (calcium channel blocker — reduces vasospasm), neurovascular imaging (CTA or catheter angiography), aneurysm coiling (preferred) or clipping. Intracerebral haemorrhage (ICH): lenticulostriate vessel rupture — hypertension is the most common cause. Management: reverse anticoagulation (PCC for warfarin; idarucizumab for dabigatran; andexanet alfa for factor Xa inhibitors), BP control (target <140 mmHg within 1 hour), neurosurgical input for posterior fossa haemorrhage or hydrocephalus.

Epilepsy: Classification, Drug Choice, and DVLA Rules

Epilepsy Classification

Seizure TypeFeaturesFirst-Line AED
Focal aware (simple partial)Consciousness preserved; motor, sensory, autonomic, or psychic features; auraLamotrigine, levetiracetam, carbamazepine
Focal impaired awareness (complex partial)Consciousness impaired; automatisms (lip smacking, fumbling); post-ictal confusionLamotrigine, levetiracetam, carbamazepine
Focal to bilateral tonic-clonicStarts focal then secondarily generalisesLamotrigine, levetiracetam
Generalised tonic-clonic (GTC)Loss of consciousness, tonic (stiffening) then clonic (jerking) phases, post-ictal confusion, tongue bite, incontinenceSodium valproate (men/women not of childbearing potential); lamotrigine (women of childbearing potential)
Absence seizureBrief (5–30 sec) staring episodes; no post-ictal phase; 3Hz spike-wave on EEG; childhood onsetEthosuximide (first-line); sodium valproate; lamotrigine
MyoclonicBrief, sudden muscle jerks; often on waking; juvenile myoclonic epilepsySodium valproate (avoid in WOCBP); levetiracetam; lamotrigine
Tonic / Atonic (drop attacks)Sudden tone increase or loss; falls; Lennox-Gastaut syndromeSodium valproate; lamotrigine; rufinamide

Antiepileptic Drug Side Effects and Monitoring

DrugKey Side EffectsSpecial Concerns
Sodium valproateWeight gain, hair loss, tremor, hepatotoxicity, pancreatitisPREVENT programme: highly teratogenic (10Ã— risk NTD, 30–40% developmental delay); CANNOT be used in women of childbearing potential unless enrolled in PREVENT and using effective contraception
LamotrigineRash (Stevens-Johnson risk — titrate slowly), headache, insomniaEfficacy reduced by enzyme-inducing AEDs; dose increases required in pregnancy
CarbamazepineDiplopia, ataxia, hyponatraemia (SIADH), rash, agranulocytosisEnzyme inducer (affects OCP, warfarin, other AEDs); HLA-B*1502 testing in East Asian patients (risk of SJS)
LevetiracetamIrritability, behavioural changes, depressionRenal dose adjustment; no drug interactions; safer in pregnancy (relative to valproate)
PhenytoinGingival hyperplasia, hirsutism, coarse facies, cerebellar atrophy, nystagmus, folate deficiencyZero-order kinetics (saturatable); narrow therapeutic index; enzyme inducer; not first-line
TopiramateCognitive impairment ("dopamax"), kidney stones, glaucoma, weight lossMHRA 2024: teratogenic risk; contraception required; PREVENT-equivalent programme
EthosuximideGI upset, hiccups, SLE-like reactionFirst-line for absence seizures only

Status Epilepticus Management

Convulsive status epilepticus (CSE): seizure lasting >5 minutes, or two seizures without recovery of consciousness. Management by time: 0–5 minutes: position, ABC, recovery position, O2, IV/IO access, glucose check, BM; 5–10 minutes: IV lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg (max 4 mg) — repeat once if no IV access use buccal midazolam 10 mg or rectal diazepam 10 mg; 10–20 minutes: if seizure continues — IV levetiracetam 60 mg/kg (max 4500 mg) over 10 minutes, OR IV valproate 40 mg/kg over 15 minutes, OR IV phenytoin 20 mg/kg over 20 minutes (monitor ECG); 20–30 minutes: if refractory — anaesthetic referral, RSI with propofol or thiopental, transfer to ITU for continuous EEG monitoring.

Driving Rules After Seizure (DVLA)

First unprovoked seizure: must not drive for 6 months (Group 1 — car/motorcycle). Group 2 (HGV/PSV): 5 years seizure-free. Epilepsy (two or more seizures): must be seizure-free for 12 months before driving (Group 1); or 10 years seizure-free and off AEDs for Group 2. Provoked seizure (clear structural cause identified and treated, or metabolic): DVLA may allow earlier return to driving — seek DVLA advice. Seizure only during sleep (sleep seizures for 1 year before driving Group 1 if pattern established). Patients have a legal duty to notify DVLA; doctors have a duty to advise patients — and may breach confidentiality to inform DVLA if patient refuses and continues to drive.

Headache: Red Flags, Migraine, and Cluster

Red Flags: SNOOP Criteria

Systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss, malignancy); Neurological symptoms or signs; Onset sudden (thunderclap); Older age (>50, new headache); Previous headache history change (pattern change); Postural component (worse on lying down — raised ICP); Precipitated by Valsalva (cough, sneeze — suggests posterior fossa lesion). Any red flag warrants urgent investigation: CT head (non-contrast) for thunderclap headache, followed by LP at 12 hours if CT negative. MRI brain for chronic progressive headache, postural headache, or associated signs.

Headache TypeFeaturesTreatment
MigraineUnilateral, pulsating, moderate-severe, 4–72 hours, nausea/vomiting, photophobia, phonophobia; aura in 30% (visual, sensory, speech — 20–60 min before)Acute: triptan (sumatriptan) + NSAID/paracetamol; antiemetic (metoclopramide). Prophylaxis (≥4/month): propranolol, amitriptyline, topiramate, valproate; CGRP inhibitors (erenumab, fremanezumab) for refractory
Tension-typeBilateral, pressing/tightening, mild-moderate, 30 min–7 days, no nausea, no photophobia/phonophobiaAcute: paracetamol, NSAID. Prophylaxis: amitriptyline, acupuncture, CBT
ClusterStrictly unilateral periorbital/temporal, severe, 15–180 min, 1–8/day; ipsilateral autonomic features (lacrimation, ptosis, miosis, nasal congestion) — "suicide headache"Acute: 100% O2 (15 L/min for 15–25 min) or sumatriptan SC 6 mg. Prophylaxis: verapamil (drug of choice), short-course prednisolone for cluster period
Medication overuse headache (MOH)Headache >15 days/month for >3 months; analgesic/triptan use >10–15 days/month; headache worse on wakingWithdraw offending analgesia; bridge with naproxen or prednisolone; education
Idiopathic intracranial hypertensionObese young women; daily headache worse on lying; papilloedema; pulsatile tinnitus; VI nerve palsy; normal CT; raised LP opening pressure (>25 cmH2O)Weight loss (10% body weight); acetazolamide; topiramate; LP drainage; optic nerve sheath fenestration if vision threatened

Multiple Sclerosis, MND, and Dementia

Multiple Sclerosis

MS is a chronic demyelinating disease of the CNS presenting in young adults (peak 20–40), more common in women and in those living further from the equator. Relapsing-remitting MS (RRMS) is the most common subtype. Clinical presentations: optic neuritis (unilateral visual loss, pain on eye movement, relative afferent pupillary defect), internuclear ophthalmoplegia (failure of adduction ipsilateral eye, nystagmus in abducting eye — MLF lesion), Lhermitte's phenomenon (electric shock down spine on neck flexion), trigeminal neuralgia, spastic paraparesis, bladder dysfunction, Uhthoff's phenomenon (worsening with heat). McDonald criteria 2017: evidence of demyelination disseminated in space and time — MRI showing T2 lesions in at least 2 of 4 CNS regions (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord). CSF: oligoclonal bands in >90% (IgG bands in CSF not in serum). Evoked potentials: delayed VEP in optic neuritis. Acute relapse: IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 3–5 days. Disease-modifying therapy (DMTs): interferon beta, glatiramer (first-line); natalizumab, ocrelizumab, alemtuzumab (high-efficacy).

Dementia Types Comparison

TypeKey FeaturesPathologyTreatment
Alzheimer's disease (60–70%)Insidious onset; short-term memory loss first; progressive; visuospatial difficulties late; depression commonBeta-amyloid plaques, neurofibrillary (tau) tangles; hippocampal atrophy on MRIAChEI (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) for mild-moderate; memantine for moderate-severe or if AChEI intolerant; lecanemab (amyloid antibody) licensed in US
Vascular dementia (15–20%)Stepwise deterioration; history of strokes/TIAs; executive dysfunction, gait disturbance early; emotional labilityMulti-infarct; small vessel disease; white matter changes on MRIVascular risk factor modification; aspirin; no specific dementia drug licensed but AChEI used off-label
Lewy body dementia (DLB)Fluctuating cognition; visual hallucinations (vivid, well-formed); Parkinsonism; REM sleep behaviour disorder; extreme sensitivity to antipsychoticsAlpha-synuclein Lewy bodies in cortexAChEI (rivastigmine preferred); AVOID antipsychotics (risk of severe neuroleptic sensitivity reaction — mortality risk)
Frontotemporal dementia (FTD)Young onset (<65); personality change, disinhibition, compulsive behaviour, executive dysfunction; memory relatively preserved early; language variants (progressive non-fluent aphasia, semantic dementia)TDP-43, FUS, tau protein inclusions; frontal and temporal atrophySupportive; SSRIs for behaviour; no licensed disease-modifying therapy

Delirium vs Dementia

Delirium (acute confusional state): acute onset (hours-days), fluctuating consciousness, inattention, disorganised thinking, perceptual disturbances (hallucinations), reversible cause. Dementia: chronic, insidious onset (months-years), consciousness initially preserved, progressive, not fully reversible. Delirium can be superimposed on dementia (most common scenario in elderly inpatients). 4AT score screens for delirium (alertness, cognition [AMT4], attention [months backwards], acute onset/fluctuating). Causes of delirium: PINCH ME — Pain, Infection, Nutrition, Constipation, Hydration, Medication, Environment. Management: treat underlying cause, ensure orientation, avoid sedation (except extreme agitation — low-dose haloperidol), single room, familiar faces.

Meningitis, Peripheral Neuropathies, and Bell's Palsy

Meningitis: LP Findings

TypeAppearanceCellsProteinGlucose (CSF:serum)
Bacterial meningitisTurbid/cloudyNeutrophils (>1000/mmÂ³)Raised (>1 g/L)Low (<0.5)
Viral meningitisClearLymphocytes (10–1000/mmÂ³)Normal or mildly raisedNormal
TB meningitisCloudy/xanthochromicLymphocytes (predominant)Very high (>1 g/L)Very low
SAHXanthochromic (>12h)RBCs (equal in all tubes)RaisedNormal
Cryptococcal meningitisClear (immunocompromised)LymphocytesRaisedLow

Bacterial meningitis management: if meningococcal septicaemia suspected (non-blanching petechial rash), give IV benzylpenicillin immediately (or cefotaxime if penicillin allergy). Do NOT delay antibiotics for LP. In hospital: ceftriaxone 2g IV BD; add dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg QDS for 4 days (reduce inflammation, improve outcomes especially in pneumococcal meningitis). Notifiable disease — public health notification required. Close contacts: rifampicin prophylaxis or ciprofloxacin single dose.

UMN vs LMN Facial Nerve Palsy

Bell's palsy (LMN VII palsy): all ipsilateral facial muscles weak including forehead (cannot raise eyebrow). Cause: idiopathic viral (HSV reactivation likely). Treatment: prednisolone 50 mg/day for 10 days within 72 hours of onset; eye care (lubricating drops, eye patch at night). UMN facial palsy (stroke, tumour): contralateral lower face weakness only — forehead is spared because it has bilateral cortical representation. Distinguishing forehead sparing (UMN) from forehead involvement (LMN/Bell's) is a classic UKMLA question. Other LMN VII causes: Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster of geniculate ganglion — ear pain, vesicles in ear canal, sensorineural hearing loss), parotid tumour, acoustic neuroma, sarcoidosis.

UMN vs LMN Signs

SignUMN (Corticospinal tract)LMN (Anterior horn/nerve)
ToneIncreased (spasticity)Decreased (flaccid)
PowerReduced (pyramidal distribution — extensors arm, flexors leg)Reduced (myotomal distribution)
ReflexesBrisk/hyperreflexicReduced/absent
Plantar responseUpgoing (Babinski positive)Downgoing
WastingMinimal (disuse atrophy only)Prominent (denervation)
FasciculationsAbsentPresent (lower motor neurone irritation)
CausesStroke, MS, spinal cord compression, MND (corticospinal component)Guillain-BarrÃ©, diabetic neuropathy, MND (anterior horn), disc prolapse

Spinal Cord Disease and Cranial Nerve Disorders

Spinal cord compression is a neurological emergency and a high-yield UKMLA topic, particularly in the context of metastatic malignancy. Presentation: back pain (the earliest and most common symptom), bilateral leg weakness (UMN signs), sensory level on the trunk, bladder and bowel dysfunction (urinary retention, constipation). Any patient with known cancer presenting with back pain should be treated as malignant spinal cord compression (MSCC) until proven otherwise. Management: immediate high-dose dexamethasone (16 mg IV stat); urgent MRI spine (whole spine); radiotherapy or surgical decompression depending on fitness and prognosis. Do not delay imaging — neurological outcome correlates directly with how quickly treatment is initiated.

Cauda equina syndrome: compression of the cauda equina nerve roots (below L1–L2) — most commonly by a central disc prolapse at L4/5 or L5/S1. Symptoms: bilateral leg pain/weakness, saddle anaesthesia (perianal, perineal, inner thigh), urinary retention (with overflow incontinence), faecal incontinence, loss of anal tone. This is a surgical emergency — immediate MRI spine and urgent neurosurgical referral. Any delay increases risk of permanent bladder and bowel dysfunction. Red flag features requiring same-day MRI: saddle anaesthesia, bilateral leg weakness, loss of bladder/bowel control, signs in two or more nerve root levels.

Cranial nerve palsies commonly tested: CN III (oculomotor) palsy — "down and out" eye, ptosis, dilated pupil; painful = posterior communicating artery aneurysm (surgical emergency); painless = diabetic mononeuropathy (pupil usually spared due to peripheral nerve supply to pupillary muscles). CN VI (abducens) palsy — failure of abduction, diplopia on lateral gaze; false localising sign in raised ICP. CN VII — Bell's palsy vs UMN (see neurology main body). CN VIII (vestibulocochlear) — acoustic neuroma presents with unilateral sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and vertigo; investigate with MRI internal auditory meati; manage with observation, stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife), or surgical resection. CN IX/X — glossopharyngeal and vagus nerve palsy: dysarthria, dysphagia, loss of gag reflex (bulbar palsy — LMN); pseudobulbar palsy (UMN) — dysarthria, dysphagia, brisk jaw jerk, emotional lability.

Neurological Emergencies: Raised Intracranial Pressure and Coning

Raised intracranial pressure (ICP) presents with headache (worse on waking, coughing, or bending forward — due to recumbency increasing venous pressure), nausea and vomiting, visual obscurations, papilloedema, and progressive conscious level decline. Cushing's triad (a late sign of severely raised ICP): hypertension, bradycardia, and abnormal respiratory pattern — indicates imminent coning (transtentorial herniation). Management principles: elevate head of bed to 30°, hyperventilate (reduces CO2, causes cerebral vasoconstriction, rapidly lowers ICP), IV mannitol 0.25–0.5 g/kg (osmotic diuretic), dexamethasone (for vasogenic oedema around tumours or abscesses, NOT for traumatic brain injury), neurosurgical referral. LP is absolutely contraindicated if raised ICP is suspected and there are focal neurological signs, altered consciousness, or papilloedema — risk of coning. Always obtain CT head first.

Temporal arteritis (giant cell arteritis, GCA): affects patients over 50 (rarely under); unilateral throbbing temporal headache, jaw claudication (pathognomonic), scalp tenderness, temporal artery tenderness/absence of pulse, visual disturbance (amaurosis fugax — irreversible blindness if untreated). ESR typically above 50 mm/hr, CRP elevated. Diagnosis confirmed by temporal artery biopsy (skip lesions mean biopsy may be negative in 10–20%). Treatment: prednisolone 40–60 mg daily immediately (do NOT wait for biopsy — blindness can occur within hours); IV methylprednisolone 500 mg TDS if visual symptoms present. Taper steroids slowly over 1–2 years guided by ESR/CRP; tocilizumab (IL-6 inhibitor) is NICE approved for relapsing GCA to allow steroid sparing. Screen for osteoporosis and diabetes with long-term steroid use.

Neurological Assessment Tools and the CPSA

The CPSA tests practical neurological examination skills. The structured neurological assessment for the upper or lower limbs follows: inspection (wasting, fasciculations, tremor, involuntary movements), tone (spasticity, lead-pipe rigidity, cogwheel rigidity, flaccidity), power (MRC scale 0–5: 0=no movement, 1=flicker, 2=movement with gravity eliminated, 3=movement against gravity, 4=movement against resistance, 5=normal), reflexes (biceps C5/6, triceps C7, supinator C5/6, knee L3/4, ankle S1, plantar), sensation (light touch, pinprick, vibration, proprioception), and coordination (finger-nose test, heel-shin test, dysdiadochokinesis). A complete neurological examination also includes assessment of speech (dysarthria/dysphasia), cranial nerves, gait (broad-based=cerebellar; shuffling=Parkinsonian; steppage=foot drop; scissor=spastic diplegia), and Romberg test (positive=dorsal column or vestibular dysfunction).

Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) and Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) are brief cognitive screening tools. MMSE scores orientation, registration, attention/calculation, recall, and language (max 30 points; below 24 suggests cognitive impairment). MoCA is more sensitive for mild cognitive impairment (max 30; below 26 = impairment). The 4AT is a rapid delirium screening tool (max 12; score 4 or above = probable delirium). For the CPSA, candidates may be asked to administer or interpret abbreviated cognitive assessments — practise the AMT10 (Abbreviated Mental Test score) which tests 10 questions including date, year, age, address recall, recognition of two people, recall of date of World War I, and counting backwards from 20.

Frequently Asked Questions About Neurology in the UKMLA

What is the thrombolysis window for ischaemic stroke and what are the contraindications?

IV alteplase can be given within 4.5 hours of definite symptom onset (or last known well time). Absolute contraindications include haemorrhage on CT, BP >185/110 mmHg (must treat first), recent major surgery (<14 days), previous ICH, active bleeding, and platelet count <100 Ã— 10â¹/L. Relative contraindications include anticoagulation (INR >1.7 or DOAC use — specific reversal agent needed), recent MI (<3 months), and severe stroke (NIHSS >25 increases haemorrhage risk). Mechanical thrombectomy extends to 6–24 hours for large vessel occlusion if perfusion imaging shows viable penumbra (DAWN and DEFUSE-3 trial criteria).

How does valproate PREVENT programme affect prescribing?

Sodium valproate must not be used in women of childbearing potential (WOCBP) unless enrolled in the Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Programme (PREVENT). This requires annual review with a specialist, patient acknowledgment form, and two forms of effective contraception. The restrictions were strengthened by the MHRA following evidence of 10-fold increased neural tube defect risk and 30–40% risk of neurodevelopmental disorders in children exposed in utero. Topiramate received similar MHRA restrictions in 2024. For WOCBP with epilepsy, lamotrigine or levetiracetam are preferred alternatives. In the AKT, if a scenario describes a woman of childbearing potential being prescribed valproate without PREVENT, this is the incorrect management option.

What investigations are needed after a first seizure?

All patients with a first suspected seizure should be referred urgently to a specialist (ideally within 2 weeks). Investigations: EEG (may show epileptiform activity but a normal EEG does not exclude epilepsy); MRI brain (preferred over CT — detects structural causes including hippocampal sclerosis, cortical dysplasia, tumour); blood glucose (at time of seizure), Na, Ca, Mg, FBC, LFTs, U&Es (metabolic causes); ECG (cardiac syncope mimic); LP only if meningitis/encephalitis suspected. AED treatment is not routinely started after a single seizure — risk of recurrence must be weighed against medication side effects. Two unprovoked seizures or one seizure with high recurrence risk (structural lesion, epileptiform EEG) warrants AED initiation.

How do I differentiate Parkinson's disease from other Parkinsonian syndromes?

Idiopathic Parkinson's disease (IPD): asymmetric onset; resting tremor (pill-rolling, 4–6 Hz); bradykinesia; rigidity (cogwheel); postural instability late; excellent response to levodopa; non-motor features (anosmia, constipation, REM sleep behaviour disorder, depression). Red flags suggesting Parkinson's plus syndrome: symmetrical onset, early falls (PSP — progressive supranuclear palsy), early autonomic failure (MSA — multiple system atrophy), early dementia (DLB), poor or no levodopa response, cerebellar signs, eye movement abnormality (vertical gaze palsy in PSP). Drug-induced Parkinsonism: caused by dopamine antagonists (metoclopramide, haloperidol, prochlorperazine) — typically symmetrical, no tremor, responds to drug withdrawal. Levodopa + carbidopa (co-careldopa) or + benserazide (co-beneldopa) is first-line for IPD with functional disability.

What is Guillain-BarrÃ© syndrome and how is it managed?

GBS is an acute demyelinating polyneuropathy typically triggered by infection (Campylobacter jejuni is most common; also CMV, EBV, Zika). Ascending flaccid paralysis with areflexia; paraesthesia; autonomic instability; respiratory failure in ~30% (monitor with spirometry — intubate if FVC <20 mL/kg or <15 mL/kg). Albuminocytological dissociation in CSF: raised protein (>0.45 g/L) with normal cell count. Nerve conduction studies show demyelination. Management: IVIg (0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days) or plasmapheresis (equally effective — not both). Supportive care: DVT prophylaxis, analgesia, cardiac monitoring, nutritional support. Steroids are NOT beneficial in GBS (unlike other inflammatory neuropathies). Recovery is over weeks to months; 80% make a good recovery.

Conclusion

Neurology UKMLA revision centres on mastering the acute management pathways (stroke thrombolysis, status epilepticus protocol), knowing the DVLA rules for epilepsy and stroke, applying the NICE NG217 epilepsy drug selection criteria, and recognising clinical patterns for dementia subtypes, MS, and peripheral neuropathies. The UMN vs LMN signs table and the LP findings table are essential clinical frameworks. Use the UKMLA syllabus to identify specific conditions listed and cross-reference with the preparation resources for neurology question practice.

post_title: Obstetrics and Gynaecology in the UKMLA: Key Topics to Revise
post_excerpt: O&G presentations appear across both UKMLA components. Here are the highest-yield conditions, emergency management steps, and the sensitive communication scenarios.
seo_title: Obstetrics and Gynaecology in the UKMLA: Key Revision Topics
seo_description: O&G is a core UKMLA domain. Revise ectopic pregnancy, pre-eclampsia, miscarriage, cervical cancer screening, and the communication skills these scenarios demand.
html_content:
Obstetrics gynaecology UKMLA questions span acute emergencies requiring immediate action, long-term gynaecological conditions, and the application of guideline-based contraceptive and screening pathways. This system is consistently well-represented in the AKT and CPSA, covering obstetric emergencies such as postpartum haemorrhage and eclampsia, gynaecological cancers with 2WW referral thresholds, and the updated UKMEC 2025 criteria for contraception. This guide consolidates the high-yield obstetrics gynaecology UKMLA content you need, with tables, clinical algorithms, and FAQs based on NICE, RCOG, and FSRH guidelines.

Obstetric Emergencies: Immediate Management

Obstetric emergencies require instant pattern recognition. Obstetrics gynaecology UKMLA questions frequently test the initial step in management — not the full protocol — so knowing the first response is critical.

EmergencyKey FeaturesImmediate Management
Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) — primary (<24h)Blood loss >500 mL (vaginal) or >1000 mL (CS) within 24 hours; uterine atony (most common — 70%); 4 Ts: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, ThrombinCall for help; IV access Ã— 2; bimanual uterine compression; oxytocin 10 IU IV/IM; ergometrine; carboprost (PGF2Î±); misoprostol; tranexamic acid; blood transfusion; consider B-Lynch suture, uterine artery ligation, balloon tamponade, interventional radiology, or hysterectomy
Cord prolapseUmbilical cord presenting below the presenting part after membrane rupture; fetal bradycardia on CTGCall obstetric emergency; push presenting part off cord manually (do NOT push cord back); knee-chest position or Trendelenburg; fill bladder with 500 mL saline; immediate CS; do NOT attempt vaginal delivery unless imminent
Shoulder dystociaHead delivered but shoulders impacted; turtle sign (head retracts); failure to deliver body with gentle tractionHELPERR: Help, Episiotomy, Legs (McRoberts — hyperflexion of hips), Pressure (suprapubic — Rubin II), Enter (internal rotation manoeuvres — Woods screw), Remove posterior arm, Roll (Gaskin manoeuvre — all-fours)
Placental abruptionPainful antepartum haemorrhage; tender, woody-hard uterus; fetal distress; may have concealed haemorrhageIV access, FBC, clotting (risk of DIC), group and save; CTG; if fetal compromise — emergency CS; if mild and preterm — conservative management with steroids
Uterine ruptureSudden severe abdominal pain; scar tenderness (previous CS); fetal parts palpable abdominally; loss of fetal heart sounds; maternal collapseEmergency laparotomy; category 1 CS; resuscitation; repair or hysterectomy
Amniotic fluid embolismSudden cardiovascular collapse during labour or immediately postpartum; hypoxia; DIC; seizures; rare but highly fatalResuscitation (ABC); massive haemorrhage protocol; ITU; supportive; no specific antidote

Antepartum Haemorrhage: Placenta Praevia vs Abruption

FeaturePlacenta PraeviaPlacental Abruption
PainPainlessPainful (sudden, severe)
BleedingBright red, revealedDark red; may be concealed
UterusSoft, non-tenderWoody-hard, tender (boardlike)
Fetal presentationMalpresentation, high headNormal presentation usually
Fetal compromiseLess common acutelyCommon; fetal distress, bradycardia
USSLow-lying placenta (within 20 mm of os); diagnosis at 20-week anomaly scanRetroplacental clot (often not visible); clinical diagnosis
Do NOTDo NOT perform digital vaginal examinationCheck clotting (risk of DIC)
ManagementAdmit; steroids if preterm; elective CS at 36–37 weeks (major praevia)Emergency CS if fetal compromise or severe haemorrhage; conservative if mild and preterm

Pre-eclampsia, Eclampsia, and HELLP Syndrome

Pre-eclampsia is defined as new-onset hypertension (≥140/90 mmHg on two occasions >4 hours apart) after 20 weeks of pregnancy with significant proteinuria (≥0.3 g/24h, PCR ≥30 mg/mmol, or 2+ on dipstick confirmed) or end-organ dysfunction. Risk factors: nulliparity, previous pre-eclampsia, obesity (BMI >35), multiple pregnancy, CKD, diabetes, hypertension, antiphospholipid syndrome, age >40, family history. Aspirin 150 mg nightly from 12 weeks in high-risk patients (NICE definition: ≥1 high-risk factor or ≥2 moderate-risk factors) reduces risk by ~15%.

Management: antihypertensives (labetalol first-line, then nifedipine MR, then methyldopa if both fail; avoid ACE inhibitors and ARBs in pregnancy); corticosteroids (betamethasone) if <34 weeks for fetal lung maturity; magnesium sulphate 4 g IV loading dose then 1 g/hour infusion for eclampsia prophylaxis (in severe pre-eclampsia) and treatment. Monitor urine output, reflexes (loss of patellar reflex = early toxicity), and respiratory rate on magnesium. Calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% IV is the antidote for magnesium toxicity. Definitive treatment: delivery. Deliver at 37 weeks if mild-moderate pre-eclampsia; immediate delivery (regardless of gestation) if severe features (BP >160/110, haemolysis, thrombocytopaenia, hepatic involvement, eclampsia, pulmonary oedema).

Eclampsia: tonic-clonic seizure in a woman with pre-eclampsia. Management: left lateral position, ABC, magnesium sulphate 4 g IV over 5 minutes (repeat 2 g if seizure recurs), airway management, deliver after stabilisation. HELLP syndrome (Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets): variant of severe pre-eclampsia; right upper quadrant/epigastric pain, nausea, malaise; LDH elevated, liver enzymes elevated, platelets <100 Ã— 10â¹/L. Risk of DIC, liver rupture. Management: immediate delivery, blood transfusion, platelet transfusion if <20 or bleeding.

Gestational Diabetes and Antenatal Screening

Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is glucose intolerance first recognised in pregnancy. Risk factors: BMI >30, previous GDM, previous macrosomic baby (>4.5 kg), family history T2DM, South Asian/Black/Middle Eastern ethnicity. Screening: OGTT (75 g at 24–28 weeks) — diagnostic if fasting glucose ≥5.6 mmol/L or 2-hour glucose ≥7.8 mmol/L (NICE NG3). Management: dietary advice, blood glucose monitoring; metformin if targets not met; insulin if metformin insufficient or contraindicated. Targets: fasting <5.3 mmol/L, 1-hour post-meal <7.8 mmol/L, 2-hour post-meal <6.4 mmol/L. Complications: macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, neonatal hypoglycaemia, stillbirth, NICU admission. Monitor with growth scans from 28–32 weeks.

Antenatal screening programme (UK): first trimester combined screening (11+0 to 13+6 weeks) — nuchal translucency USS + maternal age + PAPP-A + free beta-hCG gives trisomy 21/18/13 risk. High risk (≥1:150): offer NIPT or diagnostic testing (CVS/amniocentesis). Anomaly scan at 18+0 to 20+6 weeks: structural survey including cardiac, spine, brain, abdominal wall, limbs. Hb and blood group at booking; repeat at 28 weeks. Group B Streptococcus (GBS): not routinely screened in UK (unlike USA); treat intrapartum if risk factors (previous GBS baby, preterm, prolonged rupture of membranes, maternal fever).

Contraception: UKMEC 2025 and Methods Comparison

The UK Medical Eligibility Criteria (UKMEC) 2025 classifies contraceptive methods as: Category 1 (no restriction); Category 2 (benefits outweigh risks); Category 3 (risks outweigh benefits — use other method if available); Category 4 (absolute contraindication). UKMEC 2025 introduced upgraded VTE risk categories for combined hormonal contraception (CHC) and added multiple VTE risk factors as a new condition.

ConditionCOCP (CHC)POPIUD (copper)IUS (LNG)Implant
Migraine with aura4 (contraindicated)2122
Migraine without aura2 (<35), 3 (≥35 + smoker)1111
Previous VTE42122
Breast cancer (current)44144
Breastfeeding <6 weeks postpartum42222
Hypertension (≥160/100)41111
Smoking ≥35 years and ≥15 cigs/day41111
Focal liver lesion (hepatocellular adenoma)43133

Emergency Contraception

Levonorgestrel EC (Levonelle 1500 micrograms): effective within 72 hours of unprotected sexual intercourse (UPSI); effectiveness decreases with time. Ulipristal acetate (ellaOne 30 mg): effective within 120 hours of UPSI; more effective than LNG-EC in hours 72–120 and in heavier women. Copper IUD: most effective EC (failure rate <0.1%); insert within 120 hours of UPSI or within 5 days of calculated ovulation; also provides ongoing contraception. After LNG-EC: advise patient to use barrier method until next period. After ulipristal: avoid any hormonal contraception for 5 days (reduces effectiveness). EC does not cause abortion — it prevents implantation or ovulation.

Cervical Screening, STIs, and Gynaecological Cancers

Cervical Screening Programme

England moved to HPV primary testing in 2019. Women aged 25–49: HPV test every 3 years. Women aged 50–64: every 5 years. Result: HPV not detected — return to routine screening. HPV detected but negative cytology — repeat HPV in 12 months. HPV detected and abnormal cytology — colposcopy referral. High-grade cytology (HSIL) regardless of HPV: colposcopy. HPV is the cause of virtually all cervical cancers; types 16 and 18 are high-risk. HPV vaccination: Gardasil 9 offered to boys and girls aged 12–13 (school year 8) — protects against HPV 6, 11, 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52, 58.

STI Recognition and Management

STIKey FeaturesDiagnosisTreatment
Chlamydia trachomatisOften asymptomatic; mucopurulent discharge, dysuria; PID risk; epididymo-orchitis in menNAAT (nucleic acid amplification test) — vulvovaginal swab or first-pass urineDoxycycline 100 mg BD for 7 days; azithromycin 1 g stat if compliance concern; partner notification
Gonorrhoea (N. gonorrhoeae)Purulent discharge; dysuria; often co-infected with chlamydia; pharyngeal/rectal infection possible; increasing resistanceNAAT and culture (for sensitivity); urethral/endocervical swabCeftriaxone 1 g IM stat + azithromycin 1 g (check local sensitivity); test of cure at 2 weeks
Syphilis (T. pallidum)Primary: painless chancre. Secondary: systemic illness, maculopapular rash (palms/soles), condylomata lata. Tertiary: neurosyphilis, aortitis, gummaDark-field microscopy (primary); serology: TPHA, RPR/VDRL (non-treponemal — useful for monitoring)Benzathine penicillin G 2.4 MU IM stat (primary/secondary); prolonged IV for neurosyphilis; Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction with first dose
Herpes simplex (HSV-1/2)Painful genital ulcers; dysuria; first episode often severe with systemic features; recurrences less severeClinical + PCR swab of ulcerAciclovir 400 mg TDS for 5–10 days (first episode); suppressive therapy (400 mg BD) if ≥6 recurrences/year
Trichomonas vaginalisOffensive, frothy, yellow-green discharge; pruritus; strawberry cervixMicroscopy (motile protozoa); NAATMetronidazole 400 mg BD for 7 days (or 2 g stat); treat partner

Gynaecological Cancer Referral and Presentations

Ovarian cancer: insidious onset; bloating, abdominal distension, urinary frequency, early satiety. NICE: CA-125 first-line test if symptoms (bloating, pelvic/abdominal pain, early satiety, urinary frequency) ≥12 times/month. If CA-125 ≥35 IU/mL: USS pelvis. If USS abnormal: 2WW referral. Endometrial/uterine cancer: postmenopausal bleeding (PMB) is the most common presentation (90% of uterine cancers present with PMB). Urgent 2WW: postmenopausal bleeding; unexplained vaginal discharge in post-menopausal women. USS to assess endometrial thickness; if >4 mm post-menopause or suspicious — hysteroscopy and biopsy. Risk factors: obesity, unopposed oestrogen (HRT without progestogen), tamoxifen, nulliparity, PCOS, Lynch syndrome. Treatment: total abdominal hysterectomy + bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (TAH + BSO); radiotherapy for advanced disease.

Menopause, PID, and Endometriosis

Menopause and HRT

Menopause is defined as 12 months of amenorrhoea in a woman over 45. Perimenopause: irregular cycles, vasomotor symptoms (hot flushes, night sweats), mood changes, sleep disturbance. Diagnosis is clinical if ≥45; FSH not routinely needed (levels fluctuate). FSH >30 IU/L on two occasions 4–6 weeks apart confirms menopause in women under 45 (premature ovarian insufficiency). HRT: systemic oestrogen (with progestogen if intact uterus) is most effective for vasomotor symptoms. Benefits: reduces fracture risk, may reduce cardiovascular risk if started within 10 years of menopause/age <60. Risks: combined HRT slightly increases breast cancer risk (similar to 1–2 years of delay in menopause); VTE risk increased with oral oestrogen (transdermal avoids first-pass, lower VTE risk). Contraindications: current/recent breast cancer, undiagnosed vaginal bleeding, active liver disease, known thrombophilia (use transdermal with caution). NICE (NG23): HRT should be offered to women with menopausal symptoms after discussing risks and benefits; do not routinely limit duration.

Pelvic Inflammatory Disease and Endometriosis

PID: infection of upper genital tract (uterus, fallopian tubes, ovaries). Usually ascending from cervicovaginal flora; often polymicrobial (Chlamydia, Gonorrhoea, anaerobes). Symptoms: lower abdominal/pelvic pain, dyspareunia, abnormal vaginal discharge, fever, cervical motion tenderness (CMT — excitation). Management: empirical antibiotics — outpatient: ofloxacin 400 mg BD + metronidazole 400 mg BD for 14 days; inpatient (severe): IV cefoxitin or ceftriaxone + doxycycline + metronidazole. Complications: ectopic pregnancy risk (Ã—6), infertility, chronic pelvic pain, tubo-ovarian abscess (USS-guided drainage or laparoscopy). Endometriosis: endometrial tissue outside the uterus (most commonly on ovaries, peritoneum, POD). Symptoms: cyclical pelvic pain, dysmenorrhoea, deep dyspareunia, dyschezia, subfertility. Diagnosis: laparoscopy (gold standard); USS can identify endometriomas (chocolate cysts). Medical management: COCP (continuous), Mirena IUS, GnRH analogues (with add-back HRT), dienogest. Surgical: laparoscopic ablation/excision of deposits.

Miscarriage and Ectopic Pregnancy

Type of MiscarriageFeaturesOS on USS/ExaminationManagement
ThreatenedBleeding, no pain or mild; fetus viable on USSClosedReassurance, repeat USS; rest; no proven intervention
InevitableHeavy bleeding, cramping; pregnancy not yet passedOpenExpectant, medical (misoprostol), or surgical (ERPC)
IncompleteSome products of conception remainOpenMedical (misoprostol) or surgical (ERPC/MVA)
CompleteAll products passed; empty uterus on USSClosedReassurance; anti-D if Rh-negative and >12 weeks
Missed (silent)No symptoms; fetal demise without expulsion; detected on USSClosedExpectant, medical, or surgical — patient preference
SepticRetained products + signs of infection; fever, offensive discharge, uterine tendernessOpenIV antibiotics, urgent surgical evacuation

Anti-D Rh prophylaxis: all Rh-negative women with threatened miscarriage at or after 12 weeks (or with heavy bleeding/pain before 12 weeks), ectopic pregnancy, termination, or invasive procedures should receive anti-D 250 IU IM to prevent Rh sensitisation. Ectopic pregnancy: implantation outside the uterine cavity (most commonly in fallopian tube — ampullary portion 70%). Risk factors: previous ectopic, PID, tubal surgery, IVF, IUD (not cause but raises relative risk of ectopic if contraceptive fails), smoking. Presentation: unilateral pelvic pain, amenorrhoea, vaginal bleeding; shoulder-tip pain (diaphragmatic irritation from haemoperitoneum) indicates rupture — surgical emergency. Investigations: urine hCG; serum beta-hCG (doubles every 48h in viable IUP; plateaus/falls in ectopic); TVUSS (gestational sac outside uterus, free fluid in pouch of Douglas). Management: surgical (salpingectomy or salpingotomy) for haemodynamically unstable or large ectopic; methotrexate IM for stable, small ectopic (serum hCG <5000 IU/L, no cardiac activity, patient compliant for follow-up); expectant management for serum hCG <1000 IU/L and falling.

Normal Labour, Induction, and Operative Delivery

Understanding the stages of normal labour is essential for obstetrics gynaecology UKMLA questions. First stage: onset of regular contractions to full cervical dilation (10 cm). Latent phase: from onset of labour to 4 cm dilation; active phase: 4–10 cm dilation (expected rate approximately 0.5–1 cm/hour; refer to obstetrician if progress is slow). Second stage: full dilation to delivery of the baby. Third stage: delivery of the placenta (managed actively with oxytocin 10 IU IM to reduce PPH risk; expect placental delivery within 30 minutes). The partogram (labour record) documents cervical dilation, fetal head descent, CTG features, maternal observations, and urine output — a deviation from the expected curve triggers review.

Induction of labour (IOL): indications include post-dates pregnancy (offered at 41 weeks in UK), pre-eclampsia, gestational diabetes, IUGR, PPROM (prelabour rupture of membranes at term), maternal request. Methods: prostaglandin E2 (dinoprostone) pessary or gel to ripen the cervix (Bishop score used to assess cervical favourability; score below 6 = unfavourable cervix requiring cervical ripening); if cervix favourable — amniotomy (ARM) Â± Syntocinon (IV oxytocin infusion). Contraindications to IOL: previous uterine scar with unfavourable cervix (relative — risk of uterine rupture with prostaglandins), placenta praevia, cord presentation, malpresentation. Caesarean section categories: category 1 (immediate threat to life — within 30 minutes); category 2 (maternal/fetal compromise, not immediately life-threatening — within 75 minutes); category 3 (elective timing needed, no immediate compromise — within 24 hours); category 4 (at time to suit patient and team).

Instrumental delivery: ventouse (vacuum cup) and forceps are used to expedite delivery in the second stage. Indications: presumed fetal compromise, maternal exhaustion, failure to progress, medical conditions where pushing is contraindicated (e.g. raised ICP, recent cardiac surgery). Ventouse: less maternal trauma but higher failure rate and risk of neonatal cephalhaematoma and retinal haemorrhages. Forceps: higher maternal perineal trauma but lower failure rate; Kielland's forceps for rotational delivery. Both require the head to be at or below the level of the ischial spines, membranes ruptured, bladder emptied, adequate analgesia, and full informed consent.

Subfertility and Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome

Subfertility is defined as failure to conceive after 12 months of regular unprotected sexual intercourse (offer investigation after 6 months if woman is over 36 or known risk factors). Causes: anovulation (35%) — PCOS, hypothalamic dysfunction, hyperprolactinaemia, premature ovarian insufficiency; tubal damage (25%) — PID, endometriosis, previous ectopic; male factor (30%) — semen analysis (volume, count, motility, morphology); unexplained (10%). Initial investigations: semen analysis; mid-luteal progesterone (day 21 in a 28-day cycle — level above 30 nmol/L confirms ovulation); FSH, LH, oestradiol, AMH (anti-MÃ¼llerian hormone — reflects ovarian reserve); USS pelvis; consider hysterosalpingography (HSG) or laparoscopy for tubal assessment. Treatment: anovulatory infertility — clomifene citrate (anti-oestrogen; ovulation induction; increases multiple pregnancy risk); letrozole (aromatase inhibitor — now preferred for PCOS-related anovulation); gonadotrophins; IVF if tubal factor or failed oral ovulation induction.

Polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS): the most common endocrine disorder in women of reproductive age, affecting 5–10%. Rotterdam criteria (2 of 3 needed): oligo/anovulation; clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism (acne, hirsutism, raised free testosterone/DHEAS); polycystic ovaries on USS (12 or more follicles per ovary measuring 2–9 mm, or increased ovarian volume above 10 mL). Associated with insulin resistance, metabolic syndrome, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular risk, and endometrial hyperplasia/cancer (due to unopposed oestrogen from anovulation). Management: weight loss (improves insulin sensitivity and restores ovulation in overweight patients); metformin (improves insulin sensitivity, may restore ovulation and menstrual regularity); COCP (manages hyperandrogenism, acne, hirsutism, and provides endometrial protection); clomifene/letrozole for fertility; eflornithine (cream) or laser for unwanted facial hair. Screen for IGT/T2DM with fasting glucose or OGTT, lipid profile, and BP annually.

Safeguarding, Mental Health in Pregnancy, and Domestic Violence

Perinatal mental health is a critical area of obstetrics gynaecology UKMLA revision. Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS): 10-item validated questionnaire scored 0–30; score 10 or above suggests postnatal depression (PND); score 13 or above indicates major depression; question 10 (thoughts of self-harm) scores separately and requires immediate assessment regardless of total score. PND affects approximately 10–15% of new mothers; risk factors include previous depression, poor social support, adverse life events, and complicated delivery. Treatment: psychological therapies (CBT, counselling) for mild-moderate; antidepressants (sertraline is first-line in breastfeeding — lowest breast milk transfer) for moderate-severe. Postnatal psychosis (puerperal psychosis): rare (1–2/1000); onset within 2 weeks of delivery; confusion, hallucinations, delusions, rapidly fluctuating mood — psychiatric emergency requiring inpatient admission (mother and baby unit). All women booking for antenatal care should be screened for mental health history, domestic violence, and substance misuse using NICE-recommended questions. Healthcare professionals have a duty to safeguard — make a referral to children's social care if there is concern that an unborn or newborn child is at risk, even if the mother does not consent.

Frequently Asked Questions About Obstetrics and Gynaecology in the UKMLA

What is the first-line management of primary postpartum haemorrhage?

The first step is to call for help and activate a major haemorrhage protocol. Simultaneously: establish large-bore IV access (two wide-bore cannulae), take blood (FBC, clotting, U&E, group and crossmatch), give IV fluids, and perform uterine massage. The most common cause of PPH is uterine atony (70%) — treat with uterine massage, bimanual compression, and uterotonics: oxytocin 10 IU IV bolus (first-line), then ergometrine 500 micrograms IV (avoid in hypertension), then carboprost 250 micrograms IM (avoid in asthma), then misoprostol 1000 micrograms PR. Tranexamic acid 1 g IV should be given as early as possible (within 3 hours of PPH onset). If medical management fails: intrauterine balloon tamponade (Bakri balloon), B-Lynch brachysystole suture, uterine artery ligation, interventional radiology, and finally hysterectomy as last resort.

What are the UKMEC 4 contraindications to the combined oral contraceptive pill?

UKMEC 4 conditions (absolute contraindications to COCP) include: current or history of venous thromboembolism (VTE); migraine with aura (stroke risk); ischaemic heart disease or stroke; valvular heart disease with complications; current breast cancer; active viral hepatitis; cirrhosis (severe decompensated); smoking ≥35 years old with ≥15 cigarettes/day; uncontrolled hypertension (≥160/100); postpartum (breastfeeding <6 weeks or not breastfeeding <3 weeks). UKMEC 2025 added enhanced VTE risk conditions — women with multiple VTE risk factors should be classified as UKMEC 4 for CHC.

When is magnesium sulphate used in obstetrics?

Magnesium sulphate has two main obstetric indications: (1) Neuroprotection for the preterm fetus — given to women in established preterm labour <30 weeks gestation to reduce the risk of cerebral palsy in the neonate; (2) Eclampsia prophylaxis and treatment — in severe pre-eclampsia (as prophylaxis) and in eclampsia (to stop and prevent recurrent seizures). The loading dose is 4 g IV over 5–20 minutes followed by a maintenance infusion of 1–2 g/hour. Signs of toxicity: loss of patellar reflexes (first sign, at ~3.5–5 mmol/L), respiratory depression (>5 mmol/L), cardiac arrest (>7.5 mmol/L). Antidote: calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% IV. Renal function must be monitored as magnesium is renally excreted.

How do I interpret a CTG for the UKMLA?

CTG (cardiotocography) interpretation uses the DR C BRAVADO mnemonic: Define Risk, Contractions, Baseline Rate (normal 110–160 bpm), Rhythm, Accelerations (normal: ≥2 in 20 minutes, amplitude ≥15 bpm for ≥15 seconds), Variability (normal: 5–25 bpm), Decelerations. Reassuring features: baseline 110–160, variability 5–25, accelerations present, no decelerations. Non-reassuring: one abnormal feature. Abnormal/pathological: two or more non-reassuring or one abnormal feature (late decelerations — after contraction peak — most concerning, suggesting fetal hypoxia; variable decelerations — cord compression; prolonged deceleration >3 min). If pathological CTG: fetal scalp blood sampling (pH <7.20 = deliver; pH 7.20–7.25 = repeat; pH >7.25 = reassuring) or immediate delivery if acute bradycardia.

What are the risk factors for pre-eclampsia and who should receive aspirin prophylaxis?

NICE classifies risk as high or moderate. High-risk factors (one required): hypertensive disease in previous pregnancy, CKD, autoimmune disease (SLE, antiphospholipid syndrome), type 1 or 2 diabetes, chronic hypertension. Moderate-risk factors (two or more required): first pregnancy, age ≥40, BMI >35 at first visit, family history of pre-eclampsia, multiple pregnancy, interpregnancy interval >10 years. Aspirin 150 mg nightly from 12 weeks (until delivery) is recommended for all women with one high-risk factor or two or more moderate-risk factors. Regular BP monitoring and urine dipstick at each antenatal visit. Fetal growth scans and uterine artery Doppler if high risk. Low-dose aspirin reduces pre-eclampsia incidence by ~15% and preterm pre-eclampsia by ~60% in high-risk women.

Conclusion

Obstetrics gynaecology UKMLA revision requires integrating acute emergency management (the obstetric emergencies table is exam-essential) with guideline-based contraceptive prescribing using UKMEC 2025, antenatal screening pathways, and gynaecological cancer referral thresholds. The magnesium protocol for eclampsia, the miscarriage management table, and the CTG interpretation framework are particularly high-yield for both AKT and CPSA stations. Use the UKMLA syllabus to confirm which O&G conditions are listed, and practise application questions in the preparation resources.
