post_title: Surgical Presentations in the UKMLA: What a Foundation Doctor Needs to Know
post_excerpt: Surgical knowledge in the UKMLA focuses on recognition and initial management, not operative technique. Here are the most frequently tested surgical presentations.
seo_title: Surgical Presentations in the UKMLA: Foundation-Level Revision
seo_description: The UKMLA tests surgical presentations at foundation level — recognition and initial management, not operative technique. Learn the key conditions and referral criteria.
html_content:
Surgical presentations UKMLA questions test the knowledge a foundation doctor needs from day one on the wards: recognising acute abdominal emergencies, managing perioperative risk, understanding postoperative complications, and knowing when to call for urgent surgical review. This topic spans primary surgical conditions such as appendicitis and bowel obstruction through to the practical management of drains, wounds, fluid balance, and DVT prophylaxis. This guide covers every major surgical presentations UKMLA area with data tables, clinical algorithms, and the high-yield details most frequently tested in the AKT and CPSA.

The Acute Abdomen: Assessment and Diagnosis

Surgical presentations UKMLA questions on the acute abdomen require rapid differential diagnosis based on location, character of pain, and associated features. The initial assessment follows ABCDE, then history (site, onset, character, radiation, associations, timing, exacerbating/relieving factors, severity) and targeted examination.

DiagnosisKey Clinical FeaturesSignsInvestigation
AppendicitisPeriumbilical pain migrating to RIF; nausea; low-grade fever; anorexiaRebound tenderness RIF; Rovsing's sign; McBurney's point; Psoas signFBC (raised WCC); CRP; USS (children/women); CT abdomen (adults)
Small bowel obstructionColicky central abdominal pain; vomiting (bilious); absolute constipation; distensionTinkling bowel sounds (early); silent (late); distended abdomenAXR (ladder pattern, central loops, no gas in colon); CT abdomen
Large bowel obstructionDistension; absolute constipation; late vomiting (faeculent); less severe painDistended abdomen; peripheral colonic gas on AXRAXR (peripheral loop distension, colonic gas); CT abdomen/colonoscopy (cause)
Perforated peptic ulcerSudden severe epigastric pain; history of PUD/NSAIDs/steroids; rigid abdomenPeritonism; board-like rigidity; absent bowel sounds; guardingErect CXR (free air under diaphragm); CT abdomen if equivocal
Acute cholecystitisRUQ pain (constant); nausea; fever; history of fatty food intolerance; Murphy's sign positiveMurphy's sign; RUQ tenderness; fever >38°CUSS (gallstones, thick-walled GB, pericholecystic fluid); FBC; LFTs
Mesenteric ischaemiaSudden severe periumbilical pain; pain out of proportion to signs; AF; vascular disease; nauseaMay be unremarkable initially; peritonism develops lateCT angiography (gold standard); raised lactate; metabolic acidosis
Sigmoid volvulusElderly/institutionalised; rapid onset massive distension; absolute constipationGrossly distended abdomen; "coffee bean" sign on AXRAXR; CT; rigid sigmoidoscopy (diagnostic and therapeutic)
Ruptured AAATriad: sudden severe back/abdominal pain, pulsatile mass, hypotension; males >60; smokerPulsatile expansile mass; haemodynamic instabilityImmediate USS (if stable); CT angiography; OR if unstable

Perioperative Management: Drugs, Consent, and Preparation

Perioperative Drug Management

Drug ClassManagementRationale
WarfarinStop 5 days preoperatively; check INR day before; bridge with LMWH if high thrombotic risk (e.g. mechanical heart valve, AF with CHA2DS2-VASc ≥4); restart 24–48h post-opAvoid bleeding; bridging for high VTE/stroke risk
DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran)Stop 24–48h before (apixaban/rivaroxaban); 48–72h before (dabigatran, especially if eGFR <50); no bridging required for mostShorter half-life; no monitoring needed
Antiplatelet (aspirin)Low-dose aspirin: continue for most surgery (benefit outweighs risk); stop if high bleeding risk surgery (neurosurgery, ophthalmology, posterior spinal); clopidogrel: usually stop 5 days beforeAspirin withdrawal risk of MI/stent thrombosis in cardiac patients
MetforminWithhold on day of surgery and 48h post-op if major surgery with risk of AKI/contrast; restart when eating and renal function normalRisk of lactic acidosis with AKI/contrast nephropathy
ACE inhibitors / ARBsOmit on day of surgery (risk of refractory intraoperative hypotension); restart when haemodynamically stableVasodilation + anaesthetic agents = severe hypotension
Steroids (long-term)Continue at usual dose; add steroid cover for major surgery (hydrocortisone 25 mg at induction, 25 mg TDS for 24h, taper back)Adrenal suppression risk; cortisol response to surgical stress impaired
COCP/HRTStop COCP 4 weeks before major or lower limb surgery (VTE risk); restart after full mobilisation. HRT: individualise risk; transdermal HRT lower VTE risk than oralOestrogen increases VTE risk perioperatively

Consent for Surgery

Valid consent requires three elements: the patient must have capacity, be adequately informed, and consent voluntarily (without coercion). Adults are presumed to have capacity under the Mental Capacity Act 2005 unless demonstrated otherwise. Capacity assessment: can the patient understand the information, retain it, weigh it up, and communicate their decision? For consent to be informed, the patient must be told about the nature of the procedure, its purpose, benefits, risks (including common risks and rare but serious risks), alternatives (including no treatment), and what happens without treatment. The Montgomery v Lanarkshire judgment (2015) requires disclosure of any risk that a reasonable patient in their position would want to know — not just what a reasonable doctor would disclose. Consent for a competent adult cannot be given by a relative. For an incapacitous patient, proceed in their best interests following MCA principles; check for Lasting Power of Attorney (LPA) for health and welfare.

WHO Surgical Safety Checklist

The WHO Surgical Safety Checklist (introduced by the NHS in 2009) is completed at three stages: Sign In (before anaesthetic induction — confirm patient identity, site, procedure, consent, allergies, anaesthetic machine check, pulse oximeter); Time Out (before skin incision — team introductions, confirm procedure, site, antibiotic prophylaxis given, imaging available); Sign Out (before patient leaves theatre — confirm procedure performed, instrument count, specimen labelling, postoperative instructions). The checklist has been shown to reduce surgical complications by ~36% and mortality by ~47% in high-income countries.

Postoperative Complications: The 5 Ws

Postoperative fever follows a temporal pattern captured by the "5 Ws" mnemonic, guiding the differential diagnosis by time from surgery.

Time"W" CauseDetailsManagement
Day 1 (0–24h)Wind (atelectasis)Most common early cause; small airway collapse; basal crackles; low-grade feverDeep breathing exercises; physiotherapy; early mobilisation; incentive spirometry
Day 1–3Wind (pneumonia)Aspiration pneumonia risk (post-GA); consolidation on CXR; productive coughAntibiotics; chest physio; sputum culture
Day 3–5Water (UTI)Urinary catheter; dysuria; frequency (when catheter removed); cloudy urineMSU; antibiotics if confirmed; remove catheter as soon as possible
Day 5–7Wound (SSI)Erythema, swelling, warmth, discharge at surgical wound; feverWound swab; antibiotics (if cellulitis); open and drain (if collection); debridement
Day 5–7Walk (DVT/PE)Calf pain/swelling (DVT); breathlessness, pleuritic pain, haemoptysis (PE)Doppler USS (DVT); CTPA (PE); therapeutic anticoagulation
Day 7+Wonder drugs (drug fever)Drug reaction; no localising source; eosinophilia; rashReview drug chart; withdraw culprit if identified

DVT Prophylaxis in Surgical Patients

All surgical inpatients must be assessed for VTE risk (Caprini score or local equivalent). Mechanical prophylaxis: anti-embolic stockings (TEDS, correctly fitted) and intermittent pneumatic compression (IPC) devices — started preoperatively and continued until mobile. Pharmacological prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. dalteparin or enoxaparin) for moderate-high risk surgical patients — started 6–12 hours post-operatively (earlier timing for laparoscopic surgery). Duration: 28 days for cancer surgery (NICE NG89). Contraindications to LMWH: active bleeding, severe thrombocytopaenia (<50 Ã— 10â¹/L), HIT. Fondaparinux is an alternative if HIT suspected. Early mobilisation is mandatory for all patients.

Hernias, Wound Healing, and Surgical Site Infection

Abdominal Hernias Comparison

Hernia TypeLocationFeaturesRisk FactorsRepair
Inguinal (indirect)Passes through deep inguinal ring, inguinal canal, superficial ring; may enter scrotumMost common hernia (75% of all); controlled by finger at deep inguinal ring; cough impulseMales; raised intra-abdominal pressure; patent processus vaginalisLaparoscopic (TEP/TAPP) or open Lichtenstein mesh repair
Inguinal (direct)Directly through posterior wall of inguinal canal (Hesselbach's triangle); does not pass through deep ringMedial to inferior epigastric vessels; not controlled by deep ring pressure; less likely to descend to scrotumElderly males; raised IAP; weak abdominal wallAs above
FemoralBelow and lateral to pubic tubercle; through femoral canal (medial compartment of femoral sheath)More common in women; high risk of strangulation; small, often irreducible; below inguinal ligamentFemales; multiparous; raised IAPEmergency repair if strangulated; elective repair (Lockwood/McEvedy approach)
UmbilicalThrough umbilical ringCommon in infants (usually close spontaneously by age 3–4); adults: acquired (obesity, ascites, pregnancy)Obesity; ascites; pregnancy; multiparityElective repair in adults; watchful waiting in children
IncisionalThrough previous surgical scarHistory of laparotomy; most common at midline scars; risk of strangulationObesity; wound infection; malnutrition; steroids; poor surgical techniqueMesh repair; ERAS optimisation preoperatively
EpigastricThrough linea alba, above umbilicusSmall, fatty hernias; often not reducible; rarely strangulateMiddle-aged; raised IAPElective repair if symptomatic

Complications of hernias: irreducibility (cannot be pushed back), obstruction (bowel lumen obstructed), strangulation (blood supply compromised — surgical emergency requiring immediate repair). Femoral hernias have the highest risk of strangulation — all femoral hernias should be repaired. A strangulated hernia presents with severe pain, erythema over the hernia, systemic sepsis, and may progress to bowel gangrene and perforation.

Wound Healing

Primary intention: wound edges approximated (sutures, staples, glue) — clean surgical wounds. Secondary intention: wound left open to granulate — infected wounds, pressure ulcers. Tertiary intention (delayed primary closure): wound initially left open (to control infection), then closed days later. Four phases of wound healing: haemostasis (immediate — platelet plug, clot); inflammation (0–3 days — neutrophils, macrophages clear debris); proliferation (day 3–21 — fibroblasts, granulation tissue, angiogenesis, epithelialisation); remodelling (21 days–2 years — collagen type III replaced by type I; maximum tensile strength 80% of original). Factors impairing wound healing: malnutrition (low zinc, vitamin C); diabetes; steroids; immunosuppression; poor perfusion; infection; obesity; haematoma; necrotic tissue. Surgical site infection (SSI): most common healthcare-associated infection. Incisional SSI: cellulitis Â± abscess at wound site. Deep SSI: involves fascia/muscle. Organ space SSI: involves cavity opened during surgery. Management: wound swab; IV antibiotics for cellulitis; open and drain abscess; debridement of necrotic tissue; negative-pressure wound therapy (VAC) for complex wounds.

Vascular Emergencies and Urological Presentations

Vascular Presentations

Acute limb ischaemia (6 Ps): Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paraesthesia, Paralysis, Perishing with cold (poikilothermia). This is a surgical emergency — call vascular surgery immediately. Causes: embolism (from AF, recent MI — sudden onset, no prior claudication, contralateral pulses present) vs thrombosis (sudden deterioration of chronic ischaemia — prior claudication, atrophic skin changes, absent contralateral pulses). Management: heparin anticoagulation immediately; angiography Â± embolectomy (Fogarty catheter), bypass, or thrombolysis. Amputation if non-salvageable. Critical limb ischaemia (chronic): rest pain, ulceration, or gangrene for >2 weeks; ABPI <0.5. Requires urgent vascular assessment. AAA management: screening programme in England (all men aged 65 invited for one-time USS). AAA <5.5 cm: surveillance (3-monthly/annual USS depending on size). AAA ≥5.5 cm: elective repair (EVAR preferred if suitable anatomy; open repair otherwise). Ruptured AAA: immediate resuscitation and emergency vascular surgery (mortality >50%).

Urological Presentations

Renal colic: sudden severe loin-to-groin pain (colicky); haematuria; nausea/vomiting; patient unable to lie still (distinguishes from peritonitis). Investigation: CT KUB (gold standard for stone diagnosis, size, and site — non-contrast); urine dipstick (blood); USS (less sensitive than CT but avoids radiation, used in pregnancy). Management: NSAIDs (diclofenac) first-line analgesia; IV opioids if severe; alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) to facilitate passage of distal ureteric stones ≤10 mm; urological referral if stone >10 mm, urosepsis, complete obstruction, single kidney, or failure to pass spontaneously. Lithotripsy (ESWL), ureteroscopy (laser), or PCNL depending on stone size and location. Testicular torsion: surgical emergency (salvage rate drops from >90% if <6h to <10% if >24h from onset). Sudden onset severe scrotal pain; nausea/vomiting; high-riding tender testis; loss of cremasteric reflex. Management: do NOT delay for investigations — immediate surgical exploration and bilateral fixation (orchidopexy) or orchidectomy if non-viable. Urinary retention: acute retention — painful inability to void. Causes: BPH (most common in men), urethral stricture, constipation, post-op, drugs (anticholinergics, opioids, TCAs). Management: urethral catheterisation; trial without catheter (TWOC) after 24–48h Â± alpha-blocker (tamsulosin); transurethral resection of prostate (TURP) if fails TWOC. Haematuria: painless haematuria in an adult is bladder cancer until proven otherwise — 2WW referral. Frank haematuria at any age or microscopic haematuria in age >45: cystoscopy and upper tract imaging (CT urogram).

Breast Conditions and Surgical Investigations

Breast Triple Assessment

Any breast lump or suspicious symptom should undergo triple assessment: (1) Clinical examination (E1–5); (2) Imaging — USS (<35 years, USS preferred; ≥35 years mammogram Â± USS); (3) Histology (core biopsy preferred over FNA for definitive diagnosis). Each component scored 1–5 (1 = normal, 3 = equivocal, 4 = suspicious, 5 = malignant). Discordant triple assessment (e.g. clinical suspicious but biopsy benign) requires repeat biopsy or excision. 2WW referral for breast symptoms (NICE NG12): unexplained breast lump in age ≥30; lump in age ≥50 with skin changes, nipple discharge, nipple retraction; unilateral nipple changes in age ≥50. Breast cancer risk factors: age, family history (BRCA1/2 — lifetime risk up to 85%), early menarche, late menopause, nulliparity, HRT (combined), obesity, alcohol, previous breast radiation.

Mastitis vs breast abscess: mastitis presents in breastfeeding women (day 3–21 postpartum) with tender, red, warm breast Â± flu-like symptoms. Management: continue breastfeeding, analgesia, flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS for 10–14 days. Breast abscess: fluctuant tender lump developing from untreated mastitis. Management: USS-guided aspiration (preferred — avoid incision if possible) or formal drainage; send pus for MC&S; antibiotics. Non-puerperal mastitis (periductal/plasma cell) in non-breastfeeding women: associated with smoking; periareolar; treat with antibiotics Â± surgery (excision of affected ducts).

Surgical Drains, ERAS, and Perioperative Nutrition

Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) protocols reduce length of stay and complications by: preoperative carbohydrate loading (clear carbohydrate drink up to 2h before surgery for elective patients — reduced insulin resistance); multimodal analgesia (avoiding opioids where possible — NSAIDs, paracetamol, regional blocks, local anaesthesia); early oral feeding (within hours of surgery for colorectal, upper GI); early mobilisation; restrictive IV fluid administration; VTE prophylaxis. Surgical drains: closed suction (Jackson-Pratt, Redivac) — remove when <30 mL/day drainage; open drain (corrugated, Penrose) — for infected spaces; chest drain — for pneumothorax/pleural effusion (see respiratory section). Nasogastric (NG) tube placement confirmation: chest X-ray (gold standard) — tip should be below diaphragm, to the left of midline, in stomach. Aspiration of 0.5 mL confirms pH ≤5.5 (do NOT use blue litmus alone). NG tubes are used for decompression (post-op ileus, obstruction) or enteral feeding (nasojejunal preferred for proximal GI surgery).

Fluid Management and Electrolyte Prescribing in Surgical Patients

Fluid prescribing is one of the most commonly tested surgical presentations UKMLA topics and a core foundation doctor skill. The NICE NG29 guideline (2013) provides the framework. Maintenance fluids for a 70 kg adult: approximately 25–30 mL/kg/day water, 1 mmol/kg/day sodium, potassium, and chloride, plus 50–100 g/day glucose to prevent starvation ketosis. This equates to roughly 2–2.5 litres per day of fluid in total. Standard maintenance regimen: 1 litre sodium chloride 0.9% over 8 hours, then 1 litre glucose 5% over 8 hours with 20 mmol KCl, then 1 litre glucose 5% over 8 hours with 20 mmol KCl — reassess regularly and adjust based on fluid balance, renal function, and clinical status.

Resuscitation fluids for hypovolaemia: use crystalloids (sodium chloride 0.9% or Hartmann's/Ringer's lactate) in 250–500 mL boluses and reassess. Hartmann's solution is physiologically closer to plasma and preferred for large-volume resuscitation (less risk of hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis than normal saline). Avoid glucose solutions for resuscitation. Colloids (albumin, gelatin solutions) are not recommended for routine resuscitation. Blood products are required for haemorrhagic shock once crystalloid exceeds 2 litres — activate major haemorrhage protocol and transfuse packed red cells, FFP, and platelets in a 1:1:1 ratio (damage control resuscitation). Signs of fluid overload: pulmonary oedema, rising JVP, peripheral oedema, increasing oxygen requirement — reduce or stop IV fluids, consider furosemide. Signs of dehydration: tachycardia, hypotension, reduced urine output (target above 0.5 mL/kg/hour), raised urea and creatinine, concentrated urine (SG above 1.020).

Electrolyte abnormalities in surgical patients: hypokalaemia (K+ below 3.5 mmol/L) — causes: vomiting, NG losses, diarrhoea, diuretics; replace orally (Sando-K) if mild, or IV at no more than 10 mmol/hour via peripheral line (risk of cardiac arrhythmia at higher rates); monitor ECG. Hyponatraemia (Na+ below 135 mmol/L) — commonest inpatient electrolyte abnormality; causes: SIADH (post-op, pneumonia, drugs), excess hypotonic fluid, hypothyroidism, Addison's; correct slowly (no faster than 8–10 mmol/L in 24 hours) to avoid central pontine myelinolysis. Hypercalcaemia — causes: malignancy (PTHrP, bone metastases), primary hyperparathyroidism; symptoms: bones (pain), stones (renal calculi), groans (abdominal), psychic moans (depression, confusion); management: IV fluids, bisphosphonates (zoledronate), treat underlying cause.

Colorectal Surgery and Enhanced Recovery

Colorectal surgery is a major component of surgical practice relevant to foundation doctors on general surgical attachments. Elective sigmoid colectomy, right hemicolectomy, and anterior resection are performed for colorectal cancer, diverticular disease, and IBD. Stoma types: colostomy (end-stoma using colon — effluent is formed; usually left iliac fossa); ileostomy (effluent is liquid; usually right iliac fossa; higher risk of dehydration and electrolyte imbalance); loop stoma (temporary; two limbs visible); Hartmann's procedure (sigmoid resection with end colostomy, rectal stump oversewn — used in emergency perforation or obstruction). Stoma complications: high output (above 2 litres/day — risk of dehydration; manage with loperamide, codeine, dietary advice), prolapse, retraction, parastomal hernia, skin excoriation. Foundation doctors must be able to identify stoma complications, assess fluid status in high-output stomas, and prescribe appropriate fluid replacement.

Appendicitis management: diagnosis is clinical (Alvarado score: migration of pain to RIF, anorexia, nausea/vomiting, RIF tenderness, rebound tenderness, elevated temperature, leucocytosis, left shift — score 7–10 = high probability). CT abdomen is used when diagnosis uncertain (sensitivity 94%, specificity 95%); USS is preferred in children and pregnant women to avoid radiation. Management: laparoscopic appendicectomy is standard (lower wound infection rate, shorter hospital stay than open). Antibiotics alone for uncomplicated appendicitis are increasingly used (APPAC trial) — acceptable for carefully selected patients who prefer non-surgical management with informed consent. Perforated appendicitis: laparoscopic washout Â± appendicectomy; postoperative antibiotics; risk of appendix mass (treated conservatively with antibiotics, interval appendicectomy at 6–8 weeks if mass resolves).

Trauma, Shock Classification, and ATLS Principles

Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS) provides the framework for managing trauma patients — tested in UKMLA surgical presentations questions. Primary survey follows ABCDE: Airway (with cervical spine control), Breathing, Circulation (with haemorrhage control), Disability (neurological status — GCS, pupils), Exposure (full exposure with environmental control). Resuscitative measures happen simultaneously with assessment. Haemorrhage control: direct pressure, tourniquets for extremity bleeding, pelvic binder for pelvic fractures, REBOA (resuscitative endovascular balloon occlusion of the aorta) or emergency laparotomy for internal haemorrhage. Massive transfusion protocol (MTP): ratio-based resuscitation (1:1:1 packed red cells:FFP:platelets) to prevent dilutional coagulopathy; tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours of injury (CRASH-2 trial).

ClassBlood loss (mL)Blood loss (%)HR (bpm)SBPRRUrine output
Class I<750<15%<100Normal14–20>30 mL/hr
Class II750–150015–30%100–120Normal20–3020–30 mL/hr
Class III1500–200030–40%120–140Decreased30–405–15 mL/hr
Class IV>2000>40%>140Decreased>35Negligible

Secondary survey: head-to-toe examination to identify all injuries after primary survey is complete and patient is stabilised. FAST scan (Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma): bedside USS to detect free fluid (haemoperitoneum, haemopericardium) in four windows (pericardium, right upper quadrant, left upper quadrant, pelvis). Extended FAST (eFAST) adds bilateral chest views to detect pneumothorax and haemothorax. A positive FAST in a haemodynamically unstable patient mandates immediate surgical intervention (laparotomy) without waiting for CT.

Frequently Asked Questions About Surgical Presentations in the UKMLA

What are the 6 Ps of acute limb ischaemia and what is the immediate management?

The 6 Ps are: Pain (severe, sudden onset), Pallor (white/mottled limb), Pulselessness (absent distal pulses), Paraesthesia (tingling — indicates early nerve ischaemia), Paralysis (fixed, weak foot — indicates advanced ischaemia, poor prognosis for limb), and Perishing cold (poikilothermia). Immediate management: call vascular surgery; IV heparin bolus (5000 units) to prevent thrombus propagation; analgesia; DO NOT elevate the limb (worsens perfusion). Imaging: CT angiography or Duplex USS to identify the level of occlusion. Treatment: embolectomy (Fogarty catheter) for embolic occlusion; bypass or thrombolysis for thrombotic occlusion. Paraesthesia and paralysis are signs of impending irreversibility — escalate immediately.

What is the WHO Surgical Safety Checklist and why does it matter for foundation doctors?

The WHO Surgical Safety Checklist is completed in three stages at Sign In, Time Out, and Sign Out in every operating theatre. Foundation doctors are often present in theatre and may be asked to participate in, or lead, part of the checklist. The purpose is to prevent wrong-site surgery, ensure allergy checks, confirm antibiotic prophylaxis, ensure all team members know the procedure, and confirm correct specimen labelling. UKMLA questions may ask which stage a particular check occurs at, or test knowledge of when antibiotic prophylaxis should be given (at induction, before skin incision — confirmed at Time Out). The checklist is a statutory requirement in NHS England under patient safety initiatives.

How do I manage a patient with a postoperative fever on day 5?

Day 5 postoperative fever raises the probability of wound infection (SSI) or DVT/PE — both peak at this time. Systematic approach: take history (wound symptoms, calf pain, breathlessness), examine wound (erythema, swelling, discharge), check calves and respiratory system, review fluid balance and urine output. Investigations: wound swab, FBC (WCC), CRP, blood cultures if systemically unwell, urine dipstick and MC&S, Doppler USS of legs if DVT suspected, D-dimer only if low-moderate clinical probability of PE. If wound infection: open wound, irrigate, send swab, prescribe antibiotics (flucloxacillin for superficial SSI; co-amoxiclav if broader cover needed). If DVT confirmed: therapeutic LMWH or DOAC. Do not diagnose wound infection without examining the wound — this is a common CPSA station pitfall.

What are the signs of bowel obstruction on plain AXR?

Small bowel obstruction: dilated small bowel loops (greater than 3 cm) in the central abdomen; the valvulae conniventes (circular folds, plicae circulares) cross the full width of the bowel lumen — distinguishing feature from colonic haustral folds which are incomplete; absence of gas in the large bowel. Large bowel obstruction: dilated colon (>6 cm, caecum >9 cm — higher risk of perforation) around the periphery of the abdomen; haustral folds are incomplete and do not cross the full width. Sigmoid volvulus: "coffee bean" or "omega loop" sign — massively dilated sigmoid loop in the right upper quadrant. Caecal volvulus: single dilated loop in left upper quadrant. Perforation: free air under the diaphragm on erect CXR (Rigler's sign on AXR — air on both sides of bowel wall).

What is the difference between strangulated, incarcerated, and reducible hernias?

A reducible hernia can be manually pushed back (reduced) into the abdominal cavity. An irreducible (incarcerated) hernia cannot be reduced — the contents are stuck but the blood supply is not yet compromised. A strangulated hernia has a compromised blood supply to the herniated contents — this is a surgical emergency because the bowel can become gangrenous within hours. Features of strangulation: severe localised pain over the hernia, tender irreducible lump, erythema and warmth of overlying skin, systemic signs of sepsis (fever, tachycardia), signs of bowel obstruction. All strangulated hernias require emergency surgical repair. Femoral hernias have the highest risk of strangulation due to the narrow femoral canal — hence all femoral hernias, even when asymptomatic, should be referred for elective repair without delay.

Conclusion

Surgical presentations UKMLA questions reward candidates who can apply acute management protocols under time pressure, know their perioperative drug management rules, interpret postoperative complication timelines using the 5 Ws, and understand hernia types, vascular emergencies, and urological presentations. The triple assessment pathway for breast disease, the WHO checklist, and consent under the Mental Capacity Act are also consistently tested. Link your revision to the UKMLA syllabus and use preparation resources including the UKMLA question banks that feature surgical scenario questions.

post_title: Dermatology in the UKMLA: Rashes, Lesions, and What to Know
post_excerpt: Skin presentations appear across both UKMLA components. Here are the common rashes and lesions tested in the AKT, and the communication skills required for skin-related CPSA stations.
seo_title: Dermatology in the UKMLA: Rashes and Lesions Revision Guide
seo_description: Skin presentations are tested in the UKMLA AKT and CPSA. Our revision guide covers common rashes, skin lesion assessment, melanoma, and the ABCDE mole assessment.
html_content:
Dermatology UKMLA questions test four core competencies: describing skin lesions accurately using precise terminology, recognising common inflammatory dermatoses and their stepped management, identifying skin cancer red flags and applying 2WW referral criteria, and spotting drug reactions and systemic disease skin manifestations. Although candidates rarely spend extended time on dermatology wards, dermatology UKMLA AKT questions appear consistently across systems — rashes occur in infectious disease, rheumatology, oncology, and paediatrics. This guide covers every high-yield dermatology UKMLA topic with glossaries, management tables, and FAQs aligned to NICE and BAD (British Association of Dermatologists) guidelines.

Skin Lesion Vocabulary: Primary and Secondary Lesions

Dermatology UKMLA questions frequently require accurate lesion description before diagnosis. The AKT may use photographs and ask candidates to identify the morphological type. Systematic description covers: type (primary/secondary), size, shape, colour, border, distribution, surface characteristics, and associated features.

Primary Skin Lesions

LesionDefinitionSizeExamples
MaculeFlat, non-palpable colour changeAnyFreckle, cafÃ©-au-lait spot, port wine stain, vitiligo, petechiae (<2 mm), purpura (2–10 mm), ecchymosis (>10 mm)
PapuleElevated, palpable, solid; <5 mm<5 mmMolluscum contagiosum, acne comedone, wart, lichen planus, SCC (early)
PlaqueElevated, flat-topped, palpable; >10 mm>10 mmPsoriasis, eczema (lichenified), mycosis fungoides, BCC (superficial)
NodulePalpable solid lesion deeper than papule; >5 mm>5 mmLipoma, rheumatoid nodule, BCC (nodular), melanoma (advanced), erythema nodosum
VesicleSmall fluid-filled blister; <5 mm<5 mmChickenpox, herpes simplex, herpes zoster, dyshidrotic eczema
BullaLarge fluid-filled blister; >5 mm>5 mmBullous pemphigoid, pemphigus vulgaris, burns, bullous impetigo, epidermolysis bullosa
PustulePus-filled elevated lesionAnyAcne vulgaris, folliculitis, pustular psoriasis, rosacea
Wheal (urtica)Transient, oedematous, elevated; resolves within 24hVariableUrticaria (hives), dermographism, insect bites (early)
TelangiectasiaDilated small superficial blood vesselsAnyRosacea, hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia, systemic sclerosis, liver disease
ComedoPlugged pilosebaceous unit<5 mmOpen (blackhead), closed (whitehead) — acne

Secondary Skin Lesions

LesionDefinitionExamples
ScaleAccumulation of stratum corneum; flakesPsoriasis (silvery), seborrhoeic dermatitis (greasy), pityriasis versicolor
CrustDried exudate (serum, pus, blood)Impetigo (golden-yellow), herpes crusting, eczema (weeping)
ErosionLoss of epidermis only; heals without scarringPemphigus (ruptured bullae), herpes, aphthous ulcers
UlcerLoss of epidermis AND dermis; heals with scarringVenous leg ulcer, arterial ulcer, pressure ulcer, SCC, melanoma (advanced)
LichenificationThickened epidermis with exaggerated skin markings (chronic rubbing/scratching)Chronic eczema, lichen simplex chronicus
ExcoriationLinear marks from scratching through epidermisEczema, scabies, prurigo
AtrophyThinning of epidermis or dermisSteroid-induced atrophy, ageing, lichen sclerosus
Scar/KeloidFibrotic replacement of dermis; keloid extends beyond wound marginsPost-surgical, acne scarring, burn; keloid more common in Afro-Caribbean skin

Dermatology Distribution Patterns

DistributionConditions
Flexural (antecubital fossa, popliteal fossa, neck, groin)Atopic eczema (classic in children); intertrigo; inverse psoriasis; candidiasis
Extensor (elbows, knees, scalp)Psoriasis (classic); dermatitis herpetiformis (extensor surfaces)
Sun-exposed (face, dorsum of hands, forearms, V of neck)SLE (malar/butterfly rash — spares nasolabial folds); polymorphous light eruption; drug photosensitivity; actinic keratosis; rosacea
Dermatomal (unilateral, band-like)Herpes zoster (shingles)
Centripetal (trunk > limbs)Chickenpox; pityriasis rosea; roseola
Palms and solesSecondary syphilis; hand-foot-mouth disease; erythema multiforme; pustular psoriasis; tinea pedis; scabies (burrows); Janeway lesions (infective endocarditis)
Photosensitive (exposed areas) with facial sparingPellagra (niacin deficiency); dermatomyositis (heliotrope rash, Gottron's papules)
Widespread/generalisedDrug reactions (morbilliform rash); viral exanthems; erythroderma; pemphigoid
Acral (hands, feet, ears, nose)Raynaud's; scleroderma; pernio (chilblains); frostbite; Osler's nodes

Eczema: Stepped Management

Atopic eczema (atopic dermatitis) is a chronic inflammatory skin condition characterised by itchy, dry, inflamed skin. It follows a relapsing-remitting course. Associations: asthma, allergic rhinitis, food allergies (filaggrin gene mutations increase susceptibility). NICE (NG190, 2023) recommends a stepped approach based on severity.

StepSeverityTreatment
Step 1Mild (dry skin, infrequent itching, small area)Emollients (apply liberally multiple times daily; leave-on and wash-off); avoid soap; mild potency topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1%) for flares
Step 2Mild-moderate (frequent itching, redness, some sleep disturbance)Moderate potency TCS (clobetasone butyrate 0.05%) for face/flexures; moderately potent or potent TCS for body; topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus) for face/sensitive areas
Step 3Moderate (daily itching, widespread, sleep disturbance)Potent TCS (betamethasone valerate 0.1%) for body; wet wrap therapy; bandaging; sedating antihistamines (chlorphenamine) short-term for sleep; refer to specialist
Step 4Severe (continuous itching, widespread, severe sleep loss)Very potent TCS (clobetasol propionate 0.05%) short-term; dupilumab (IL-4/IL-13 inhibitor — NICE approved for adults and adolescents with moderate-severe uncontrolled AD); tralokinumab; systemic immunosuppressants (ciclosporin, methotrexate, azathioprine)

Topical corticosteroid (TCS) potency ladder: mild (hydrocortisone 1%), moderate (clobetasone butyrate 0.05%), potent (betamethasone valerate 0.1%, mometasone furoate 0.1%), very potent (clobetasol propionate 0.05%). Side effects of TCS: skin atrophy, striae, telangiectasia, purpura, perioral dermatitis, steroid acne. Use lowest effective potency; never use potent/very potent on face; fingertip unit (FTU) guides quantity — 1 FTU = 500 mg = adequate for area of 2 adult palms.

Psoriasis: Types and Management

Psoriasis is a chronic immune-mediated inflammatory skin disease with systemic associations (psoriatic arthritis in ~30%). Chronic plaque psoriasis (psoriasis vulgaris) is the most common type — well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery-white scale on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees), scalp, and lumbosacral area. Nail changes: pitting, onycholysis, oil-drop sign, subungual hyperkeratosis. Auspitz sign: pinpoint bleeding when scale removed. Koebner phenomenon: new psoriasis at sites of skin trauma.

Psoriasis TypeFeaturesFirst-Line Treatment
Chronic plaqueWell-demarcated red plaques with silver scale; extensor, scalp, nailsTopical: potent TCS + vitamin D analogue (calcipotriol) in combination (e.g. Enstilar foam, Dovobet); coal tar; salicylic acid (for scale)
GuttateSmall drop-like papules; follows streptococcal throat infection; trunk; common in children/young adultsTopical TCS; emollients; consider UVB phototherapy; may self-resolve
Pustular (generalised)Sterile pustules on erythrodermic skin; systemic illness; urgent referralUrgent dermatology assessment; biologic therapy (spesolimab)
ErythrodermicGeneralised erythema (>90% BSA); fever; fluid/electrolyte imbalance; hospital admissionDermatology admission; temperature control; emollients; systemic (ciclosporin, biologics)
InverseFlexural areas (axilla, groin, submammary); shiny, well-demarcated, less scaleMild TCS; calcineurin inhibitors; exclude candidal superinfection

Phototherapy: narrowband UVB (NB-UVB) for moderate-severe psoriasis not controlled by topicals. Systemic: methotrexate (monitor FBC, LFTs; folic acid to reduce toxicity; contraindicated in pregnancy); ciclosporin (short-term; monitor BP and renal function); acitretin (retinoid; teratogenic — contraception required for 3 years post-treatment). Biologics: anti-TNF (adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab), anti-IL-17 (secukinumab, ixekizumab), anti-IL-12/23 (ustekinumab), anti-IL-23 (guselkumab, risankizumab) — NICE approved for severe plaque psoriasis not responding to conventional systemic therapy.

Common Infectious Rashes

ConditionOrganismFeaturesTreatment
ImpetigoS. aureus (most common), S. pyogenesGolden-yellow crusted lesions; highly contagious; face and limbs; childrenTopical fusidic acid or mupirocin (mild); oral flucloxacillin (extensive); exclude from school until crusts resolved
ErysipelasS. pyogenes (Group A Strep)Superficial dermal infection; well-demarcated raised red plaque with clear border; face (butterfly distribution) or leg; feverPenicillin V (mild oral); benzylpenicillin IV (severe)
CellulitisS. aureus, S. pyogenesDeep dermis/subcutaneous; poorly defined, spreading erythema; warmth; no raised border (unlike erysipelas); usually unilateral legFlucloxacillin (mild); IV flucloxacillin or benzylpenicillin (severe); no LMWH routinely; bilateral leg redness — consider venous eczema
Ringworm (Tinea corporis)Dermatophytes (Trichophyton, Microsporum)Annular (ring-shaped) scaly plaque with central clearing; pruritic; Tinea capitis (scalp), Tinea pedis (athlete's foot), Tinea unguium (onychomycosis)Topical clotrimazole/terbinafine (2–4 weeks); oral terbinafine (scalp, nail, extensive)
ScabiesSarcoptes scabiei (mite)Intense nocturnal pruritus; burrows (linear tracks) in web spaces, wrists, genitalia; papular rash; household transmissionPermethrin 5% cream (whole body including scalp in elderly/immunosuppressed; wash off after 8–12h; repeat at 7 days); treat all household contacts simultaneously; launder clothing/bedding
Pityriasis versicolorMalassezia furfur (yeast)Hypo- or hyperpigmented macules/patches on trunk; fine scale; more noticeable after sun exposure; not itchyKetoconazole 2% shampoo; selenium sulfide; oral itraconazole for extensive
Herpes zoster (shingles)VZV reactivationPainful dermatomal vesicular rash; prodromal pain before rash; thoracic most common; Ramsay Hunt (ear, VII nerve); post-herpetic neuralgia riskAciclovir 800 mg 5Ã—/day for 7 days (within 72h of rash); analgesia; amitriptyline for PHN; shingles vaccine (Zostavax/Shingrix) for adults ≥70

Systemic Disease Skin Manifestations

Systemic DiseaseSkin ManifestationNotes
Diabetes mellitusNecrobiosis lipoidica, diabetic dermopathy (shin spots), acanthosis nigricans (insulin resistance), Candida infections, poor wound healingAcanthosis nigricans also in obesity, malignancy (stomach cancer — sign of Leser-TrÃ©lat)
SLEButterfly (malar) rash (spares nasolabial folds), photosensitivity, discoid lupus, oral ulcers, livedo reticularis, Raynaud'sAnti-dsDNA, anti-Smith; ANA >1:80
SarcoidosisErythema nodosum (tender nodules on shins), lupus pernio (purple plaques on nose/cheeks), violaceous plaques at scarsLofgren syndrome: EN + bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy + arthritis; good prognosis
IBDErythema nodosum (Crohn's), pyoderma gangrenosum (UC more than Crohn's), aphthous ulcersPyoderma gangrenosum: pathergy (trauma worsens); treat with systemic steroids; biopsy only if diagnostic doubt
Thyroid diseaseHypothyroid: dry skin, coarse hair, myxoedema, puffy face, non-pitting leg oedema. Hyperthyroid: sweaty/warm skin, fine hair loss, Graves' dermopathy (pretibial myxoedema)Pretibial myxoedema is a feature of Graves' disease, not hypothyroidism despite the name
Liver diseaseJaundice, spider naevi (>5 is significant; in SVC territory), palmar erythema, leukonychia, caput medusae, pruritusSpider naevi: blanch with pressure, refill from centre
DermatomyositisHeliotrope rash (periorbital purple erythema), Gottron's papules (knuckles), V-sign (chest), shawl sign, mechanic's handsAssociated with occult malignancy (>40 years — investigate)
Pseudoxanthoma elasticumYellow papules (neck, axillae, groin); "plucked chicken skin"; retinal angioid streaks; cardiovascular complicationsAutosomal recessive; ABCC6 mutation

Drug-Induced Skin Reactions

Reaction TypeFeaturesCommon CulpritsManagement
Morbilliform/exanthematous rashMeasles-like maculopapular rash; trunk; starts days 5–10; pruritic; no mucosal involvementPenicillins (especially ampicillin/amoxicillin + EBV), anticonvulsants, allopurinolWithdraw drug; antihistamines; self-limiting
Urticaria (drug)Wheals, itch; angioedema if severe; may indicate IgE-mediated allergyNSAIDs, aspirin, penicillin, contrast media, opioids (histamine release)Withdraw drug; antihistamines; adrenaline if anaphylaxis
Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS)Target lesions; mucosal involvement (eyes, mouth, genitalia); fever; skin detachment <10% BSA; SCORTEN score for prognosisAllopurinol (most common), carbamazepine, lamotrigine, sulfonamides, NSAIDsImmediate drug withdrawal; ophthalmology; supportive care; burns unit if severe; IVIG considered
Toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN)As SJS but skin detachment >30% BSA; Nikolsky's sign positive (skin peels with lateral pressure); high mortality (20–30%)Same as SJSBurns unit; wound care; IVIG; ciclosporin; fluid balance
Fixed drug eruptionRecurring at same site on re-exposure; round/oval dusky red macule → hyperpigmented; mucosae may be involvedNSAIDs, tetracyclines, sulfonamides, paracetamolIdentify and avoid culprit drug; topical steroids for acute episode
PhotosensitivityExaggerated sunburn reaction on exposed skin; may blisterTetracyclines (especially doxycycline), amiodarone, thiazides, fluoroquinolones, St John's WortSun protection; withdraw drug if possible
Drug-induced lupusArthralgia, rash, positive ANA; anti-histone antibodies; resolves on drug withdrawalHydralazine, procainamide, isoniazid, minocyclineWithdraw drug; self-limiting; steroids if systemic

Skin Cancer: Diagnosis, Referral, and Management

Skin cancer is the most common cancer in the UK. NICE NG12 sets out 2WW referral criteria. The ABCDE criteria for melanoma recognition: Asymmetry, Border (irregular), Colour (two or more colours or uneven), Diameter (>6 mm), Evolution (changing over time). Any suspicion of melanoma warrants a 2WW referral. Dermoscopy by trained clinicians improves diagnostic accuracy.

TypeAppearanceRisk Factors2WW ReferralTreatment
Basal cell carcinoma (BCC)Pearly/waxy nodule with rolled telangiectatic border; central ulceration (rodent ulcer); slow-growing; rarely metastasisesUV exposure, fair skin, immunosuppressionConsider 2WW if appearance suggests BCCSurgical excision (4 mm margins); Mohs micrographic surgery (high-risk sites — face, ears); radiotherapy; topical imiquimod or 5-FU for superficial BCC
Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC)Hyperkeratotic, ulcerated nodule; arises from actinic keratosis; grows faster than BCC; metastasises (5–10%)UV exposure, actinic keratosis, immunosuppression, HPV (anogenital SCC), Marjolin's ulcer (in chronic wounds)2WW if suspected SCC (firm, scaly, ulcerated nodule)Excision (4–6 mm margins); Mohs for high-risk; RT; sentinel node biopsy if high-risk
MelanomaPigmented lesion with ABCDE changes; superficial spreading most common; nodular melanoma grows rapidly and may lack pigmentUV, fair skin/red hair, family history, numerous naevi, CDKN2A mutation, previous melanoma2WW for suspected melanoma (NICE NG12 — any lesion with 2+ ABCDE features or dermoscopy concern)Wide local excision (margins 0.5–2 cm depending on Breslow thickness); sentinel lymph node biopsy if Breslow >1 mm; adjuvant therapy (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, dabrafenib-trametinib for BRAF V600E mutation) for stage III/IV

Melanoma excision margins by Breslow thickness: <1 mm = 1 cm margin; 1–2 mm = 1–2 cm; >2 mm = 2–3 cm. Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB): offered if Breslow thickness >1 mm or 0.8–1 mm with ulceration or mitoses. Actinic keratosis (AK): precursor to SCC; scaly, rough, pink-red patch on sun-exposed skin; treatment: cryotherapy, topical 5-FU (Efudix), imiquimod, diclofenac 3% gel, photodynamic therapy (PDT). Bowen's disease (SCC in situ): well-demarcated red scaly plaque; full-thickness epidermal dysplasia; treat with cryotherapy, PDT, or 5-FU.

Acne, Rosacea, and Isotretinoin

Acne Vulgaris Management

SeverityTypeTreatment
MildComedonal (<20 comedones)Topical retinoid (adapalene) or topical BPO (benzoyl peroxide) alone
Mild-moderatePapulopustular (<20 papules/pustules)Topical BPO + topical antibiotic (clindamycin) — use fixed-combination to reduce resistance; OR topical retinoid + BPO
ModeratePapulopustular (>20 papules) or mild nodularOral tetracycline (doxycycline or lymecycline 12 weeks minimum) + topical retinoid/BPO; OR COCP (Dianette — co-cyprindiol) in females; do not combine oral and topical antibiotics (resistance)
SevereNodular, cystic, scarringOral isotretinoin (refer to dermatology); if isotretinoin declined or contraindicated: high-dose oral antibiotic + topical; intralesional triamcinolone for large cysts

Isotretinoin (Roaccutane): vitamin A derivative; highly effective for severe nodular/cystic acne; teratogenic (REMS/Pregnancy Prevention Programme — two forms of contraception for 1 month before, during, and 1 month after; monthly pregnancy tests). Side effects: dry skin/lips (cheilitis — most common), dry eyes, photosensitivity, raised triglycerides/cholesterol, transient hair thinning, raised LFTs, mood changes (monitor for depression). Monitor FBC, LFTs, fasting lipids before and during treatment. Avoid concurrent tetracyclines (risk of benign intracranial hypertension).

Rosacea vs acne: rosacea is characterised by facial flushing, background erythema, telangiectasia, papules and pustules (no comedones), rhinophyma (thickened sebaceous nose). Triggers: alcohol, spicy food, hot drinks, sun, temperature changes. Management: sun protection, avoid triggers; topical metronidazole or azelaic acid (mild); oral tetracycline for moderate-severe; brimonidine gel for erythema; laser/IPL for telangiectasia.

Childhood Rashes and Urticaria

ConditionOrganism/CauseFeaturesManagement
Chickenpox (varicella)VZV primary infectionItchy vesicular rash, all stages simultaneously (macule→papule→vesicle→crust); centripetal; fever; infectious 2 days before rash until crusted (5–6 days)Symptomatic (calamine, chlorphenamine); aciclovir if immunocompromised or adults; school exclusion until all lesions crusted
Slapped cheek (fifth disease)Parvovirus B19Bright red facial slapped-cheek appearance; lace-like truncal rash; child no longer infectious once rash appears; aplastic crisis risk in sickle cell/immunocompromisedSymptomatic; warn pregnant contacts (hydrops fetalis risk — refer for USS and serology)
Hand, foot, and mouth diseaseCoxsackie A16, Enterovirus 71Vesicles on palms, soles, oral mucosa (oral ulcers); low-grade fever; children; self-limitingSymptomatic; oral hygiene; exclude from nursery if unwell
MeaslesParamyxovirusProdrome (CCCR: cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, rash); Koplik's spots (buccal mucosa); maculopapular rash spreading head to toe; notifiable disease; complications: pneumonia, encephalitis, SSPESupportive; vitamin A (deficient children); prophylaxis: MMR within 72h of exposure; notifiable
Kawasaki diseaseUnknown; immune-mediated vasculitisFever ≥5 days + ≥4 of: conjunctivitis (bilateral, non-purulent), rash (polymorphous), strawberry tongue/lip cracking/erythema, cervical lymphadenopathy, peripheral oedema/erythema + desquamation; risk of coronary artery aneurysmsIVIG (2 g/kg single dose) + high-dose aspirin; ECHO monitoring; aspirin continued 6–8 weeks; seek specialist input
Roseola (exanthem subitum)HHV-6High fever 3–5 days then sudden defervescence; then blanching pink truncal rash; febrile convulsion risk at fever onset; infants/toddlersSymptomatic; self-limiting; reassure parents

Urticaria management: acute urticaria (<6 weeks) — non-sedating antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine) first-line; identify and avoid trigger. Chronic urticaria (>6 weeks) — regular non-sedating antihistamine (up-titrate dose); add H2 antihistamine (cimetidine); omalizumab (anti-IgE, NICE approved for chronic spontaneous urticaria unresponsive to antihistamines); short-course prednisolone for severe flares. Angioedema without urticaria: consider ACE inhibitor-induced (bradykinin-mediated, not antihistamine-responsive — withdraw ACE inhibitor); hereditary angioedema (C1-esterase inhibitor deficiency — treat with C1-INH concentrate, icatibant, tranexamic acid for prophylaxis).

Autoimmune Blistering Disorders and Inflammatory Conditions

Autoimmune blistering disorders are a well-defined area of dermatology UKMLA questions. Bullous pemphigoid (BP): the most common autoimmune blistering disorder; affects elderly patients; IgG antibodies against BP180 and BP230 in the basement membrane zone causing subepidermal blistering. Clinical features: tense, intact bullae on erythematous or urticarial base; pruritic; inner thighs, flexures, abdomen. Nikolsky's sign is negative (unlike pemphigus). Diagnosis: skin biopsy for H&E (subepidermal blister with eosinophil infiltrate) and direct immunofluorescence (linear IgG and C3 at BMZ). Treatment: superpotent topical corticosteroids (clobetasol propionate) for localised disease; oral prednisolone for extensive; doxycycline + nicotinamide (steroid-sparing); rituximab for refractory. Pemphigus vulgaris (PV): IgG antibodies against desmoglein 1 and 3 (keratinocyte cell adhesion molecules) causing intraepidermal blistering. Clinical features: flaccid, easily ruptured bullae; mucosal involvement (mouth ulcers often first); Nikolsky's sign positive; predominantly in middle-aged adults; Jewish and Middle Eastern populations at higher risk. Diagnosis: biopsy shows intraepidermal acantholysis (loss of intercellular adhesion, "tombstone" cells); DIF shows intercellular IgG (chicken-wire pattern). Treatment: oral prednisolone high-dose; immunosuppressants (azathioprine, mycophenolate); rituximab increasingly first-line for moderate-severe PV.

Dermatitis herpetiformis (DH): extremely itchy vesicular rash on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees, buttocks, scalp); associated with coeliac disease (virtually all patients have gluten-sensitive enteropathy); IgA deposits in dermal papillae on DIF (granular pattern, unlike BP which is linear). Treatment: gluten-free diet (resolves DH and coeliac enteropathy over months-years); dapsone for rapid symptom control (check G6PD before starting — haemolytic anaemia risk). Lichen planus: pruritic, violaceous, flat-topped polygonal papules; Wickham's striae (white lacy network on surface); predilection for wrists, ankles, lower back; mucosal involvement (white lacy patches in mouth — buccal mucosa, risk of SCC); nail involvement (pterygium, thinning). Cause: unknown, possibly T-cell mediated; associated with HCV infection; drug-induced LP (gold, antimalarials, thiazides, beta-blockers). Treatment: topical corticosteroids; systemic steroids for widespread; retinoids, tacrolimus for mucosal involvement.

Leg Ulcers: Venous, Arterial, and Neuropathic

Leg ulcers are a common clinical problem encountered by foundation doctors, particularly in elderly patients. Accurate classification guides management, and misclassification (e.g. applying compression to an arterial ulcer) can cause serious harm. Venous leg ulcers: most common type (70%); occur in the gaiter area (medial ankle, above medial malleolus); irregular shape; sloping edges; base may be granulating or sloughy; surrounded by lipodermatosclerosis (indurated, hyperpigmented skin), varicose eczema, haemosiderin deposition. ABPI (ankle-brachial pressure index) measures arterial supply: normal 0.9–1.3; mild arterial disease 0.7–0.9; significant 0.5–0.7; critical ischaemia below 0.5. Management of venous ulcers: compression bandaging (four-layer bandage system or equivalent — do NOT apply if ABPI below 0.8); leg elevation; wound debridement; treat underlying venous insufficiency (duplex USS; varicose vein treatment). Arterial ulcers: punched-out appearance; painful; on pressure points (heels, toes, malleoli); cold, pale, pulseless limb; ABPI below 0.5. Management: vascular surgical referral, angioplasty or bypass, wound care. Neuropathic ulcers: painless ulcers on pressure areas (metatarsal heads, heel) in diabetics or those with peripheral neuropathy; well-defined edges; surrounded by callus; deep, may probe to bone (osteomyelitis risk). Management: offloading (total contact cast, special footwear), glycaemic control, wound care, antibiotics if infected, consider MRI for osteomyelitis.

Pressure ulcers: caused by prolonged pressure on bony prominences impairing microcirculation; at risk patients include elderly, immobile, incontinent, malnourished, unconscious. National Pressure Ulcer Advisory Panel (NPUAP) classification: Grade 1 — non-blanching erythema, intact skin; Grade 2 — partial-thickness skin loss, shallow ulcer; Grade 3 — full-thickness skin loss, subcutaneous visible; Grade 4 — full-thickness tissue loss, bone/tendon/muscle visible. Prevention: pressure-relieving mattresses and cushions, two-hourly repositioning, skin inspection, nutritional support, incontinence management. Treatment: wound debridement, appropriate dressings (moisture-balancing, antimicrobial), negative-pressure wound therapy for complex wounds, surgical debridement and flap repair for severe ulcers. Document and report all new pressure ulcers as a patient safety incident — grade 3 and 4 pressure ulcers acquired in hospital are classified as serious incidents.

Hair and Nail Disorders in the UKMLA

Hair disorders are testable in the dermatology UKMLA AKT, particularly alopecia. Androgenetic alopecia (male-pattern/female-pattern hair loss): most common cause; androgen-dependent miniaturisation of hair follicles; in men — bitemporal recession and vertex thinning (Hamilton-Norwood classification); in women — diffuse crown thinning with frontal hairline preserved (Ludwig classification). Treatment: topical minoxidil (men and women); finasteride 1 mg daily (men only — inhibits 5-alpha reductase; avoid in women of childbearing potential); hair transplant surgery. Alopecia areata: autoimmune, T-cell mediated attack on hair follicles; well-circumscribed patches of non-scarring hair loss; exclamation mark hairs at periphery; associated with thyroid disease and vitiligo. Alopecia totalis (entire scalp) or universalis (entire body) in severe cases. Treatment: potent topical corticosteroids; intralesional triamcinolone (for localised patches); DPCP (contact immunotherapy); baricitinib (JAK inhibitor) is NICE approved for severe alopecia areata. Telogen effluvium: diffuse hair shedding 2–3 months after a trigger (surgery, illness, childbirth, crash dieting, hypothyroidism, iron deficiency); hair count on pull test positive (>6 telogen hairs per pull); self-limiting; treat underlying cause. Tinea capitis: dermatophyte infection of the scalp; children; scaly patches, broken hairs, lymphadenopathy; Wood's lamp fluorescence (Microsporum species); requires systemic antifungal (oral terbinafine or griseofulvin) — topical alone is ineffective for scalp.

Nail disorders: onychomycosis (tinea unguium) — distal/lateral subungual infection most common; dystrophic, thickened, discoloured nails; confirm with nail clippings for MC&S before treating (to avoid unnecessary long courses of systemic antifungals); treatment: oral terbinafine 250 mg daily for 6 weeks (fingernails) or 12 weeks (toenails). Psoriatic nail disease: pitting, onycholysis, oil-drop sign, subungual hyperkeratosis, splinter haemorrhages. Beau's lines: transverse grooves across the nail plate following severe systemic illness (the nail temporarily stops growing). Koilonychia (spoon-shaped nails): iron deficiency anaemia. Leukonychia (white nails): hypoalbuminaemia (liver disease, nephrotic syndrome). Terry's nails (proximal white, distal normal band): hepatic cirrhosis, heart failure. Clubbing: COPD, IPF, cyanotic heart disease, IBD, hepatic cirrhosis, mesothelioma, bronchiectasis — increased nail-fold angle (>180°) and fluctuation of the nail bed.

Frequently Asked Questions About Dermatology in the UKMLA

How do UKMLA AKT questions use dermatology photographs?

The AKT includes single-best-answer questions with clinical images, including dermatology photographs. The approach is the same as in clinical practice: describe the lesion systematically (distribution, morphology, colour, borders, secondary changes) before trying to name the diagnosis. Common AKT dermatology images include psoriasis plaques (silver scale on extensor surfaces), eczema (flexural, excoriated), impetigo (golden crust), shingles (dermatomal vesicles), melanoma (ABCDE changes), and BCC (pearly nodule with rolled edge). Practise describing images aloud during revision — it trains pattern recognition and reinforces terminology.

What are the 2WW referral criteria for skin cancer in NICE NG12?

NICE NG12 criteria for suspected skin cancer 2WW referral: melanoma — any lesion with two or more ABCDE features, a dermoscopically suspicious lesion, or any nodule without obvious benign cause; SCC — firm scaly or ulcerated nodule, especially in sun-damaged or immunosuppressed skin; BCC — pearly nodular lesion with telangiectasia. Urgent non-2WW referral: actinic keratosis (routine unless rapidly changing). Note that 2WW for skin cancer applies when the clinician suspects cancer — not for every pigmented lesion. Dermatoscopy by trained primary care clinicians can reduce unnecessary referrals.

What is Stevens-Johnson Syndrome and how is it different from TEN?

Both SJS and TEN (toxic epidermal necrolysis) are severe cutaneous adverse drug reactions characterised by mucosal involvement, target lesions or maculae, and epidermal detachment. The distinction is the percentage of body surface area affected: SJS involves <10% BSA detachment, SJS/TEN overlap involves 10–30%, and TEN involves >30%. Nikolsky's sign is positive in TEN (lateral pressure causes skin to shear). Both require immediate drug withdrawal and urgent specialist management — ophthalmology is critical (corneal scarring can cause blindness). Mortality of TEN is 20–30%; SCORTEN score is used for prognosis (0–1 = 3.2% mortality; ≥5 = 90%). Commonest culprit drugs: allopurinol, carbamazepine, lamotrigine, sulfonamides, NSAIDs. HLA-B*1502 screening before carbamazepine in East Asian patients reduces risk.

What is the difference between erysipelas and cellulitis?

Both are bacterial skin infections presenting with erythema, warmth, swelling, and pain. The key distinction is depth of infection: erysipelas involves the superficial dermis and lymphatics — the border is sharply demarcated and raised, and it is commonly caused by Streptococcus pyogenes. Cellulitis involves the deep dermis and subcutaneous tissue — the border is poorly defined and not raised. Erysipelas more commonly affects the face (classic butterfly distribution) or lower legs. Both respond to penicillin-based antibiotics, but erysipelas is more reliably treated with penicillin V (streptococcal), while cellulitis covering staphylococcal cover (flucloxacillin) is preferred. Bilateral leg erythema should raise the question of venous eczema/stasis dermatitis rather than cellulitis, which is almost always unilateral.

How do I manage acne in a woman of childbearing potential in the UKMLA?

This is a classic UKMLA question combining dermatology and prescribing safety. In a woman of childbearing potential with moderate acne: avoid isotretinoin unless all REMS conditions are met (two forms of contraception, monthly pregnancy tests, specialist prescribing). Oral tetracyclines (doxycycline, lymecycline) are safe if not pregnant but must be stopped if pregnancy is suspected (tooth discolouration in fetus; use after 12 weeks). COCP with anti-androgenic progestogen (co-cyprindiol/Dianette) is NICE-recommended for women seeking contraception alongside acne treatment, but carries a higher VTE risk than standard COCP and should only be used for acne, not as a first-line contraceptive. Topical options (BPO, adapalene, clindamycin) are generally safe in pregnancy. Azelaic acid is safe in pregnancy. Always check pregnancy status before prescribing systemic acne treatments.

Conclusion

Dermatology UKMLA revision rewards systematic learning of lesion terminology, management ladders, and red flag recognition. Mastering the primary and secondary lesion glossary enables accurate description in both AKT written questions and CPSA clinical skills. The stepped management tables for eczema and psoriasis, the skin cancer referral criteria from NICE NG12, and the drug reaction comparison table are particularly high-yield. Systemic disease skin manifestations — from pyoderma gangrenosum in IBD to heliotrope rash in dermatomyositis — appear regularly across integrated AKT questions. Use the UKMLA syllabus to confirm which skin conditions are listed, and supplement your revision with the preparation resources including photo-based question banks.
