post_title: Disability, Access, and Inclusion in the UKMLA: A Candidate's Guide
post_excerpt: Candidates with disabilities or health conditions have the right to access the UKMLA with appropriate support. Here is a practical guide to what is available and how to apply.
seo_title: Disability and Access in the UKMLA: A Guide to Reasonable Adjustments
seo_description: Candidates with disabilities have the right to reasonable adjustments in the UKMLA. Learn what support is available, how to apply, and the deadlines you must meet.
html_content:
Understanding your rights around UKMLA disability access adjustments is essential whether you are a UK medical student or an international medical graduate preparing for any component of the UK Medical Licensing Assessment. The process is less complicated than many candidates fear, but it requires proactive planning, the right documentation, and an understanding of who to contact and when. This guide covers the legal framework, every type of available adjustment, the evidence required for different conditions, step-by-step application processes for both UK students and IMGs, and what happens if your application is declined.

The Legal Framework for UKMLA Disability Access Adjustments

The foundation for all examination adjustments in the UK is the Equality Act 2010. This legislation defines disability broadly: a physical or mental impairment that has a substantial and long-term adverse effect on normal day-to-day activities. Crucially, this definition encompasses not just visible physical disabilities but also neurodevelopmental conditions (dyslexia, ADHD, autism spectrum conditions), chronic pain, mental health conditions, chronic illness, hearing and visual impairment, and many other health conditions.

What the Equality Act Requires

Under the Equality Act, examining bodies — including the GMC and medical schools — are required to make reasonable adjustments to their services, including examinations, to give disabled people the same access as non-disabled people. Critically, the Act places the duty to make adjustments on the institution, not the individual. You should not have to fight for adjustments you are legally entitled to receive.

The Act does not define what is "reasonable" — this is determined on a case-by-case basis, taking into account the nature and severity of the candidate's difficulty, the cost and practicability of the adjustment, and whether the adjustment would undermine the purpose of the assessment. In practice, adjustments that have negligible cost and are commonly granted in other settings are almost always considered reasonable.

GMC Obligations

The GMC has a legal obligation under the Equality Act to make reasonable adjustments for candidates sitting PLAB 1 (AKT) and PLAB 2 (CPSA). Their published approach emphasises exploring whether an adjustment would remove the barriers created by the candidate's condition, and they assess each application individually based on the evidence provided. The GMC's approach to reasonable adjustments is publicly documented at gmc-uk.org and is regularly updated to reflect current good practice.

The GMC is also bound by the Public Sector Equality Duty, which requires public bodies to actively advance equality — not merely to respond reactively to discrimination claims. This means the GMC should be actively supportive of adjustment applications that are well-evidenced.

Types of Adjustments Available for the UKMLA

The range of adjustments available is wider than many candidates realise. The table below summarises those available for UKMLA components:

Adjustment TypeApplicable ComponentTypical Conditions Supported
Extra time (25%)AKT and CPSADyslexia, ADHD, processing speed differences, autism, chronic pain, long COVID
Extra time (more than 25%)AKT and CPSA (case-by-case)More significant processing impairments; requires specialist evidence
Separate exam roomAKT and CPSAAnxiety disorders, autism (sensory sensitivity), hearing impairment, conditions requiring equipment
Large print question paperAKTVisual impairment
Coloured paper (maize, blue, etc.)AKTDyslexia, visual stress (Meares-Irlen syndrome)
Coloured overlayAKTVisual stress, dyslexia
Screen magnificationAKT (computer-based)Visual impairment
Screen reader / assistive softwareAKTSevere visual impairment, dyslexia
Human readerAKTSevere dyslexia, visual impairment
Scribe (amanuensis)AKT and CPSAPhysical disability affecting writing, severe dyspraxia
Rest breaksAKT and CPSAChronic pain, fatigue conditions, diabetes (glucose monitoring), ADHD, bladder conditions
Seating near toilet facilitiesAKT and CPSAIBD, bladder conditions, pregnancy
Ergonomic seating / furnitureAKT and CPSAChronic pain, physical disability, musculoskeletal conditions
Permission to bring medication or equipmentAKT and CPSADiabetes (insulin, glucose testing), asthma (inhaler), epilepsy (rescue medication)
Female invigilatorAKT and CPSAFaith requirements, trauma history, specific support needs
Alternative exam dateAKT and CPSAAcute illness flare preventing attendance
Exam at regional locationAKT (PLAB 1)Physical disability affecting travel, severe anxiety around travel

How to Request Adjustments: Step-by-Step for UK Medical Students

For students sitting the UKMLA through their medical school (i.e., the medical school-delivered AKT), the adjustment application is typically made through your university's disability services rather than directly through the GMC or exam provider.

Step 1: Register With Your University Disability Service

As early as possible in your medical training — ideally at the start of Year 1 — register with your university's disability or accessibility support service. This is the gateway to all exam adjustments and academic support. Do not wait until finals year.

Step 2: Obtain and Submit Evidence

You will need documentation confirming your condition and its impact on academic performance. The type of evidence required varies by condition (see the evidence table below). Submit this to disability services, who will then confirm your adjustment entitlement.

Step 3: Confirm Adjustments Each Exam Period

Most universities require you to confirm your adjustment requirements each academic year, and to notify disability services before each exam period. Do not assume adjustments automatically transfer from one year to the next without confirmation.

Step 4: Liaise With the Exam Unit

For high-stakes university examinations, your disability services team communicates your adjustments to the exam unit. You may need to sign a consent form allowing this disclosure.

How to Request Adjustments: Step-by-Step for IMG Candidates (GMC Portal)

For IMG candidates sitting PLAB 1 (AKT) or PLAB 2 (CPSA) through the GMC, the adjustment application process is managed directly by the GMC:

Step 1: Identify Your Needs Early

Identify your adjustment needs before booking your exam — ideally three to six months in advance. The GMC recommends applying as early as possible because processing times and evidence gathering can take weeks to months.

Step 2: Contact the GMC Adjustments Team

Email or contact the GMC's customer experience team, specifying that you are applying for reasonable adjustments for PLAB 1 or PLAB 2. The GMC's guidance documents on their website detail the current contact route and form requirements.

Step 3: Submit Supporting Evidence

Provide the evidence specified for your condition type (see table below). The GMC requires an original letter, certificate, or medical report from an appropriate professional confirming the nature of your health needs and the functional impact of your condition in an exam environment. Do not submit a diagnosis letter alone — functional impact on exam performance is essential.

Step 4: GMC Decision

The GMC reviews applications on a case-by-case basis and notifies you of the outcome. If adjustments are approved, they will be applied to your exam sitting. If your application is not approved, you have the right to appeal (see below).

What Evidence Is Needed: By Condition Type

ConditionRequired EvidenceNotes
Dyslexia / SpLDEducational psychologist (EP) report confirming diagnosis; should include standardised assessments and recommended adjustmentsEP reports should ideally be less than 5 years old, though older reports may be accepted if condition is stable
ADHDPsychiatrist or accredited ADHD specialist assessment confirming diagnosis; GP letter alone typically insufficientShould specify functional impact on attention, processing speed, and working memory in timed tasks
Autism Spectrum ConditionDiagnostic assessment report from psychiatrist or clinical psychologistSensory sensitivities and communication style impact should be described if relevant to requested adjustment
Visual ImpairmentOptometrist or ophthalmologist letter specifying visual acuity and recommended adaptationsMay require Low Vision Assessment for more complex adjustments
Hearing ImpairmentAudiologist report with audiogram; ENT or GP letter confirming diagnosisRelevant primarily for CPSA stations with audio components
Physical DisabilityConsultant or GP letter detailing functional limitations and impact on exam performance; occupational therapist assessment if applicableFor mobility-related adjustments, specify exact requirements (e.g., wheelchair access, ergonomic seating)
Chronic PainConsultant or GP letter confirming diagnosis (e.g., fibromyalgia, EDS, CRPS) and impact on sustained concentration and seated positioningRest breaks and ergonomic adjustments most commonly requested
Mental Health ConditionsPsychiatrist or community mental health team letter confirming diagnosis and functional impact; or GP letter if under primary care managementImpact on exam-specific function (e.g., anxiety in timed conditions, concentration) must be explicitly stated
DiabetesGP or consultant letter confirming insulin-treated or CGM-using diabetesAdjustment typically allows glucose monitoring equipment in exam room; rest breaks for glucose management
Chronic Illness (IBD, epilepsy, MS, etc.)Relevant specialist (gastroenterologist, neurologist, etc.) letter confirming diagnosis and functional impactSpecify flare frequency, medication needs, and worst-case scenario impact on exam day

How to Obtain an Educational Psychologist Report

An Educational Psychologist (EP) report is the standard evidence required for dyslexia, specific learning differences (SpLD), and often ADHD. Here is how to access one:

Through Your University

Most UK universities have access to EP assessments through their disability services, either with in-house psychologists or via referral to an approved external EP. Waiting times at universities can be two to six months, particularly at the start of the academic year. Apply early.

Privately

Private EP assessments typically cost £450 to £700. Ensure the assessor is a qualified educational psychologist registered with the Health and Care Professions Council (HCPC). The report must include standardised psychometric assessments (e.g., WAIS-IV, WIAT-III) and must specify recommended examination adjustments. A letter from a tutor saying you find reading difficult is not a substitute.

NHS Access

Direct referral for educational psychology assessment through the NHS is limited for adults. However, if you have an ADHD assessment through NHS psychiatry services, the assessment report will typically be sufficient evidence for ADHD-related adjustments without a separate EP report.

Timeline: How Far in Advance Should You Apply?

The GMC and most medical schools strongly recommend applying for adjustments at least three to six months before your exam date. This timeline accounts for:

  • Time to gather and obtain supporting evidence (EP report: 1 to 3 months; specialist letter: 4 to 12 weeks)
  • Administrative processing by the GMC or university disability service (2 to 6 weeks)
  • Any requests for additional evidence or clarification (adds 2 to 4 weeks)
  • Time to appeal if the initial application is not approved (4 to 8 weeks)

Applying late — for example, within four weeks of the exam — significantly risks adjustments not being in place for your sitting. The GMC and exam providers cannot guarantee adjustment implementation on short notice. If you have a new diagnosis or a deterioration in an existing condition close to your exam date, contact the relevant body immediately and explain the circumstances.

What Happens if Your Adjustment Application Is Rejected?

If the GMC or your medical school's exam unit does not approve your adjustment request, you have the right to challenge this decision. The process typically involves:

Step 1: Request Reasons in Writing

Ask for the specific reasons why your application was not approved. Was the evidence insufficient? Did the institution not consider the adjustment reasonable? Understanding the specific ground for rejection tells you what to address in your appeal.

Step 2: Appeal Internally

The GMC and medical schools have internal appeals processes for adjustment decisions. Submit additional evidence if the original evidence was deemed insufficient. Consider obtaining a supplementary letter from your specialist that specifically addresses the functional impact of your condition in a timed examination setting — this is the most common deficiency in initial applications.

Step 3: Seek External Support

If your internal appeal is unsuccessful, seek advice from the British Medical Association (BMA) student support team, your student union's academic advice service, or a disability rights organisation such as Disability Rights UK. The Equality and Human Rights Commission can advise on whether the institution's decision is compliant with the Equality Act.

In serious cases where the institution's decision appears to constitute disability discrimination, legal advice from a solicitor specialising in education or disability law may be appropriate. However, this step is rarely required as most well-evidenced applications are approved on appeal.

Adjusting Your Revision Strategy for Your Specific Disability

Adjustments in the exam are only part of the picture — your revision approach should also be tailored to how your brain works most effectively.

Dyslexia and Reading Difficulties

Use text-to-speech software (Natural Reader, Speechify, or the built-in accessibility features of iOS/Android) to listen to study materials rather than reading everything visually. Create visual mind maps and diagrams rather than linear notes. For SBA practice, use question banks that allow you to adjust font size and spacing. Colour-code your notes by system or theme to aid retrieval.

ADHD

Break revision into short, focused blocks (25 minutes maximum) with mandatory physical breaks. Use the Pomodoro technique as described in our time management guide. Remove digital distractions during study blocks (website blockers, phone in another room). Use external accountability — study groups, study partner check-ins, or a tutor — to maintain focus and consistency.

Autism Spectrum Conditions

Build a highly structured, predictable revision timetable to reduce cognitive load from scheduling decisions. Practise exams in conditions that match your likely exam environment as closely as possible. If you have been granted a separate exam room, visit the test centre in advance if possible to reduce novelty stress on exam day.

Visual Impairment

Configure your screen for maximum accessibility: high contrast settings, large font size, and screen magnification. If you use a screen reader, practise using it with question bank software specifically, as the accessibility of different platforms varies.

Chronic Pain and Fatigue Conditions

Build rest periods into your revision schedule as a fixed feature, not a luxury. Do not attempt marathon revision sessions — cognitive performance under chronic pain is maximised with shorter, more frequent sessions. Plan your revision schedule around your best-functioning time of day.

Disclosure: What to Tell Your Medical School and the GMC

Disclosure of disability or health conditions is a nuanced area that causes significant anxiety for many medical students and IMGs. The key principles are:

For Examination Adjustments

You must disclose the relevant information to obtain adjustments — there is no other route. However, you control what you disclose and to whom. Your disability services team at university operates under strict confidentiality obligations. Information you share to obtain adjustments is not automatically shared with clinical placement providers or your future employer without your consent.

GMC Health Declarations

The GMC registration process includes a health declaration. You are required to declare conditions that may affect your fitness to practise. This is not the same as the adjustment application process — they are separate. A learning difference (dyslexia, ADHD) does not typically affect fitness to practise and does not need to be declared unless it has caused a patient safety incident or significantly impaired your clinical practice. Mental health conditions should be declared if they are ongoing and potentially relevant to fitness to practise — your occupational health or treating clinician can advise you on this. When in doubt, seek advice from the BMA or a medical defence organisation before declaring anything you are uncertain about.

Resources for Disabled Medical Students and IMGs

Several organisations offer specific support for medical students and doctors with disabilities:

  • BMA (bma.org.uk): The BMA has dedicated guidance on reasonable adjustments throughout medical education and employment. Their "Welcomed and Valued" resource is particularly helpful.
  • Medical Defence Organisations (MDU, MPS): Can advise on health declaration obligations and fitness to practise questions.
  • Disability Rights UK (disabilityrightsuk.org): General disability rights advice including education and examinations.
  • Student Minds: Mental health support specific to students, including medical students.
  • British Dyslexia Association (bdadyslexia.org.uk): Specific guidance on dyslexia in educational settings.
  • ADHD UK: Support and resources for adults with ADHD navigating higher education and professional training.
  • Doctors with Disabilities Initiative: A peer support and advocacy network for doctors with disabilities in the UK.

Returning to Medicine After Illness or Disability

Some candidates sitting the UKMLA are returning to medicine after a period of illness, disability-related absence, or health-related interruption to training. The GMC and medical schools have specific processes for this:

If you have had an interruption to study, your medical school will typically work with you through their Fitness to Study process to agree a supported return plan. If your health condition has caused a fitness to practise concern to be raised, the GMC has a separate health-related fitness to practise process. The BMA strongly advises seeking independent advice from a medical defence organisation before engaging with any fitness to practise process.

Returning doctors often find that their clinical knowledge has drifted during their absence. If you have been away from clinical practice for more than 12 months, consider whether a knowledge refresher or clinical placement before sitting the UKMLA is appropriate. Our guide on UKMLA preparation strategies includes options for candidates returning after a break.

Frequently Asked Questions About UKMLA Disability Access Adjustments

What qualifies as a disability for UKMLA adjustment purposes?

Under the Equality Act 2010, a disability is a physical or mental impairment that has a substantial and long-term (12 months or more) adverse effect on normal day-to-day activities. This is a broad definition and includes dyslexia, ADHD, autism, visual and hearing impairment, chronic pain, mental health conditions, long-term illnesses, and many other conditions. You do not need to consider yourself "disabled" — if your condition meets the legal definition and creates a barrier in exams, you are entitled to adjustments.

Does requesting adjustments mean I am admitting I am not capable of being a doctor?

No. Reasonable adjustments are specifically designed to allow disabled candidates to demonstrate their knowledge and clinical ability on a level playing field with non-disabled candidates. They do not lower the pass standard — they remove barriers to accessing the assessment. Many highly effective doctors have adjustments throughout their training and careers.

How long does an extra time application take to process?

GMC processing times vary. Allow a minimum of six to eight weeks for a straightforward application with complete evidence. More complex cases or applications requiring additional evidence may take longer. Apply at least three to six months before your exam date to allow sufficient buffer.

Can I apply for adjustments if I was only recently diagnosed?

Yes. A recent diagnosis does not invalidate an application. The Equality Act protects you from the point of diagnosis. If your condition meets the definition of disability and you have appropriate supporting evidence from a qualified professional, you are entitled to request adjustments regardless of when you were diagnosed.

Are adjustments available for both the AKT and CPSA?

Yes. Reasonable adjustments are available for both the AKT (PLAB 1) and CPSA (PLAB 2) components of the UKMLA. Some adjustments are specific to one component — for example, screen reader access is relevant to the computer-based AKT, while adjustments for the CPSA may involve modified station formats or communication accommodations. Apply separately for each component if needed.

What if I develop a new health condition between passing the AKT and sitting the CPSA?

Apply for adjustments for the CPSA as soon as possible after diagnosis. Explain the timing in your application and include evidence from your treating clinician. The GMC will consider your application based on current need, not on whether you had adjustments for a previous component.

Is my adjustment information kept confidential?

Yes. Information submitted in support of a reasonable adjustment application is handled in accordance with data protection law (UK GDPR) and is used only for the purpose of processing your adjustment request. It is not shared with employers, clinical placement providers, or other institutions without your explicit consent. University disability services also operate under strict confidentiality obligations.

Can I get adjustments if I do not have a formal diagnosis but suspect I have a learning difference?

A formal diagnosis is typically required for adjustment applications. However, if you are in the process of being assessed (e.g., awaiting an ADHD assessment), contact your disability services team or the GMC to explain the situation. In some cases, interim adjustments can be made pending a formal diagnosis, particularly where there is a documented history of difficulty and supporting professional correspondence.

What should I do if my adjustment is not in place on exam day?

Contact the exam invigilator or test centre manager immediately and explain that adjustments should be in place for your sitting. Carry a copy of your adjustment confirmation letter from the GMC or your university. If the adjustment cannot be implemented on the day, request written confirmation of the failure and contact the GMC or your medical school immediately after the exam to report what occurred. Document everything. You may be entitled to a resit under appropriate conditions or to have the circumstances taken into account in the assessment of your performance.

Conclusion

Every candidate who qualifies for UKMLA disability access adjustments under the Equality Act 2010 is legally entitled to receive them, and both the GMC and medical schools are obligated to provide reasonable adjustments that remove barriers to accessing the assessment. The process requires proactive planning, appropriate documentation, and early application — but it is not adversarial. Most well-evidenced applications are approved. Use the evidence table and step-by-step guides in this article to prepare your application, apply at least three to six months before your exam date, and seek support from the BMA, your university disability service, or a medical defence organisation if you encounter difficulties. For further information on the UKMLA process, see our guides on eligibility requirements, the AKT exam pattern, the CPSA exam pattern, and appeals and resits.

post_title: How UK Medical Schools Implement the UKMLA: An Overview
post_excerpt: Different UK medical schools implement the UKMLA in different ways. Here is what candidates should expect from the medical-school-delivered route and how to navigate variation.
seo_title: How UK Medical Schools Implement the UKMLA
seo_description: UK medical schools implement the UKMLA within GMC-approved frameworks but with variation in timing and format. Learn what to expect from your medical school's AKT and CPSA.
html_content:
Introduction: Medical Schools as the Foundation of UKMLA Success

The United Kingdom Medical Licensing Assessment represents the single national standard that every doctor must meet before they can practise medicine in the United Kingdom. Whether you are a home student at a Russell Group university, an international medical graduate sitting PLAB, or a graduate-entry student completing an accelerated programme, the same Content Map governs what you are expected to know and demonstrate. Medical schools are therefore not merely the institutions where you learn medicine — they are the primary vehicles through which UKMLA preparation happens, whether explicitly or implicitly.

Understanding how UK medical schools are structured, how they integrate UKMLA assessment into their programmes, and what distinguishes one school from another in terms of UKMLA preparation support is essential knowledge for any prospective or current medical student. This guide explores the full landscape: programme types, curriculum philosophy, the role of the Medical Schools Council, and practical strategies for making the most of each year of medical school as a form of structured UKMLA preparation.

If you are new to the UKMLA, start with our overview at /what-is-ukmla and review the full syllabus before reading this guide.

The Structure of UK Medical Education

Five-Year Traditional Programmes

The five-year undergraduate medical degree remains the most common route to qualification in the United Kingdom. Students typically enter directly from secondary school at age 17 or 18, having achieved high A-level grades in sciences. The structure usually divides into a pre-clinical phase covering foundational sciences in years one and two, and a clinical phase covering applied medicine and surgery in years three through five. The final year is increasingly dedicated to preparation for Foundation Year 1, meaning that UKMLA preparation is concentrated in years four and five at most schools.

Notable examples of traditional five-year programmes include Oxford, Cambridge, King's College London, and Newcastle. These schools vary considerably in how they balance lecture-based teaching, small-group learning, and early clinical contact, all of which affect how well-prepared graduates feel for both components of the UKMLA.

Four-Year Graduate-Entry Programmes

Graduate-entry medicine programmes compress the standard curriculum into four years, accepting students who already hold a degree in any subject, though science graduates are particularly competitive. The accelerated pace means these students need to manage high content volume across a shorter timeline. Many graduate-entry students report that their prior academic skills — particularly critical appraisal and independent study — give them an advantage in the AKT, where evidence-based medicine reasoning features prominently.

Schools offering graduate-entry programmes include St George's, Warwick, Swansea, Nottingham, and Leicester. These programmes tend to be heavily integrated from day one, with basic science delivered in clinical context, which aligns well with the MLA Content Map's emphasis on applied clinical knowledge.

Six-Year Programmes with Intercalated Degrees

A number of students undertake an intercalated degree — an additional year of study at degree level, usually a Bachelor of Science or Bachelor of Medical Sciences — embedded within their medical programme, producing a six-year course. Some schools such as Oxford and Cambridge integrate an intercalated year by default, while others make it optional.

The intercalated year typically focuses on a specialist area: pharmacology, neuroscience, global health, medical education, or research methods are common choices. For UKMLA preparation, the most valuable intercalated subjects are those that develop critical appraisal skills, statistical literacy, and evidence-based medicine reasoning — all of which directly support performance in the AKT's data interpretation questions.

Programme TypeDurationEntry RouteUKMLA Implications
Traditional Undergraduate5 yearsPost-A level (age 17-18)Gradual exposure; AKT/CPSA focused in final year
Graduate Entry4 yearsPost-degree (any subject)Accelerated pace; prior academic skills advantageous
With Intercalated Degree6 yearsPost-A level or post-degreeResearch skills benefit AKT EBM questions
Extended Foundation Year6 yearsWidening Participation routesFoundation year builds science base before Year 1

How Medical Schools Integrate the UKMLA

AKT Delivery and Internal Mock Examinations

The Applied Knowledge Test is delivered nationally by the Medical Schools Council on behalf of all UK medical schools. Every student sits the same AKT, and the standard is set nationally using the Angoff method, ensuring consistency across institutions. However, the way medical schools prepare students for the AKT varies enormously.

Some schools — particularly those with dedicated assessment units — run internal mock AKT examinations closely modelled on the national format: 200 single best answer questions across two papers, sat under timed conditions, with question distributions matching the MLA Content Map. These mocks give students experience of the format, identify knowledge gaps while there is still time to address them, and allow the school to identify students who may need additional support.

Other schools rely on students to access the MSC's own practice examination, which provides genuine sample questions written to the same standard as the live AKT. This practice exam is invaluable regardless of what your school provides internally, and every student should complete it under timed conditions at least once before sitting the real thing. Details of the AKT format are covered at /exam-pattern/akt.

CPSA Delivery Under National Specification

The Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment is administered by each individual medical school, but under a national specification set by the MSC and overseen by the GMC. While specific stations and scenarios differ between schools, the competency domains assessed — history taking, clinical examination, procedural skills, and professional capabilities — are standardised, and all schools must demonstrate that their CPSA meets the national standard.

Examiner training is critical to CPSA quality. All examiners at all schools must be trained to assess to the same standard, and inter-rater reliability is monitored. Standard-setting for the CPSA uses modified borderline regression or similar psychometric methods to establish the pass mark, reviewed nationally to ensure equivalence across schools. Students sit their CPSA at their own school, assessed by their own institution's examiners, but the standard they are held to is identical. For more on CPSA structure, visit /exam-pattern/cpsa.

The MLA Content Map as Universal Curriculum

The MLA Content Map is the GMC's specification of what all UK medical graduates must know and be able to do. It is structured around presentations, conditions, and professional capabilities. Every medical school must align its curriculum to the Content Map, ensuring graduates have been taught across all domains.

In practice, a respiratory teaching block must cover the presentations and conditions listed in the Content Map for respiratory — breathlessness, haemoptysis, wheeze, pleuritic pain — and the associated conditions such as asthma, COPD, pneumonia, pulmonary embolism, and lung cancer. The Content Map does not prescribe how schools teach these topics, only that they are addressed. This gives schools pedagogical freedom while ensuring national consistency of graduate outcomes.

Comparing Medical School Teaching Approaches

Problem-Based Learning

Problem-based learning is a pedagogical approach in which small groups of students work through clinical cases to drive their own learning, guided by a facilitator. Strong PBL traditions are found at Manchester, Leeds, and Barts and The London. PBL produces graduates with strong clinical reasoning skills because knowledge is encoded in clinical context from the beginning. For UKMLA preparation, the clinical reasoning habits formed in PBL curricula are valuable for higher-order AKT questions requiring synthesis and application rather than recall. The main risk is uneven content coverage if learning objectives from cases are not systematically mapped to the Content Map — students should supplement self-directed learning with periodic Content Map audits.

Case-Based Learning

Case-based learning uses pre-prepared clinical cases to structure teaching with more facilitator guidance than pure PBL. Edinburgh Medical School is known for its CBL curriculum, which blends case-based small-group work with lecture series and clinical placements. CBL tends to produce more consistent content coverage than pure PBL while retaining the clinical context that improves retention and application. Students at CBL schools often feel well-prepared for the applied, vignette-based format of the AKT because they have spent their medical school years thinking in patient-centred terms.

Integrated Systems-Based Curricula

The majority of modern UK medical schools use a systems-based integrated curriculum, in which all teaching — basic science, clinical science, and professional skills — is organised around organ systems. A cardiovascular block covers cardiac anatomy, physiology, pharmacology, pathology, clinical presentations, examination skills, and relevant ethical issues simultaneously. This mirrors the structure of the MLA Content Map and produces graduates who think in integrated clinical terms from early in their training.

Schools using systems-based integration include Imperial, University College London, Bristol, Hull York, Brighton and Sussex, and Peninsula. The integrated approach means UKMLA preparation is effectively embedded throughout the curriculum, rather than requiring a separate revision phase to translate disciplinary knowledge into clinical application.

The Medical Schools Council: Coordinating the UKMLA

The Medical Schools Council is the representative body for UK medical schools and plays a central coordinating role in the UKMLA. Key MSC functions for students include: coordination of AKT delivery, managing the national process with assessment partners to ensure question quality and psychometric rigour; publication of the practice AKT examination and sample questions with explanations, freely available to all students; and publication of guidance documents on CPSA delivery, examiner training standards, Content Map alignment, and equality requirements.

Students preparing for the CPSA should read the publicly available CPSA specification documents, which describe exactly what is being assessed in each domain and what good performance looks like. The MSC also advises medical schools on Content Map interpretation and supports curriculum review as the Content Map is periodically updated by the GMC.

UK Medical School Rankings and What They Actually Mean

National Student Survey Scores

The National Student Survey is an annual survey of final-year students assessing teaching quality, assessment and feedback, academic support, organisation and management, learning resources, and student voice. High NSS scores in assessment and feedback may indicate better UKMLA preparation support, but NSS scores reflect student perception rather than objective outcomes, and students at demanding schools may rate their experience lower even if their preparation is objectively superior.

GMC Survey Outcomes

The GMC conducts annual surveys of UK medical students and Foundation Year doctors, asking about preparedness for practice, training quality, and supervision. GMC survey results published by medical school are arguably a more meaningful indicator of UKMLA-relevant preparation quality than general university rankings, because they specifically assess clinical training and readiness for Foundation Year — the exact context in which UKMLA competencies must be demonstrated.

Research Output and Clinical Placement Quality

Students at research-intensive schools benefit from exposure to research methodology, critical appraisal, and evidence-based practice — skills that contribute to AKT performance in epidemiology and statistics questions. Clinical placement quality is perhaps the most relevant ranking dimension for UKMLA preparation. Schools with strong NHS trust partnerships, diverse patient populations, and well-structured placement programmes give students more clinical exposure, translating to better performance in both AKT and CPSA. Ask at open days how many clinical placement hours are guaranteed, how placements are distributed across specialties, and what supervision arrangements exist.

Choosing Where to Study with UKMLA Success in Mind

Prospective students sometimes ask whether certain medical schools produce better UKMLA pass rates. The GMC publishes pass rate data by school, and most schools achieve high pass rates. More meaningful are the structural factors that support preparation: curriculum structure alignment with the Content Map, quality and breadth of CPSA preparation support, institutional access to question banks, and pastoral and academic support systems.

Schools using systems-based integrated curricula are inherently aligned with the UKMLA's presentation-based structure, making revision feel like revisiting familiar terrain. Ask at open days how the curriculum maps to the Content Map and whether there is an explicit Content Map guide provided to students. For CPSA preparation, ask about standardised patient practice, structured feedback from trained examiners, and peer OSCE teaching programmes. Some schools provide institutional access to question banks such as Passmedicine or BMJ OnExamination — a significant benefit that reduces financial barriers affecting some students disproportionately.

IMGs and the PLAB Route: The Same Standard, Different Path

International medical graduates who qualified outside the UK do not sit the UKMLA — they sit PLAB. PLAB 1 is a 180-question single best answer examination broadly comparable to the AKT in content coverage, and PLAB 2 is an OSCE comparable in design to the CPSA. The critical point is that the same MLA Content Map standard applies to both routes. Revision resources designed for UKMLA — question banks, Content Map revision guides, OSCE preparation resources — are also relevant for IMGs preparing for PLAB, and vice versa. The medical knowledge and clinical skills required are identical; only the administrative pathway differs.

What Makes Some Students Better Prepared

Structured learning from early years is fundamental. Students who engage systematically with the curriculum from year one, building coherent knowledge frameworks rather than relying on last-minute revision, perform better in the AKT. The breadth of the Content Map means consistent study over years yields better coverage than intense short-term revision concentrated in final weeks.

Early single best answer question practice is the preparation strategy most strongly supported by evidence in medical assessment research. Students who begin SBA practice from the first year of clinical training develop the question interpretation skills the AKT requires — identifying what the question is really asking, eliminating plausible distractors, and selecting the best answer when two or three options seem reasonable.

Clinical placement engagement separates consistently high performers from average performers more reliably than any revision resource. Students who see the most patients, take the most histories, perform the most examinations, and reflect through supervision develop clinical pattern recognition that the AKT directly tests. Peer teaching is the final high-leverage strategy — teaching peers consolidates your own learning more effectively than passive revision and identifies gaps in your understanding more reliably than re-reading notes.

Making the Most of Each Year of Medical School

First Year: Foundations and Content Map Orientation

In the first year, download the MLA Content Map from the GMC website and familiarise yourself with its structure. You do not need to learn everything in year one — understanding the architecture of the Content Map helps you file new knowledge in the right conceptual places as you acquire it. Use spaced-repetition software such as Anki to begin building flashcard decks for key facts as you encounter them in lectures and tutorials, rather than creating revision materials from scratch in the final months before the AKT. Small consistent daily practice with spaced repetition, begun in year one, accumulates into an enormous advantage by final year. Build effective study habits — retrieval practice, spaced repetition, interleaving different topics, and elaborative interrogation — into your routine from the start.

Second and Third Year: Clinical Placement Engagement

The transition to clinical placements is the most significant phase for UKMLA preparation. Every patient encounter is a UKMLA learning opportunity: every history you take maps to a Content Map presentation; every examination finding maps to a condition; every management discussion maps to a clinical decision. Keep a reflective log of learning points from clinical encounters and check them against the Content Map to ensure you are covering required presentations across each specialty block.

Focus on developing the consultation skills the CPSA tests: structured history taking including the patient's ideas, concerns, and expectations; systematic examination with appropriate technique; clear explanation and information-giving; shared decision-making; and professional behaviour in challenging situations. Every consultation with a real patient is practice for a CPSA station. Seek feedback from supervisors after consultations and treat clinical placements as deliberate practice rather than passive observation.

Final Year: AKT and CPSA Focused Preparation

Final year is where structured UKMLA preparation becomes explicit. Most students increase their SBA practice volume significantly, completing thousands of practice questions across the full Content Map. The goal is not to learn new material for the first time — if you have engaged well in previous years, you should be consolidating and refining knowledge. Complete at least one full mock AKT under timed conditions before the real examination, and treat mock feedback as a gap-analysis tool, directing final revision to the weakest domains.

For CPSA preparation in final year, increase the frequency and formality of practice OSCE stations. Work through all CPSA competency domains with a structured checklist. Practice with peers giving structured feedback, not just friendly encouragement. The most valuable CPSA practice exposes and addresses weaknesses rather than confirming existing strengths.

Intercalated Degrees and the UKMLA

The intercalated year is sometimes seen as a distraction from clinical training. In practice, students who choose research-focused intercalated subjects and engage genuinely with the research process develop strong critical appraisal skills that directly benefit AKT performance. Questions on study design, statistical interpretation, evidence quality, and clinical guideline application are consistently among the more challenging AKT questions for students without explicit research methods training. Students who have conducted their own research project — formulating a question, designing a study, analysing data, and interpreting results — bring statistical intuition to these questions that purely clinically-trained students often lack.

Students intercalating in clinical sciences — pharmacology, pathology, neuroscience — benefit from deeper mechanistic understanding supporting clinical reasoning in AKT pharmacology and pathophysiology questions. The most important thing is genuine interest — engagement and depth of learning in a genuinely interesting subject always produces better educational outcomes than superficial coverage of a subject chosen for strategic reasons alone.

Widening Participation and Diversity in Medical Schools

UK medical schools have made significant efforts to widen participation in medicine — attracting applicants from state school backgrounds, lower-income families, underrepresented ethnic groups, and areas with historically low rates of medical school entry. Widening participation initiatives include extended foundation programmes, contextual admissions, access to medicine programmes, and financial support through bursaries and grants.

From a UKMLA perspective, widening participation students are fully capable of achieving high UKMLA pass rates. However, some may have had less access to private tutoring, question bank subscriptions, and other paid preparation resources, creating accumulated disadvantage. Medical schools with strong institutional support — including free question bank access, dedicated academic support, peer-tutoring programmes, and early identification of students who are struggling — help level this playing field and ensure that the UKMLA standard is accessible to all qualified graduates regardless of background.

Frequently Asked Questions

Does my choice of medical school affect my UKMLA pass rate?

The GMC publishes pass rates by medical school and most schools achieve consistently high rates. Your individual engagement with the curriculum, clinical placements, and preparation resources matters more than school-level aggregate performance. However, schools with strong integrated curricula, excellent clinical placement quality, and robust CPSA preparation support create structural advantages that benefit all their students.

Should I choose a PBL school or a traditional lecture-based school for UKMLA preparation?

There is no strong evidence that one pedagogical approach produces systematically better UKMLA outcomes. Most modern UK medical schools use hybrid approaches. Focus on finding a curriculum that suits your learning style and a school that provides strong pastoral and academic support alongside quality clinical placements.

Is the UKMLA the same as finals at my medical school?

No. The UKMLA is a national assessment required for GMC registration. Your medical school may run additional assessments for degree classification purposes, but these are separate from the UKMLA licensing assessment. Some schools align their internal finals closely with the UKMLA format; others maintain separate examinations for degree purposes alongside the national UKMLA.

Do graduate-entry students have an advantage in the AKT?

Graduate-entry students often report stronger exam technique and time management from their prior degree. However, the AKT tests clinical knowledge acquired through medical training — prior degree subject does not provide direct clinical knowledge advantage. The accelerated pace of graduate-entry programmes means less time to consolidate clinical learning, which can be a disadvantage in clinical vignette questions requiring experiential pattern recognition.

What resources does the Medical Schools Council provide for students?

The MSC provides a practice AKT examination with sample questions, published guidance documents on the CPSA and Content Map, and coordinates national AKT delivery. The MSC practice exam is among the highest-quality free preparation resources available and should be completed under timed conditions well before the examination date.

How should I use clinical placements to prepare for the UKMLA?

Treat every patient encounter as a Content Map learning opportunity. After taking a history, identify which Content Map presentation it corresponds to and review associated conditions and management pathways. After each placement block, audit the Content Map sections relevant to that specialty to identify gaps. Discuss clinical cases with supervisors in terms of differential diagnosis and evidence-based management.

Conclusion

UK medical schools are diverse in structure, pedagogy, and culture, but share a common commitment to preparing graduates for national UKMLA standards defined by the GMC's MLA Content Map. Whether you study at a traditional research-intensive university or a modern integrated medical school, whether you follow a five-year undergraduate or four-year graduate-entry route, the UKMLA measures the same standard. Your task is to engage deeply with your curriculum from day one, take full advantage of clinical placements, practise SBA questions consistently from early in your clinical years, and approach CPSA preparation with the same systematic rigour you bring to written examinations.

For comprehensive guidance on preparing for both components of the UKMLA, visit our preparation hub at /preparation, and explore the full syllabus breakdown at /syllabus. For information on scoring and results, visit /results-and-scoring.
